framework🗣 Mark Cubanconviction: highNBAPeacockESPNstreamingfranchise valuations
So what: Reframes sports-franchise value as a bet on streaming subscriber retention; churn data, not gate/ratings, is the variable that moves valuations.
“it's driven by subscriptions to streaming services... what really matters is the number of subscribers Peacock gets, ESPN gets... If there's churn, who knows what happens with valuations” ▶ 40:32
Cuban argues the NBA's second-apron rules force teams to break up rosters because they cannot carry three max players, creating more parity and making back-to-back titles far harder.
framework🗣 Mark Cubanconviction: highNBAsecond apronsalary cap
So what: The CBA structurally caps dynasty-building, increasing competitive parity, which affects team-building strategy and league-wide competitive economics.
“the second apron is just a complete game changer. And you have to be so much better at putting together a team than you were before.” ▶ 38:48

51 Insights 1 episode(s) · 40 insights

The $88 Trillion Tokenization Shift Has Started with DTCC & BCG59 min40 insights2026-07-21
▶ Watch on YouTube
In a 51 Insights webinar, DTCC's Nadine Shakar and BCG's Christian Schmidt and Roy Shukri detail DTCC's move from tokenization pilots to live production, framing tokenization as a multi-decade infrastructure transition (not an innovation theme) that BCG projects could tokenize ~$88T (16% of real-world assets) by ~2035 and put ~35% of bank profits under scrutiny, with collateral/repo as the first-scaling use case and DTCC positioning as a neutral multi-chain 'structural orchestrator.'
DTCC moves from pilots to live production · 5Infrastructure transition, not innovation theme · 3Market sizing and the $88T projection · 5Digital asset taxonomy · 3Where value unlocks first: collateral, asset management, trading · 6Multi-chain interoperability and structural orchestration · 4Risk model becomes code · 3Adoption barriers and bank strategy · 7Stablecoins, ALM/AML and settlement responsibility · 2Legacy coexistence and netting · 2
DTCC moves from pilots to live production 5
DTCC, acting on an SEC no-action letter granted in December 2025, is starting to tokenize assets it holds in custody with live real trades beginning in July 2025 and a broader launch in October, explicitly not another pilot.
data_point🗣 Nadine Shakar, DTCC global head of tokenization/digital assetsconviction: highDTCCSEC
So what: The largest US securities depository going live with real tokenized settlement is the concrete catalyst that turns tokenization from thesis to tradable infrastructure; watch the October launch as the real inflection.
“So we are done with PC's, we're done with experiments. This is about real world assets moving on real rails to make a real difference” ▶ 7:20
DTCC's named list of 50 firms (including BlackRock, JP Morgan, Goldman, State Street) is not exclusive; because DTCC is owned and governed by its participants, tokenization access is open to all participants and a wide vendor ecosystem.
framework🗣 Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: highDTCCBlackRockJP MorganGoldman SachsState Street
So what: Firms not on the headline list of 50 are not locked out; the participant-owned utility model means broad access, so the competitive edge is readiness rather than being named.
DTCC settles roughly $10 trillion a day, custodies over $100 trillion (cited as a 114 trillion depository), and the US market alone holds $150 trillion in assets and settles four quadrillion dollars of securities a year.
data_point🗣 host / Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: highDTCC
So what: The scale of legacy volumes explains why new tokenized rails must supplement rather than replace existing plumbing and why full migration is a multi-decade process.
“there's $150 trillion in assets and we settle four quadrillion dollars worth of securities a year” ▶ 30:37
DTCC keeps digital shares and traditional shares under one CUSIP by design so holdings stay reconciled (a thousand shares stays a thousand shares) and liquidity is not fragmented across tokenized and traditional forms.
framework🗣 Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: highDTCC
So what: The single-CUSIP design is the anti-fragmentation control that lets institutions adopt tokenization without splitting liquidity pools, a key structural difference from crypto-native issuance.
“the digital shares and the traditional shares share one CQIP and that was done on purpose by design” ▶ 45:37
Shakar frames the 'holy trinity' now converging for tokenization as institutional infrastructure (DTCC, NASDAQ, NYSE), digital cash (stablecoins/tokenized deposits), and regulatory clarity, with the late-2024 regulatory unlock and the 2025 DTCC no-action letter as the 'watershed moment.'
framework🗣 Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: highDTCCNASDAQNYSESEC
So what: Regulatory clarity was the missing leg after a decade of stalled experiments; its arrival is what distinguishes this cycle and unlocks institutional capital sitting on the sidelines.
“the 25 no action letter for DTCC was honestly a watershed moment” ▶ 15:32
Infrastructure transition, not innovation theme 3
BCG argues tokenization is an 'infrastructure transition' analogous to telecom's shift from circuit-switched to packet-switched networks or 2G-to-5G, transitions that in telecom took 20-plus years, implying a multi-year, possibly multi-decade migration.
framework🗣 Christian Schmidt, BCG global banking/CIB leadconviction: highBCG
So what: Framing it as infrastructure (not a fad) means the strategic question for banks is timing and who pays, not whether it happens; positions for a slow-then-fast adoption curve.
“there are some analogies to other industries like the telecommunications industry when it's you know switched from circuit switch to packet switch” ▶ 8:11
Schmidt says the pattern for such transitions is that markets overestimate impact in the short term and underestimate it in the long term, so there is no reason for banks to panic now but strong reason to set strategy on where to invest.
framework🗣 Christian Schmidt, BCGconviction: mediumBCG
So what: Sets expectations against near-term hype disappointment while flagging long-term underappreciation, a cue to build optionality rather than chase immediate returns.
“we uh overestimate them in the short term but we underestimate them in the long term” ▶ 9:50
BCG models that up to ~30-35% of bank profits are under scrutiny/at stake from tokenization out to 2033-2035, presented as scenario modeling rather than a firm forecast.
data_point🗣 Christian Schmidt, BCG / hostconviction: mediumBCG
So what: A third of bank profit pools being contestable is the number that forces boards to treat this as strategic; use it to gauge which business lines (trading, transaction banking, NIM) are most exposed.
“almost 35% of profit is under scrutiny until 2035” ▶ 10:26
Market sizing and the $88T projection 5
BCG's headline $88 trillion tokenized real-world-asset figure rests on the assumption that 16% of all real-world assets are tokenized within ~10 years via exponential growth, differentiated by asset class (low for native equities, higher for commodities and bonds).
data_point🗣 Christian Schmidt, BCGconviction: mediumBCG
So what: The bull case hinges on a single 16% penetration assumption; investors should stress-test it (BCG itself flags 8% as a plausible alternative) rather than treat $88T as certain.
“the the key assumption was like it's 16% of all the real world assets are tokenized by in 10 years from now” ▶ 38:06
Schmidt himself hedges the $88T number, saying to 'take it with a grain of salt,' noting the 16% could be 8%, and that its real value is as a scenario base for bespoke company-level strategy rather than an absolute truth.
contrarian🗣 Christian Schmidt, BCGconviction: lowBCG
So what: The report's own author downplaying its headline number is a signal to discount consultancy TAM figures and focus on directional momentum instead of the projection.
“So I would take it with a grain of salt. It's not the absolute truth but it's simply you know 16% it's not unthinkable.” ▶ 39:20
Current market sizes cited: cryptocurrency roughly $3 trillion (down from end of last year), tokenized money about $300 billion (roughly the stablecoin market cap), and tokenized real-world assets only about $330 billion against a ~$300 trillion pool of real-world assets.
data_point🗣 Christian Schmidt, BCGconviction: highBCG
So what: Tokenized RWAs at ~$330B are a rounding error versus the $300T addressable pool, quantifying how early the transition is and the runway if penetration rises.
“Tokenized money is like 300 billion. That's more or less the stable coin market cap. And then tokenized real world assets is at 30 only 330 billion” ▶ 19:55
Shakar contrasts BCG's ~$80-88T projection with Citi's roughly $7 trillion by 2030 estimate but says the exact number doesn't matter; she'd be happy with $1 trillion in the next couple of years because momentum, not the figure, drives adoption.
thesis🗣 Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: mediumDTCCBCGCiti
So what: The operator closest to the plumbing anchors near-term expectations far below the $88T headline, a reality check on timing for anyone trading the theme.
“Listen, I' I'd be happy with a trillion in the next couple of years.” ▶ 39:38
Shukri argues concentrated markets like US Treasury repo (about $1 trillion-plus) and daily derivative margin could transition very quickly because 15-20 counterparties drive huge volume, so a few players agreeing can flip a market.
prediction🗣 Roy Shukri, BCG capital markets lead North Americaconviction: mediumBCGUS Treasury repo market
So what: Concentrated wholesale markets are the fast-migration candidates; watch repo and derivative margin for near-term tokenization tipping points rather than fragmented retail assets.
“many of them are pretty concentrated markets where 15 to 20 counterparties drive a huge amount of volume” ▶ 41:16
Digital asset taxonomy 3
BCG segments the ecosystem into three buckets: cryptocurrencies, tokenized money (tokenized deposits, stablecoins, CBDCs, synthetic CBDCs, tokenized money market funds), and tokenized real-world assets (equities, fixed income, commodities, real estate, private capital), with securities financing/repo and derivatives margin as cross-asset 'transversal' plumbing.
framework🗣 Roy Shukri, BCGconviction: highBCGFinality
So what: This taxonomy separates crypto from institutional tokenization and identifies where value case and adoption momentum differ, useful for mapping exposure by sub-asset class.
“crypto tokenized money tokenized real world asset and then there are subasset classes within each of them” ▶ 19:23
Shukri names tokenized money market funds as an interesting instrument that straddles between real-world assets and tokenized money, and cites Fnality's work as an example of a synthetic CBDC.
data_point🗣 Roy Shukri, BCGconviction: mediumBCGFinality
So what: Tokenized MMFs are a dual-use instrument (yield-bearing collateral and settlement money), a category to watch as a bridge product between the security and cash legs.
Board-level conversations over the past 18-24 months shifted from conflating crypto/DLT/tokenization to treating tokenization as a distinct institutional-grade technology, which Shukri says removed a major barrier to momentum.
causal🗣 Roy Shukri, BCGconviction: highBCG
So what: The de-conflation of crypto from tokenization is what made boards willing to fund it; a sentiment shift that underpins the current institutional wave.
“many of the board members and even senior management would conflate crypto and distributed ledger technology and tokenization” ▶ 11:34
Where value unlocks first: collateral, asset management, trading 6
Shakar calls collateral the biggest 'super app' for tokenization because moving money at network speed enables real-time mark-to-market, reducing capital, lowering cost of capital and making financing more affordable.
thesis🗣 Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: highDTCC
So what: Collateral mobility is the clearest near-term ROI use case; intraday liquidity and freed-up capital are the tangible benefits to watch as DTCC scales it first.
“the biggest super app right now uh for uh for tokenization is around collateral” ▶ 23:54
In the lightning round, Shukri bets collateral and repo scale before fund distribution, citing Shakar's existing work, high value to unlock, and the small number of participants in that ecosystem.
prediction🗣 Roy Shukri, BCGconviction: highBCGDTCC
So what: Direct sequencing call: position for collateral/repo tokenization ahead of tokenized fund distribution when timing the theme.
Schmidt argues asset management benefits most because tokenization expands the universe of manageable assets, bringing previously unmanaged holdings and hard-to-access classes (tokenized real estate, commodities, private credit) into managed, more-easily-distributed wrappers.
thesis🗣 Christian Schmidt, BCGconviction: highBCG
So what: Asset managers gain AUM expansion and new distribution channels; a structural tailwind for the sector even as fee margins stay competitive.
“the asset universe that can be managed it's going to increase” ▶ 21:50
For trading, Schmidt's hypothesis is that trading frequency rises while margins come under pressure (roughly offsetting), but return on equity could jump sharply because far less equity and liquidity are needed to run the business.
thesis🗣 Christian Schmidt, BCGconviction: mediumBCG
So what: The trading upside is capital efficiency (higher ROE via lower equity/liquidity needs), not revenue growth, reframing how to value tokenization's impact on trading desks.
“there might be a big jump in return on equity in trading because simply you need less equity and less liquidity” ▶ 22:14
Shukri says tokenization can create liquidity and better price discovery in illiquid pockets of fixed income and private capital, and improve fund-management transparency via clear traceability and lineage from fund to underlying asset.
thesis🗣 Roy Shukri, BCGconviction: mediumBCG
So what: Private capital and illiquid fixed income are where tokenization's liquidity premium is largest; a value driver for investors seeking access and price discovery in those classes.
Shakar cautions the cost-benefit will be slower to materialize because it requires a full retooling of front, middle and back office, but firms that rethink their distribution and operating model first will see an uptick much faster.
causal🗣 Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: mediumDTCC
So what: First-mover advantage accrues to firms that redesign distribution/operating models early; back-office cost savings lag, so watch commercial-model reinvention as the leading indicator.
Multi-chain interoperability and structural orchestration 4
DTCC is pursuing a multi-chain strategy (Canton and Stellar among the first, plus its own EVM-based chain) rather than picking a single winning chain, building a harmonization layer on top to move assets across chains and reconcile how each chain treats data.
framework🗣 Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: highDTCCCantonStellarEthereumEVM
So what: No single chain wins; the value accrues to the neutral interoperability/data-harmonization layer, so bet on standards-setters rather than any one blockchain token.
“I'm less concerned about how many chains we're operating on as long as we've got good interoperability standards, good data standards” ▶ 43:09
Both Shakar and Shukri assert the future is a multi-chain ecosystem, not one chain that controls all assets, though with some industry consolidation and strong shared standards for data movement replacing today's reconciliation needs.
prediction🗣 Roy Shukri, BCG / Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: highDTCC
So what: Rejects the winner-take-all chain thesis; the differentiator versus today's infrastructure is standardized interoperability that reduces reconciliation, favoring orchestration plays.
“the future is not one chain that controls all of the assets, but it's really a multi-chain state of the world” ▶ 47:44
DTCC positions itself as a neutral 'structural orchestrator' and record-keeper that does not trade or service client accounts and refuses to pick chain winners or losers, extending its record-keeper role into the digital age.
thesis🗣 Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: highDTCC
So what: DTCC's neutrality (no competition with participants) is its moat as the orchestration layer; the 'structural orchestrator' role is where durable infrastructure value concentrates.
“I love that word structural orchestrator and that is that is what we're striving to be like digital financial market infrastructure over time.” ▶ 47:16
Schmidt argues clients don't care whether settlement uses a stablecoin, tokenized deposit or CBDC, so the institutions that can shield this technical complexity from clients will win.
thesis🗣 Christian Schmidt, BCGconviction: mediumBCG
So what: Competitive advantage shifts to complexity-abstraction and orchestration, not the underlying instrument choice, a lens for picking winners among intermediaries.
“those institution that can shield all this complexity from clients will actually win” ▶ 46:21
Risk model becomes code 3
BCG and DTCC (via DTCClear) built a risk taxonomy identifying novel risks from tokenization (cyber, quantum) and argue programmability lets risk/compliance checks like AML whitelisting/blacklisting be embedded directly in smart contract code rather than run as manual post-transaction processes.
framework🗣 Roy Shukri, BCGconviction: highBCGDTCC
So what: 'Risk by design' embedded in code is a structural efficiency gain but introduces cyber/quantum/smart-contract exposures; a new risk surface for banks and their auditors to price.
“a lot of the risk processes that are done offline today can now be integrated within code” ▶ 49:32
Schmidt flags an unresolved problem that encoding risk management removes the discretion humans historically applied, especially in crises where judgment is needed, and says how to handle that is not yet solved.
contrarian🗣 Christian Schmidt, BCGconviction: mediumBCG
So what: Hard-coded risk controls could behave badly in tail events where discretion matters; an open governance risk investors should track as tokenized markets scale.
“especially in in situations of crisis right where you need to have discretion... it's put in code and I think that's something which is not completely solved yet” ▶ 50:58
Shakar says DTCC tokens are 'compliance aware tokens' into which firms can embed their own risk management and smart contracts, and that 24/7 tradability lets institutions cover exposure and move positions when a big event hits over a weekend.
framework🗣 Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: highDTCC
So what: 24/7 tokenized venues plus programmable compliance can reduce weekend/gap risk that today's closed markets impose, a genuine risk-management benefit for holders.
“the DTCC tokens are what we call compliance aware tokens” ▶ 51:32
Adoption barriers and bank strategy 7
Shakar says the binding constraint is no longer infrastructure, regulation or digital cash but firms' state of readiness, noting a decade of 'innovation by press release' left many now stepping back to ask whether they have proper wallet management, trading and risk-management infrastructure.
causal🗣 Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: highDTCC
So what: Institutional readiness, not technology or rules, is the current bottleneck; firms with real wallet/risk infrastructure will separate from those that only issued announcements.
“a lot of a lot of innovation has been done via press release over these years” ▶ 26:36
Shakar warns the 'close follower' posture many cautious institutions adopt is risky because the space is moving fast enough that the gap between early movers and followers could become tough to catch up.
prediction🗣 Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: mediumDTCC
So what: Fast-follower strategy may fail here; the widening early-mover gap argues for at least no-regret positioning now rather than waiting.
“there's going to be a gap between the people that start um in that close follower that could be could be a bit tough to to to catch up” ▶ 28:18
Shukri recommends every major financial institution treat tokenization as a top-10 board priority by defining a 5-10 year north star, making 'no regret' investments/partnerships today, deciding build-vs-partner for core tech stack, and systematically monitoring market tipping points to accelerate when adoption shifts.
framework🗣 Roy Shukri, BCGconviction: highBCG
So what: A concrete strategic playbook: the actionable question is not 'whether' but 'which no-regret moves and which tipping points to monitor,' useful for evaluating which banks are executing.
“The question they're trying to figure out is what are the no regret moves, investments, partnership use cases that I should focus on.” ▶ 35:41
Shukri says a ~$50 billion US regional bank should be a follower not a leader and rightly keeps tokenization off its top-five list, while it is now a top-10 priority for every major global systemically important bank, a shift he says did not exist two years ago.
framework🗣 Roy Shukri, BCGconviction: highBCG
So what: Adoption strategy should be sized to a bank's systemic role; edge lies with large GSIBs, and regional banks are rational adopters-not-builders, informing which names lead.
“if you are a $50 billion regional small bank in the US, is this topic on your top five list of the management team? Probably not” ▶ 33:30
Shukri advises small regional banks not to issue their own stablecoin but to join industry consortiums to mutualize adoption cost, and to gate tokenized-deposit investment on whether their client base actually moves money cross-border.
framework🗣 Roy Shukri, BCGconviction: highBCG
So what: Clear build-vs-join guidance: consortium participation over proprietary stablecoins for small banks, with cross-border client flow as the trigger for tokenized-deposit spend.
“if you're a very small regional bank, it in our mind, it doesn't make any sense to issue your own stable coin” ▶ 52:57
Shakar cites live evidence the rails are being built: a coalition of banks launching a tokenized deposit platform (reported that morning in the Wall Street Journal) and reported moves by Visa, Mastercard and Stripe on stablecoins.
data_point🗣 Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: highDTCCVisaMastercardStripeWall Street Journal
So what: Payment giants and bank coalitions building stablecoin/tokenized-deposit ramps signals the cash-leg infrastructure maturing in parallel, a prerequisite for RWA settlement to scale.
“the likes of Visa, Mastercard, and Stripe are doing the same on stable coins” ▶ 54:29
Shakar warns small banks are chasing tokenization out of FOMO ('JP Morgan is doing that or BlackRock is doing this') without a real business case, and stresses global businesses should lean to stablecoins while local businesses lean to tokenized deposits.
framework🗣 Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: mediumDTCCJP MorganBlackRock
So what: FOMO-driven adoption without a business case is a red flag; the stablecoin-vs-tokenized-deposit choice should map to whether a bank's business is global or local.
≈ “not just FOMO” paraphrase ▶ 54:41
Stablecoins, ALM/AML and settlement responsibility 2
On who owns the ALM problem if stablecoins become the settlement layer, Shukri says the stablecoin issuer owns interest-rate-risk management on the reserves, while AML obligations are shared across issuers and the banks/distribution partners providing wallet access, per the GENIUS and CLARITY acts.
framework🗣 Roy Shukri, BCGconviction: highBCGGENIUS ActCLARITY Act
So what: Clarifies liability allocation in stablecoin settlement: reserve interest-rate risk sits with issuers, AML/KYC is a shared duty, relevant for assessing stablecoin issuer and distributor risk.
“the stable coin issuer will own the interest rate risk management on the reserves” ▶ 56:09
Shukri notes a bank's stablecoin engagement may start as a defensive move but its perspective could shift rapidly if it sees significant stablecoin adoption in Asian commerce or by a large e-commerce player, a key tipping point to monitor.
prediction🗣 Roy Shukri, BCGconviction: mediumBCG
So what: Watch Asian commerce and large e-commerce stablecoin adoption as the trigger that flips banks from defensive to offensive stablecoin strategies.
“if they see a significant adoption in Asian commerce or with one of the large uh e-commerce players then their perspective could change quite rapidly” ▶ 36:01
Legacy coexistence and netting 2
Shakar rejects atomic (real-time gross) settlement demands, noting DTCC nets 98% of trades and there is not enough money on the planet to fund gross settlement of all flows, so tokenization must preserve netting, clearing and existing efficiencies.
contrarian🗣 Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: highDTCC
So what: A pointed pushback on the crypto ideal of atomic settlement; netting stays essential, meaning tokenized market structure will look more hybrid than crypto maximalists expect.
“we are so efficient that we net 98% of our trades. So there's not enough money on the planet that would allow us to take all that money in settlement and realm gross uh settlement” ▶ 31:27
Shakar stresses the new tokenized infrastructure supplements and enhances rather than replaces existing plumbing, likening the transition to 'driving on the autobahn and changing the wheels at the same time' after 55 years building the current system.
framework🗣 Nadine Shakar, DTCCconviction: highDTCC
So what: Sets realistic expectations that legacy and tokenized rails coexist for years; a caution against pricing in wholesale replacement of incumbents.
“It's really like driving on the autobon and changing the wheels at the same time.” ▶ 32:11

Invest Like the Best · Patrick O'Shaughnessy 1 episode(s) · 39 insights

The 2028 Natural Gas Crisis No One Sees Coming60 min39 insights2026-07-21
▶ Watch on YouTube
An energy investor named Matt argues that after 16-18 months modeling every US gas well, pipeline and data-center project, the combination of committed LNG exports (rising from ~15 to 35 BCF/day) and AI compute demand will drive US working gas storage below all historical lows starting mid-2028, making natural gas price risk 'unbounded and convex' and forcing electricity prices sharply higher into 2028-2030. He is bullish gas producers (Expand Energy, Range) and solar assets, bullish large-scale AP-1000 nuclear as the only long-term fix, and bearish gas-turbine/fuel-cell makers, hyperscalers and the US consumer.
The 2028 Gas Storage Deficit Thesis · 4Demand: LNG Exports and AI Compute · 6Supply Ceiling: Resource, Processing, Gathering, Pipelines · 5Market Complacency and Mispricing · 4Winners: Gas Producers and Solar · 4Nuclear as the Only Long-Term Fix · 5Losers: Consumers, Turbine and Fuel-Cell Makers, Hyperscalers · 6Policy Fixes and Global Implications · 5
The 2028 Gas Storage Deficit Thesis 4
US working gas storage will break materially below all historical lows starting mid-2028 and drop below all known historical storage evidence by 2029, driven by committed LNG exports plus AI compute demand outrunning producible supply.
prediction🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highnatural gasUS working gas storageLNG exportsAI compute
So what: A structural (not weather-driven) storage draw would break the flat forward curve; positioning long 2028-2030 gas exposure ahead of consensus is the trade.
“When you get to the middle of 2028, we start to break very materially below in a historical way where gas available in storage has ever been before. And by 2029, we drop below all known historical storage evidence.” ▶ 0:02
Natural gas now supplies over 40% of US power generation and has overtaken petroleum as the most important fuel in the country, so gas scarcity transmits directly into electricity prices.
data_point🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highnatural gasUS power generationpetroleumelectricity prices
So what: Because gas sets the marginal power price, a gas squeeze is really an electricity-price shock that hits every energy-input business.
“Natural gas which is over 40% of US power generation has become or is imminently going to become the most important fuel in the country.” ▶ 1:40
The upside price risk in natural gas is 'unbounded and convex' as the deficit builds into 2028-2030, and the pain is felt most acutely in electricity prices.
thesis🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highnatural gaselectricity prices
So what: Convexity favors owning gas/electricity upside via cheap options or under-priced producer equity rather than linear exposure.
“the upside risk price of natural gas is both you know unbounded and convex where you will feel it the most acutely will be electricity prices in 28 29 2030” ▶ 2:24
Historical gas spikes ($8-10/MCF during Russia-Ukraine and $6-10 during polar vortex events) were transitory, whereas this deficit is driven by structural demand against a known production ceiling, so a price move need not mean-revert.
causal🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highnatural gasRussia-Ukrainepolar vortexEurope
So what: Distinguishing structural from transitory means the trade is a durable re-rating, not a spike to fade.
“what we're talking about are structural drivers of demand against a known possible production of gas and they don't match up” ▶ 23:55
Demand: LNG Exports and AI Compute 6
US LNG export nameplate capacity has grown from near zero to ~15 BCF/day and is contractually scheduled to reach up to 35 BCF/day by end of 2030, on a US production base of ~110-112 BCF/day, making exports 12-15% of daily supply with the incremental demand 'die' already largely cast.
data_point🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highLNG exportsChenierenatural gas production
So what: ~20 BCF/day of new demand is locked in via project-financed LNG contracts before AI is even added, so the demand side is not speculative.
“as of today we're scheduled to export uh up to 35 BCF a day by the end of 2030 and in that case the die has been mostly cast” ▶ 3:48
The primary US gas demand driver over the past decade has been LNG exports, not AI; the AI compute pull is only a recent incremental addition on top of an already-committed export ramp.
contrarian🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highLNG exportsAI computeshale
So what: Investors fixated on data centers are underweighting the LNG base load that makes the system fragile before AI is counted.
“the the dye was cast long before AI compute came to the scene” ▶ 2:46
In the base case, credible AI-compute gas demand (P50 assets with approvals, PPAs and interconnection) is ~5 BCF/day, but a P30/P0 unmitigated scenario more than doubles that to 12-15 BCF/day by the early 2030s.
data_point🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: mediumAI computedata centersnatural gas
So what: The gap between base and extreme AI demand (5 vs 12-15 BCF/day) is the swing factor determining how severe the squeeze becomes.
“If you start to move it down and say P30 or P 0, that number, you know, more can more than double and be, you know, 12 to 15 BCF a day by the early 2030s if if unmititigated.” ▶ 7:17
Every current power solution for AI (from GE Vernova combined-cycle down to fuel cells and Caterpillar/Solar turbines) consumes more natural gas, and 'bring your own generation' (BYOG) mandates on hyperscalers structurally increase gas demand, not reduce it.
framework🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highGE VernovaBloom EnergyCaterpillarfuel cellshyperscalersBYOG
So what: Treat every new power-gen announcement (Bloom, GE Vernova) as a bullish gas-demand signal: 'think more gas.'
≈ “every time you read a press release from Bloom or from Genev, think more gas.” paraphrase
A Bloom Energy 6-series fuel cell consumes ~150 million cubic feet of gas per day per gigawatt, and the market prices Bloom at ~2 GW/year of capacity ramping toward 5 GW, for which the gas does not exist unless taken from another use.
data_point🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: mediumBloom Energyfuel cellsnatural gas
So what: Bloom's implied gas draw is a concrete way to size incremental demand and question whether its capacity ramp is physically feasible.
“6 series bloom energy you know kind of latest gen fuel cell will take 150 million cubic feet a day of gas per gawatt” ▶ 6:50
Simply shutting off LNG exports to fix the domestic shortage is impractical because of contract law, tens of billions in project financing, and US exports becoming ~one-third of global gas supply that FTA and non-FTA allies depend on.
causal🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highLNG exportsFTA countriesglobal gas supply
So what: The obvious 'just keep it home' relief valve is largely blocked, meaning the deficit is more binding than a naive analysis assumes.
“It's more complicated than that contract law.” ▶ 7:51
Supply Ceiling: Resource, Processing, Gathering, Pipelines 5
Using digitized acreage polygons and well-level data, maximum US gas deliverability is capped at roughly 128-132 BCF/day even assuming every company optimally drills its remaining captured inventory, and that highest 132 estimate is the number required just to satisfy committed LNG exports.
data_point🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highnatural gas productionAppalachiaHaynesvillePermian
So what: A hard ~132 BCF/day flow ceiling against rising committed demand is the crux of the deficit; producers with the best remaining rock are the scarce asset.
“you get to this 1328 130 132 BCF a day of of maximum deliverability” ▶ 14:28
The problem is not resource in the ground but deliverability within the time frame: the US gets through most existing captured well inventory in the next four or five years, and incremental supply is throttled by processing, gathering and pipeline constraints, not geology.
framework🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highnatural gasprocessinggatheringinterstate pipelines
So what: Because the bottleneck is midstream and time, high prices alone can't quickly fix supply, extending the squeeze's duration.
“we get through most of the existing captured inventory of companies in the next four or five years” ▶ 13:46
Processing capacity (which takes 2-3 years to build) is insufficient to reach the assumed 20 BCF/day production growth, so processing must be built imminently or the supply target is unreachable.
data_point🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highgas processingPermianAppalachia
So what: Midstream processing build-out lead times are a real-time indicator of whether the deficit can be avoided; watch announced projects.
“You really would need to in the near term even to get to our 20 BCF of incremental gas production we're willing to estimate you need to have more processing built” ▶ 18:40
Only one major interstate gas pipeline (Mountain Valley Pipeline, Appalachia to Mid-Atlantic) has been built in the last 10-12 years due to environmental permitting and regulatory hurdles, making pipeline connectivity a severe rate-limiter on moving gas to AI load.
data_point🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highMountain Valley Pipelineinterstate pipelinesAppalachia
So what: Pipeline scarcity locks gas away from demand centers; permitting reform progress (or lack of it) is a key catalyst to monitor.
“in the last 10 or 12 years we've really built one interstate a gas pipeline that was Mountain Valley pipeline connecting Appalachia to Mid-Atlantic” ▶ 19:44
The Permian 'plenty of gas' rebuttal is already in the base case (7+ BCF/day of pipelines coming 2026-2030), and producing materially more Permian associated gas would require sustained much-higher oil prices, which would only worsen the consumer crisis.
causal🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highPermianassociated gasoil prices
So what: The bull-case 'Permian saves us' argument fails unless oil stays elevated, so cheap-oil scenarios do not relieve the gas squeeze.
“In order to produce a lot more perian gas than even these seven plus BCF pipelines being built that were already modeling, you'd need much much higher oil for longer” ▶ 41:47
Market Complacency and Mispricing 4
The natural gas forward curve is flat at ~$3.50-3.60 out to 2030 because 15 years of abundance has bred complacency, and no real-time indicator (rig count, EQT shutting in gas) shows the market is onto the coming 2028-2030 tightness.
contrarian🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highnatural gasforward curveEQTrig count
So what: A flat curve mispricing a structural deficit is the core opportunity; the trade is on before rig counts or hedging reveal the tightness.
“gas has lulled everybody to sleep” ▶ 22:24
The 2028 gas market resembles the DRAM/memory shortage: capacity under-investment bites 'slowly at first and then all at once,' and the squeeze is visible six-plus months out, not two years out.
framework🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highnatural gasDRAMmemory shortage
So what: The memory-shortage analog implies an abrupt repricing; front-running utility hedging is time-sensitive.
“imagine being short memory a year ago or or 18 months ago and finding out all of a sudden you're short memory. That is what this natural gas market looks like to us, not two years out, but six plus months out.” ▶ 55:26
The 2028 gas market is currently illiquid, which is why the forward curve hasn't moved; once utilities and gas generators begin hedging and securing physical supply for 2028, a 'knife fight' for physical gas will erupt and forward curves could move up materially within six months.
prediction🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highnatural gasutilitiesforward curve
So what: The catalyst is utilities rolling forward to hedge 2028; that repricing window is the entry timing.
“you're going to start to see a knife fight to secure natural gas physical in 28 like we really haven't seen before” ▶ 0:19
Investors refuse to price near-term gas above appropriately-supplied levels yet will pay for untested 2035 technologies, an inconsistency the speaker finds surprising across the sectors he follows.
contrarian🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: mediumnatural gasgas producers
So what: This valuation asymmetry favors owning cheap, near-term cash-generating gas assets over speculative long-dated tech.
“investors are not willing to look past near-term appropriate appropriately supplied gas market, but they're willing to pay for something in 2035 that is totally untested or unproven” ▶ 54:20
Winners: Gas Producers and Solar 4
Expand Energy is the biggest winner, controlling ~70% of remaining core Haynesville wells of known high-productivity rock, yet trades at ~4x forward EBITDA with a low-to-mid teens free cash flow yield after its stock plummeted amid a leadership vacuum and CEO search.
thesis🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highExpand EnergyHaynesville
So what: A dominant Haynesville producer priced on a complacent flat curve offers highly asymmetric upside if the gas thesis plays out.
“expand we think is far and away the biggest winner uniquely expand is CEO list right now” ▶ 25:36
Range Resources is the highest-quality upstream company in Appalachia with significant room to grow production and materially grow returns to investors.
thesis🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highRange ResourcesAppalachia
So what: Range is a second high-quality producer levered to the gas re-rating with production growth optionality.
Utility-scale solar assets get a windfall from rising electricity prices at zero incremental capital cost because gas sets the marginal power price while sunlight stays free; XPLR (ticker XIFR, formerly NextEra Yield Co) benefits by marking PPAs to market at much higher values in the early 2030s.
thesis🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highXPLRXIFRNextEra Yield CosolarPPAs
So what: Solar yieldcos are a non-obvious long: margin expansion with no capex as gas-set power prices rise; watch PPA repricing schedules.
“Solar assets stand to benefit from a windfall where electricity prices are going up because gas is setting the margin of plant taking fuel where the price is increasing is rising while sun costs the same.” ▶ 26:58
Residential solar plus batteries will grow exponentially and become economic even without tax incentives for the first time, as it is the main way households can protect against very high 10am-6pm peak power prices once gas tightens.
prediction🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highresidential solarbatterieselectricity prices
So what: A depressed residential-solar industry could inflect upward on economics alone; contrarian long into a sector left for dead after incentive removal.
“We think residential solar grows exponentially from here.” ▶ 28:20
Nuclear as the Only Long-Term Fix 5
Large-scale nuclear, specifically Westinghouse AP-1000 units, is the only viable long-term solution and must come online by 2033-2034 (the soonest possible), because the gas deficit only worsens in 2031-2032 and beyond and there is no bridge fuel other than solar and wind.
thesis🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highWestinghouseAP-1000nuclearnatural gas
So what: Nuclear build timelines mean commitments must happen now; AP-1000 supply chain names are a multi-year structural long.
“the only viable solution is to build large-scale nuclear um as fast as possible, which would mean it needs to come on in 2033 or 2034” ▶ 30:48
Westinghouse is a deeply undervalued asset held inside Cameco (49%) and Brookfield (51%) and will surface value when Westinghouse comes public; the US government appears to be lining up early procurement to derisk the nuclear supply chain.
thesis🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: mediumWestinghouseCamecoBrookfieldUS government
So what: Cameco offers indirect, underpriced exposure to a Westinghouse re-rating and AP-1000 order cycle.
“when the Westinghouse comes public and it's deeply undervalued within Camo today” ▶ 33:16
BWXT, the primary supplier of nuclear components for the US Navy, significantly benefits from the coming nuclear cycle with large dollar content in the AP-1000.
thesis🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: mediumBWXTUS NavyAP-1000nuclear
So what: BWXT is a supply-chain long leveraged to both Navy nuclear and new large-scale reactor builds.
SMRs are largely unviable as the near-term fix because many are still science experiments and are not set up to manufacture and scale to the tens of gigawatts needed by the 2030s, so large-scale reactors win over small.
contrarian🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highSMRsnuclearAP-1000
So what: Fade the SMR hype for scale solutions; capital should favor proven large-scale AP-1000 economics over unscaled SMR bets.
“many of these SMR companies are not set up to manufacture and truly scale uh for the solution that's needed” ▶ 31:57
China is currently building ~39 nuclear reactors, 34 of which are 1 GW-plus and about a third modeled after the AP-1000, evidence that large-scale nuclear is buildable on a known timeline when a country commits.
data_point🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highChinanuclear reactorsAP-1000
So what: China's build proves the AP-1000 template scales, supporting the thesis that US large-scale nuclear is a matter of will, not feasibility.
“China is building 39 plus or minus nuclear reactors. 34 of them are 1 gawatt plus um I think a third of those are modeled after the AP 1000.” ▶ 32:17
Losers: Consumers, Turbine and Fuel-Cell Makers, Hyperscalers 6
The US consumer is the biggest loser, facing rising electricity bills and an 'awful trade-off' between exporting gas, powering AI compute, or keeping consumer bills low, likely fueling more NIMBYism and public backlash.
prediction🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highUS consumerelectricity pricesAI computeLNG exports
So what: Political backlash over consumer bills is itself a risk to LNG export and data-center buildout policy; a regulatory/political overhang.
“the biggest losers of this would be the US consumer” ▶ 0:25
Gas-turbine and distributed power-gen manufacturers (Caterpillar doubling Solar turbine capacity through end of 2029) are adding capacity at the exact wrong time, repeating the early-2000s boom-bust, as expensive gas may make buyers question deploying those assets.
contrarian🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highCaterpillargas turbinesdistributed generation
So what: Turbine/genset makers that have had huge stock runs may face an order air-pocket; a contrarian short/underweight into 2028-2030.
“Caterpillar is I think they're doubling their uh, solar turbine capacity between now and the end of 29, which I would judge is just at the exact wrong time” ▶ 36:00
Bloom Energy is poorly positioned because its fuel cells at 2 GW-plus scale won't be able to secure natural gas in competition with other assets amid the scarcity, so they are treated only as backup generation in the base case.
contrarian🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highBloom Energyfuel cellsnatural gas
So what: Bloom's scale story may be structurally un-fuelable; if deployed as base load it only adds to the convexity, a bearish setup.
“we don't think that Bloom Energy's assets at 2 gawatt or more will be able to get natural gas in competition with all of the other assets that are being deployed” ▶ 36:24
Orders for new large-scale natural gas generation could slow very meaningfully starting as 2026 progresses, because it won't make sense to build gas plants after ~2029-2030 until production ramps and deliverability security improves.
prediction🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: mediumgas-fired generationnatural gas
So what: A slowing new-gas-plant order cycle is a leading indicator and a headwind for gas-turbine OEM backlogs.
“we we could see orders slow very meaningfully for natural gas generating assets even at large scale as 26 progresses” ▶ 37:07
Energy is budgeted at ~10% of hyperscaler cost today (behind depreciation), but if gas-powered compute doubles or triples structurally, energy could become 20-30-40% of the cost of compute by 2029, threatening hyperscaler profitability.
data_point🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: mediumhyperscalersAI computenatural gasLCOE
So what: A gas-price shock is a material, under-modeled hit to hyperscaler margins; performance-per-watt becomes a key metric to track.
“energy is total cost of energy is supposed to be about 10%. If you plug in all of this compute and it's gas powered and we think gas could double or triple structurally even without weather, it could end up being 20 or 30% of the cost of compute by 2029.” ▶ 38:16
EPC/engineering-construction firms building gas plants and trading at ~25x cash flow (a historically high multiple) are exposed if gas-plant builds stall in 2029-2030, and should pursue accretive M&A to diversify away from gas-generation dependence.
thesis🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: mediumEPC firmsgas plants
So what: High-multiple gas-EPC names carry undocumented backlog risk; watch for diversifying M&A as a defensive signal.
“trading at 25 times cash flow which is a historically high multiple for a engineering construction firm and your main business is building natural gas plants” ▶ 56:34
Policy Fixes and Global Implications 5
The single highest-impact government action would be to fully build 2-4 AP-1000 nuclear plants (potentially funding it from the ~$260 billion Loan Program Office budget) to derisk the supply chain and catalyze a broader nuclear buildout, since currently no one wants to go first.
framework🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highUS governmentAP-1000Loan Program Officenuclear
So what: Government first-mover nuclear commitments are the key catalyst that could jumpstart the AP-1000 supply chain; a policy trigger to watch.
“I would find a way to build entirely from beginning to end two to four AP-1000 nuclear plants” ▶ 45:36
A 1-2 BCF/day pipeline from Canada into the US Midwest (MISO) could help, since Canada has the deepest, richest economic gas resource but it is trapped behind pipe; currently Canada only supplies ~11-12 BCF seasonally in January and has limited storage (~0.5 TCF, a quarter of the US).
framework🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: mediumCanadaMISOPJMnatural gas pipelines
So what: Cross-border pipeline capacity is a multi-year relief valve; Canadian gas resource and pipeline projects into MISO are worth tracking.
“Canada by far has the deepest and richest resource of economic gas in the ground, but it's been trapped behind pipe” ▶ 48:42
If the US cannot deliver its planned 30-35+ BCF/day of LNG, global rebalancing forces Europe to choose between Russian gas or much costlier US gas, while Asia (the largest LNG consumer) is squeezed, potentially harming key allies.
causal🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: mediumLNG exportsEuropeAsiaRussia
So what: US LNG curtailment has geopolitical spillover; global gas and European power prices are downstream of US domestic tightness.
“you probably impact Europe meaningfully and they're left with a trade-off of taking Russian gas or U or or or much much higher cost US gas” ▶ 50:11
A step-function battery-technology breakthrough (e.g. sodium-ion beyond today's fast-discharge lithium-ion) is the main disruption risk that could invalidate the thesis, and the speaker would welcome it as it would solve the problem.
framework🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: mediumbatteriessodium-ionlithium-ion
So what: Battery breakthroughs are the key bear-case risk to gas-long positioning; monitor non-lithium storage commercialization.
“step function battery battery technology change could be something that would would affect you”
The key challenge for any energy-input business is to lock up physical gas supply now and understand counterparty risk, which will become very meaningful in 2-3 years and has been ignored during the AI boom.
framework🗣 Matt (guest, energy investor)conviction: highnatural gascounterparty riskhyperscalers
So what: Physical gas provisioning and counterparty exposure are under-priced risks; firms that secured supply early are advantaged.
“Make sure you have physical supply locked up and that you understand your counterparties and what will likely be very meaningful counterparty risk in two or three years.” ▶ 55:07

a16z Podcast 1 episode(s) · 39 insights

Why Physical AI Is the Next Frontier | The a16z Show80 min39 insights2026-07-21
▶ Watch on YouTube
Applied Intuition co-founders Qasar (CEO) and Peter (CTO) argue physical AI will create companies bigger than digital-AI winners, walk through self-driving/trucking/robotaxi timelines and unit economics, dissect why Cruise failed inside GM, and launch Dana, an agentic platform meant to let a high-school kid build autonomous systems.
Physical AI TAM vs digital AI · 5Business model, moat & the legacy-automaker trap · 8Self-driving car timelines & unit economics · 6Autonomous trucking, mining & the labor thesis · 5Training stack: data, RL & synthetic data moat · 4Dana launch: democratizing autonomy · 3World models, simulation & the real-time moat · 4Sovereign AI & global strategy · 4
Physical AI TAM vs digital AI 5
Applied Intuition's founders argue that in the current intelligence revolution the companies that impact the physical world may end up bigger than the digital-AI winners, because physical AI touches manufacturing, mining, logistics and transportation rather than ads and software.
thesis🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highApplied Intuitionphysical AIdigital AIAmazonApple
So what: Frames physical-AI infrastructure as a potentially larger investable category than the current digital-AI/LLM trade.
“the companies that impact the physical world, you know, might actually be bigger than the companies that impact the digital world.” ▶ 3:25
Automotive is only about 30% of Applied Intuition's business today (70% non-automotive) and is expected to shrink as a share, with the founders framing autonomy's real markets as defense, construction, mining, agriculture and logistics.
data_point🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highApplied Intuitionautomotivedefenseminingagricultureconstruction
So what: Signals autonomy TAM is far broader than the crowded robotaxi/self-driving-car narrative investors anchor on.
“the automotive is like 30% of our business. So 70% already is non-automotive” ▶ 4:05
Automotive represents roughly 3% of global GDP, so even as a minority of Applied Intuition's business it remains a very large market by itself.
data_point🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: mediumautomotiveGDP
So what: Sizing anchor for the automotive slice of the autonomy opportunity.
“Automotive is something like 3% of all GDP.” ▶ 4:41
Removing the human from a machine's cab is itself a design unlock: without a driver the machine can be smaller and differently shaped, especially underground where the human (needing air and safety) is the binding constraint, enabling entirely new machine form factors.
framework🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: mediumminingautonomy
So what: Points to greenfield hardware categories, not just retrofitting existing vehicles, as autonomy matures.
“The constraint actually is the human because the human needs to breathe and it's very dangerous and so you can build a very very different machine.” ▶ 6:46
The bigger unlock is system-level intelligence across an entire port, mine or quarry where heterogeneous machines talk to each other and reroute when one goes down, plus predictive maintenance (e.g. knowing a brake system will fail before it does) that human-driven fleets cannot do.
thesis🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highportsminingpredictive maintenance
So what: Fleet/site-level orchestration and uptime, not per-vehicle autonomy, may be where the durable value and pricing power sit.
Business model, moat & the legacy-automaker trap 8
Applied Intuition has raised over ~$1 billion across its history and says all of it is still sitting in the bank, giving it flexibility on how aggressively to pursue its adjacent markets.
data_point🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highApplied Intuition
So what: A large un-spent war chest signals capacity to fund expansion into defense/mining/ag without near-term financing pressure.
“we've uh raised over about a billion dollars in the company's history all that is sitting in the bank” ▶ 9:42
Applied Intuition runs over 1,000 engineers (83% of the company is engineering) across 18 global offices, has deployed its models onto 50+ hardware platforms, and holds hundreds of petabytes of proprietary data with one of the largest data-collection fleets on the planet.
data_point🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highApplied Intuition
So what: Quantifies the engineering and data moat that would-be competitors must replicate to compete in physical AI.
“83% of the company is engineering” ▶ 2:08
Applied Intuition positions itself as the horizontal 'chipmaker' of autonomy analogous to Nvidia: it sells intelligence into partners' machines, wins on design-ins and deep long-term trust, and is hard to rip out once embedded rather than going vertical like Tesla or Waymo.
framework🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highApplied IntuitionNvidiaTeslaWaymo
So what: A horizontal supplier model implies recurring, sticky revenue across many OEMs rather than winner-take-all robotaxi competition.
“we're kind of like a like a chipmaker” ▶ 29:43
Applied Intuition already runs self-driving trucks carrying commercial loads in Japan under the Isuzu brand (with safety drivers), illustrating its white-label strategy of letting ~century-old manufacturers own distribution and brand while Applied supplies the intelligence.
anecdote🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highIsuzuJapanautonomous truckingApplied Intuition
So what: Proof point that revenue is live and hidden inside OEM brands; a lens for spotting Applied's footprint via its manufacturer partners.
“run self-driving trucks right now in Japan. They carry commercial loads.” ▶ 28:26
Legacy automakers are increasingly choosing 'buy' over 'build' for autonomy because they have tried and failed to build it internally, which favors third-party intelligence suppliers over in-house OEM programs.
thesis🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: mediumautomotive OEMsHondaNissanToyota
So what: Bullish for autonomy-software vendors; bearish for OEM captive self-driving efforts.
“more than ever we're going to buy is the common answer because they've been trying and we've been there the whole time.” ▶ 30:45
The founders attribute Cruise's shutdown not to bad technology but to GM's institutional risk aversion, a business-model conflict (robotaxis vs GM's personal-car-ownership profits), and Cruise mishandling the government relationship after a pedestrian was dragged ~20 feet.
causal🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highCruiseGeneral Motors
So what: Warns that autonomy outcomes hinge on regulatory 'dance' and parent-company incentives as much as tech; a template for reading other OEM-owned AV programs.
“you got to you got to dance a particular way when that happens and they just didn't dance exactly right” ▶ 26:10
The founders argue Cruise was actually moving faster than Waymo and was neck-and-neck despite starting behind when GM pulled the plug, implying the shutdown was a strategic/governance loss rather than a technical one.
contrarian🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: mediumCruiseWaymoGeneral Motors
So what: Reframes GM's exit as a self-inflicted forfeit, relevant when valuing surviving AV players' competitive position.
Most major legacy manufacturers still run on the ~1920s Sloan/Kettering matrix-org 'source code' with extreme safety/litigation caution (three of the top five US consumer lawsuits are automotive), which structurally slows their ability to ship Silicon-Valley-pace technology.
framework🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highGeneral Motorsautomotive OEMsJohn DeLorean
So what: Explains why partnering with, rather than competing against, incumbents is the higher-probability path in physical AI.
“three are automotive. We got the majority right so it's like you have to be extremely careful.” ▶ 23:53
Self-driving car timelines & unit economics 6
The gating factor for ubiquitous personal self-driving is cost, not capability: driver-in-seat L2++ packages (chip, sensors, software) are heading toward sub-$1,000, and once they hit ~$500 the founders expect OEMs to give them away for free, the way paid nav systems became default.
prediction🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highL2++ autonomyautomotive OEMsTesla FSD
So what: Cost curve, not R&D breakthroughs, is the investable variable; watch BOM/sensor pricing as the adoption trigger.
“once you get to like 500 the automotive OEMs will actually subsidize it for free they'll just give it to you this happened in nav systems” ▶ 36:29
The founders predict driver-in-seat self-driving reaches start-of-production ubiquity around model years 2028-2030 and becomes cheap-to-free by the early 2030s, so buying a car and simply assuming self-driving becomes routine by the early 2030s.
prediction🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highautomotive OEMsself-driving cars
So what: Concrete adoption calendar for pricing the mass-market ADAS/autonomy ramp.
“28 SOP, 29, start of production, 29, 30, and then by the early 30s, it'll start becoming very cheap to free.” ▶ 37:55
The founders forecast robotaxis will be available in the ~200 biggest US cities by 2030 but only truly routine around 2032-2033 (aggressive case ~2028), gated by capital-intensive scale-up rather than technology.
prediction🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: mediumWaymorobotaxi
So what: Sets a multi-year gap between 'available' and 'routine' robotaxi coverage for timing AV-adjacent bets.
“I would say available in 30 but routine and maybe like 32. Yeah, 33.” ▶ 41:36
The Waymo-vs-Tesla race is framed as a cost/geography problem, not a science problem: Waymo relies on HD maps (implying geofencing) and bespoke expensive sensors/compute from an Alphabet-research lineage with no commercial constraints, while Tesla/Chinese/Applied use single end-to-end model architectures; nobody is debating whether autonomy happens.
framework🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highWaymoTeslaAlphabetend-to-end modelsHD maps
So what: The debate is 'who gets cost/ubiquity first,' so the market should reward whoever bends the dollar-per-mile curve fastest.
“Who's going to get there first? Tesla with full self-driving or Whimo with cost and geographic ubiquity.” ▶ 39:37
The founders warn that if you run the numbers, Waymo eating into ride-sharing means Uber 'has to be scared,' since robotaxis are cutting directly into ride-share volume.
thesis🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: mediumUberWaymoride-sharing
So what: Direct negative read-through for Uber/ride-share economics as robotaxi city coverage scales.
“if you're Uber you got to be scared. I mean they're just eating into into ride sharing.” ▶ 42:12
Tesla FSD v14 is cited as a step-change where many people felt it 'turned the corner,' with miles-per-disengagement now in the thousands, including anecdotes of the system driving the full length of Big Sur's Highway 1 unaided.
data_point🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: mediumTeslaFSD
So what: Supports the view that consumer L2++ has crossed a usability threshold, accelerating OEM competitive pressure to match it.
“miles per disengagement are are really high. I think miles is like in the thousands.” ▶ 34:35
Autonomous trucking, mining & the labor thesis 5
Autonomous trucking is a pure 'calculator' business unlike consumer AV: buyers demand proven dollars-saved-per-mile with no valuation premium, and the founders say north of five companies already run long-haul trucks with drivers carrying loads in the US (double digits including China).
framework🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highautonomous truckinglong-haul truckingChina
So what: Trucking ROI is measurable today, but lacks the market-cap optionality investors give robotaxi players; value the two segments differently.
“you buy a car with your heartstrings. You buy a truck with a calculator.” ▶ 43:08
The bottleneck to removing safety drivers from trucks is not software but redundant hardware (fully redundant steering and braking) and its validation, which is a long pole until such systems reach high-value production and drive costs down.
causal🗣 Peter (Applied Intuition co-founder/CTO)conviction: highautonomous truckingredundant hardware
So what: Points investors to redundant actuation/brake suppliers as the real gating dependency for driverless trucking economics.
“productionizing a fully redundant steering system, fully redundant braking system, that's that's not in high that's not in high value production yet.” ▶ 49:40
The founders argue the trucking 'jobs apocalypse' narrative is backwards: there aren't enough truck drivers and nobody wants the job because commercial long-haul drivers have life expectancy ~10 years below peers (obesity, heart disease, vibration, left-arm melanoma) and are away from family for 4-8 days.
contrarian🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highlong-haul truckinglabor shortage
So what: Reframes autonomous trucking as filling a structural labor shortage, reducing the regulatory/political backlash risk investors fear.
“there's not enough truck drivers and guess what? Nobody wants to freaking be a truck driver.” ▶ 45:21
Mining accounts for ~1% of the global labor pool but ~8% of work-related fatalities, and agriculture faces a demographic cliff (average US farmer age 58, under-35s less than 10%), so demand for automation in these sectors comes from operators who 'want it today.'
data_point🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highminingagriculturelabor shortage
So what: Non-automotive autonomy demand is pull-driven by labor scarcity, implying faster adoption and less price resistance than consumer AV.
“Mining is 1% of the labor pool globally, 8% of work related fatalities.” ▶ 48:10
Autonomy could collapse goods-transport cost from a few dollars a mile toward ~20 cents a mile, and cut food costs by making agriculture far more efficient, producing large downstream deflationary effects.
prediction🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: mediumlogisticsagriculturetransportation
So what: A ~10x transport-cost reduction reshapes logistics, retail and supply-chain economics if realized.
“instead of a few dollars a mile it's 20 cents a mile” ▶ 8:28
Training stack: data, RL & synthetic data moat 4
Physical-AI foundation models can't be trained purely on internet data like LLMs; they require heavy proprietary physical data collection, which Applied Intuition has spent years securing (including gaining government permission to map/collect in restricted markets like Korea, the Middle East and Latin America).
framework🗣 Peter (Applied Intuition co-founder/CTO)conviction: highphysical AIfoundation modelsproprietary dataKorea
So what: Data-access relationships with governments are a structural moat that pure-software AI entrants can't easily buy.
“to actually build a foundation model in physical AI, there's also a lot of private data collection.” ▶ 13:45
The state of the art in self-driving has shifted from imitation learning (models copying human drivers) to end-to-end reinforcement learning in a closed loop, where the system identifies its own failure scenarios and synthetically generates data to fix them, trending toward a fully human-free closed loop.
framework🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highreinforcement learningimitation learningsynthetic dataTesla
So what: The winning technical approach is closed-loop RL plus synthetic data; a screen for which AV players are on the modern architecture.
“The real state-of-the-art right now is endtoend reinforcement learning in in a closed loop” ▶ 17:55
Applied Intuition started its synthetic-data team over five years ago and treats synthetic data plus neural sim as an accelerant for autonomy development, betting early that synthetic data would be decisive.
anecdote🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highsynthetic dataneural simApplied Intuition
So what: Multi-year head start in synthetic data compounds the data moat versus newer entrants.
“we started our synthetic data team like 5 years ago now plus” ▶ 17:01
The transformer revolution hitting self-driving around 2021-2022 made most pre-2021 self-driving work a mere starting point, marking a discontinuity that reset the competitive field.
causal🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: mediumtransformersself-drivingTesla
So what: Legacy AV IP predating ~2021 may be less durable than headcount/history suggests; recency of the stack matters.
Dana launch: democratizing autonomy 3
Applied Intuition is launching Dana, an agentic platform for physical AI (both onboard model dev and offboard tooling) that packages a decade of its tooling so that workflows which took days or weeks now run in minutes, with the explicit goal of letting a high-school kid build autonomous systems.
thesis🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highDanaApplied Intuitionagentic AIhumanoidsdrones
So what: The company's biggest product bet; a platform play to become the developer standard (IDE) for physical AI.
“That platform for designing and developing is what we're launching. It's called Dana.” ▶ 0:45
Applied Intuition is deliberately willing to arm competitors with Dana, using the Google analogy (Google thrived via search/YouTube even as it enabled the broader web), betting the platform TAM outweighs cannibalization of its own autonomy deployments.
framework🗣 Peter (Applied Intuition co-founder/CTO)conviction: highDanaGoogleYouTube
So what: Signals a platform/ecosystem strategy that trades near-term exclusivity for developer lock-in and scale.
≈ “are you going to like enable all these competitors that's great >> right >> that's absolutely completely fine” paraphrase
The founders expect that collapsing autonomy development cost toward zero (via Dana) triggers a mobile-app-style Cambrian explosion of niche bots (delivery robots, leaf-picking lawn bots, dog-poop bots, agriculture/construction/defense) analogous to Instagram/Uber emerging ~4 years after the iPhone.
prediction🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: mediumDanahumanoidsdelivery robotsiPhone
So what: If tooling truly lowers the barrier, expect a wave of new physical-AI startups and long-tail hardware categories.
World models, simulation & the real-time moat 4
Simulation for physical AI spans a spectrum from deterministic physics-based sim (CGI-style hand-built assets, with a sim-to-real gap) through Gaussian-based 3D-consistent representations to fully neural simulation that generates reactive video feeds, with each point useful for different training jobs.
framework🗣 Peter (Applied Intuition co-founder/CTO)conviction: highworld modelssimulationGaussian splattingneural simulation
So what: A concrete map of the world-model landscape for evaluating simulation/world-model startups' claims.
The real moat in physical AI is real-time performance under safety and determinism constraints: unlike LLM labs that can run slow trillion-parameter models, onboard physical-AI models must fit tight millisecond budgets on constrained hardware, which is the hard, defensible part.
thesis🗣 Peter (Applied Intuition co-founder/CTO)conviction: highphysical AIonboard modelsreal-time inference
So what: Edge/real-time constraints, not raw model quality, are where durable differentiation lives in autonomy.
“the labs they they have it easy because they they can they can make models that are trillions of parameters and those models can be super slow and that's fine but we don't have that luxury” ▶ 1:04:46
Peter predicts GTA6 will be the last major real-world video game built in the legacy traditional-computer-graphics pipeline, and GTA7 will likely be a world-model-based game, mirroring the same generative shift already hitting Hollywood.
prediction🗣 Peter (Applied Intuition co-founder/CTO)conviction: lowGrand Theft Autoworld modelsvideo gamesHollywood
So what: A speculative but concrete marker for when generative world models displace traditional 3D content pipelines.
“Grand Theft Grand Theft Auto 6 will be perhaps the last major uh realworld video game that's still really developed let's say in that legacy era of traditional computer graphics tooling” ▶ 1:06:21
Humanoid laundry-folding is 'not terribly far' from being solved once the time constraint is removed (research videos are sped up 8x), and the hardware is now fast and accurate enough; reaching human-parity speed is the remaining harder gap, with housekeeping and entertainment cited as the killer humanoid use cases.
prediction🗣 Peter (Applied Intuition co-founder/CTO)conviction: mediumhumanoidslaundry foldinghousekeepingentertainment
So what: Humanoid capability is now bottlenecked on speed/reliability, not feasibility; watch human-parity throughput as the milestone.
“the trick that's played and if you look at the latest research videos is they'll say like play it at 8x real or whatever” ▶ 1:10:38
Sovereign AI & global strategy 4
Sovereign AI is fundamentally about physical AI (defense and physical machines), and as geopolitics fractures, countries will increasingly resist foreign robotaxis/AV deployment and favor local players, following an internet-to-social-to-rideshare pattern of rising localization resistance.
thesis🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highsovereign AIphysical AIWaymoPony.aiChina
So what: A horizontal, locally-collaborative tech provider is better positioned than a vertical foreign operator as sovereignty demands rise.
“Sovereign AI really is about physical AI because that's where you're talking about AI in defense.” ▶ 11:48
Applied Intuition frames itself as an intentionally global, horizontal technology provider working collaboratively with local economies (offices everywhere except China), invoking the Standard Oil/Aramco precedent that great companies were built around geopolitical realities.
framework🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: mediumApplied IntuitionStandard OilAramcoChinaJapan
So what: International-by-design positioning is a competitive edge over myopic Silicon Valley peers as physical AI localizes.
“we don't have an office in China but really everyone else on the globe.” ▶ 1:18:25
China's EV/AV ecosystem is structurally different because many players don't care about profits, which changes the entire competitive calculus and lets Chinese L2++ systems proliferate on economics Western profit-seeking OEMs can't match.
causal🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: mediumChinaEVL2++ autonomyautomotive OEMs
So what: Chinese non-profit-maximizing behavior is a margin threat to Western OEMs and a reason autonomy pricing races to the bottom.
“China is kind of a different EV ecosystem mainly because they don't care about profits.” ▶ 35:53
The founders cite the 2013 YC-talk framework that timing is the decisive variable in new-technology businesses: two years early and you're doomed, two years late and there are too many competitors, so market entry timing beats the technology itself.
framework🗣 Qasar (Applied Intuition co-founder/CEO)conviction: highY Combinatormarket timing
So what: A reminder to weight deployment timing over technical prowess when underwriting autonomy bets.
“You're two years early and you're doomed. You're two years late, there's too many competitors.” ▶ 27:57

New Era Finance Podcast 1 episode(s) · 38 insights

Lawrence Lepard: Buy Bitcoin Now Before It Hits $200,000 in 202761 min38 insights2026-07-21
▶ Watch on YouTube
Lawrence Lepard argues Bitcoin at ~$58,000 is deeply discounted versus its power-law median (~$134,000) and is buying both Bitcoin and MicroStrategy/Strategy into the drawdown, framing the current selloff as a false 'monetary-debasement-trade-is-dead' narrative. He lays out a longer-run thesis of unavoidable fiat debasement, a coming 'big print,' and an eventual forced return to sound money. (Note: the video title's '$200,000 in 2027' does not appear in the transcript; Lepard's stated working model is Bitcoin near $180,000 roughly two years out from ~$60,000.)
MicroStrategy / Strategy as levered Bitcoin · 9Bitcoin power-law valuation · 6Bitcoin-gold lag and the debasement selloff · 5Fed policy, inflation and the 'big print' · 7Sound money endgame and the fourth turning · 6Portfolio construction and volatility · 5
MicroStrategy / Strategy as levered Bitcoin 9
Lawrence Lepard is buying MicroStrategy (Strategy) into the drawdown because he views it as a leveraged play on Bitcoin whose only real failure mode is Bitcoin itself failing, which he does not expect.
thesis🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highMicroStrategyStrategyBitcoinMichael Saylor
So what: A high-conviction contrarian long on MSTR at a distressed level, tied directly to Bitcoin recovery rather than company-specific solvency risk.
“Micro Strategy is going to be absolutely fine if Bitcoin continues to go up the way it has historically over fiveyear periods. Um if Bitcoin fails, Micro Strategy is going to fail and it's going to fail faster” ▶ 2:18
MicroStrategy stock dipped below an mNAV of 1, which Lepard considers extraordinarily cheap relative to the value of its underlying Bitcoin.
data_point🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highMicroStrategymNAVBitcoin
So what: An mNAV below 1 signals the market is pricing the equity below its Bitcoin holdings, the entry condition Lepard is buying on.
“it it it dipped kind of below an MNAV of one” ▶ 2:52
Lepard dismisses liquidation and liquidity-cascade fears for MicroStrategy, noting the preferred has no redemption feature, the dividend could be cut if forced, and the first maturing debt is a couple years out and modest versus the capital structure.
causal🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highMicroStrategyPeter Schiffpreferred stock
So what: Directly rebuts the Peter Schiff-style forced-seller thesis; the balance-sheet structure removes the near-term liquidation trigger bears are pricing.
“I think it's a bulletproof balance sheet. I think they're going to make it through this no problem.” ▶ 3:19
MicroStrategy's Monday announcement that it will sell Bitcoin to buy back common shares when the stock trades below fair value is designed to deter speculative short-selling attacks on the common.
causal🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highMicroStrategyBitcoinshort sellers
So what: A structural backstop that puts a floor under the common and penalizes shorts, changing the risk/reward for betting against MSTR here.
“by announcing that he was willing to sell Bitcoin to buy common shares if the common shares become undervalued, you know, that's preventing a speculative shortselling attack on the common shares” ▶ 11:14
Lepard models that if Bitcoin rises 3x over two years (from ~$60,000 to ~$180,000), MicroStrategy could rise roughly 6x, with a minimum of 4-5x, given assumptions about mNAV multiples.
prediction🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: mediumMicroStrategyBitcoinmNAV
So what: Quantifies the embedded leverage: MSTR offers roughly double Bitcoin's upside in a bull case, at the cost of amplified downside in a bear market.
“Bitcoin would be up 3x micro strategy might be up 6xes based on the numbers that I ran and it kind of you have to make some assumptions about MNAV multiples and so forth but at a minimum it'd be up four to 5x” ▶ 13:33
MicroStrategy only needs Bitcoin to appreciate roughly 3% per year to cover its preferred dividend obligation, versus Bitcoin's historical annual return in the 40s trending toward the 20s-30s, well above its 12% cost of preferred capital.
data_point🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highMicroStrategyBitcoinpreferred stock
So what: The wide gap between Bitcoin's expected return and the 12% cost of capital is the core carry that makes the levered structure accretive to common shareholders.
“As long as Bitcoin goes up faster than his cost of capital, the shareholders will have an appreciating stock.” ▶ 12:45
MicroStrategy raised its preferred dividend from 11.5% to 12% after the STRC preferred fell from a $100 par value into the 70s in a liquidity cascade, and publicly detailed how it would cover the dividend.
data_point🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highMicroStrategySTRCpreferred stock
So what: Concrete evidence of the preferred's stress and the company's real-time response; the STRC drop was a specific tradable dislocation Lepard bought.
“we're going to take the dividend from 11 and a half to 12” ▶ 18:17
Lepard argues MicroStrategy's failure would not threaten Bitcoin itself because it holds about 4% of Bitcoin, a large but non-controlling stake, in a market with roughly $30 billion of daily trading versus the ~$1.7 billion annual preferred dividend obligation.
data_point🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highMicroStrategyBitcoin
So what: Decouples the 'MSTR blows up, Bitcoin blows up' contagion trade; the size mismatch means MSTR's flows are small relative to Bitcoin liquidity.
“He's got 4% of it. That's a big number, but it's not control of it in any sense.” ▶ 6:13
Lepard calls comparisons of MicroStrategy to FTX absurd, stressing that Strategy is a heavily regulated ~$50 billion market-cap company with every position, obligation and debt disclosed in real time on its website.
