contrarian🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highMicroStrategyFTXStrategy
So what: Pushes back on the fraud/opacity framing driving bearishness; transparency and regulation remove the FTX-style hidden-liability risk.
“it's a heavy heavily regulated, you know, $50 billion market cap company. I mean, this is not, you know, there's no slight of hand here.” ▶ 16:27
Bitcoin power-law valuation 6
Lepard values Bitcoin off a power-law model whose median value is about $134,000, so buying at ~$58,000 represents a steep discount to fair value.
framework🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highBitcoinpower law model
So what: Gives a concrete fair-value anchor (~$134k) implying Bitcoin trades at roughly 43% of model value, the basis for his 'on sale' call.
“the power law median value is like 134,000 so you're if you're buying Bitcoin today at $58,000 you're you're really getting quite a discount” ▶ 0:24
Lepard says his power-law model has a 96% correlation with Bitcoin's price data since launch, which is why he places heavy faith in it as a valuation tool.
data_point🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highBitcoinpower law model
So what: The claimed 96% fit is his justification for treating current prices as a mean-reversion buy rather than a broken thesis.
“that model has a 96% correlation with with the data since the time Bitcoin was launched” ▶ 9:02
Lepard expects Bitcoin to make new all-time highs within the next one to two years and works off a model of roughly $180,000 about two years out from ~$60,000.
prediction🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highBitcoin
So what: A specific price/time target (~$180k in ~2 years, ~3x) that anchors both his Bitcoin and MSTR trades; note it undershoots the title's $200k/2027 framing.
“my my working model is kind of two years out we'll be at 180” ▶ 13:17
Lepard frames Bitcoin as an emerging monetary network analogous to early Amazon, which corrected 50-90% repeatedly before becoming dominant, implying today's drawdown is a normal early-adoption dislocation.
framework🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highBitcoinAmazonMichael Saylor
So what: Reframes volatility as characteristic of a winning early network, supporting holding/buying through 50%+ drawdowns.
“just like people didn't understand Amazon and Amazon was correcting 90%, 70%, 50% in the early days, you know, now it's an enormous business. I mean, I think the same will be true with Bitcoin.” ▶ 15:14
Lepard sizes Bitcoin's long-run upside at potentially 10x from current levels and then another 10x over a 15-20 year horizon, driven by its tiny share of the ~$1,000 trillion of global financial assets.
prediction🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: mediumBitcoinJesse Meyergold
So what: Frames the asymmetry: Bitcoin at ~$1.3T versus gold's ~$30T and $1,000T total assets leaves large room for monetary share gains.
“I think Bitcoin will go up 10x from here and then it'll go up 10x again I mean we're talking over a 15 20 year time frame” ▶ 50:33
Lepard argues Bitcoin is a sounder form of money than gold because its annual supply dilution is lower on a stock-to-flow basis and it is easier to move, store and verify.
thesis🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highBitcoingold
So what: The monetary-superiority argument underpins expected share migration from gold's ~$30T market to Bitcoin over time.
“it's dilution is smaller per year than gold on a stock toflow basis. So, it's much easier to move than gold. It's much easier to store than gold. It's much easier to verify than gold.” ▶ 19:38
Bitcoin-gold lag and the debasement selloff 5
Lepard attributes the top reason for the Bitcoin, gold and silver selloff to a market belief that the 'monetary debasement trade is dead,' driven by expectations of a more hawkish Fed under Kevin Warsh raising rates and slowing M2 growth.
causal🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highBitcoingoldsilverFederal ReserveKevin WarshM2
So what: Identifies the narrative he thinks is wrong; if the hawkish-Fed premise fails, all three debasement assets re-rate together.
“people are kind of thinking well the monetary debasement trade is dead” ▶ 24:20
Lepard says gold typically moves first and Bitcoin follows with sharper, faster gains, citing 2020 when gold ran from ~$1,300 to ~$2,000 while Bitcoin flatlined near $10,000 before surging 5x to ~$50,000 in seven to eight months.
framework🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highgoldBitcoinFederal ReserveM2
So what: A lead-lag playbook: gold's recent bid may presage a delayed but violent Bitcoin catch-up move.
“it went from 10 to 50 in like seven or eight months, 5x, right?” ▶ 20:41
Lepard notes gold corrected from about $5,400 to touch $4,000 (around $4,330 that morning) after rising roughly 60% last year, and sees fresh bids returning to gold as a leading signal for Bitcoin.
data_point🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: mediumgoldBitcoin
So what: A returning gold bid, per his lead-lag model, is the tell to watch before Bitcoin's next leg higher.
“gold corrected pretty severely from 5,400 into the, you know, 4,000s range, you know, touched 4,000” ▶ 22:26
Lepard says the recent Bitcoin drawdown was tightly correlated to the fall in the STRC preferred rather than broad crypto weakness, evidenced by some altcoins holding up better than usual.
causal🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: mediumBitcoinSTRCaltcoinsMicroStrategy
So what: Implies the selloff was a MicroStrategy-linked, mechanical dislocation, not a fundamental crypto breakdown, supporting a mean-reversion buy.
Lepard argues silver's ~100%+ rise from under $50 to $120 last year, alongside gold's ~60% gain, was part of the same sound-money repricing now being unwound on hawkish-Fed fears.
data_point🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: mediumsilvergoldBitcoin
So what: Silver's magnitude of move and subsequent ~50% drawdown illustrate the high-beta nature of the debasement basket he expects to re-rate.
“Silver went up a huge amount, you know, 100 plus percent went from, you know, under 50 to 120” ▶ 24:35
Fed policy, inflation and the 'big print' 7
Lepard argues new Fed chair Kevin Warsh cannot deliver on his stated mandate to stop inflation and reduce the balance sheet because a credit-based system requires continual growth in credit and money supply or it collapses.
thesis🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highKevin WarshFederal Reserveinflation
So what: If the hawkish pivot is structurally impossible, positioning for renewed easing/debasement is the higher-probability trade.
“he's gaslighting us because he's lying. He knows he can't. He says he wants to reduce the balance sheet. You can't do that either.” ▶ 27:17
Lepard says last year's gold, silver and Bitcoin rally was triggered when DOGE (the government-efficiency effort) failed, with promised savings collapsing from $2 trillion to $1 trillion to roughly $100 billion, removing the fiscal-responsibility threat to the debasement thesis.
causal🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highDOGEElon MuskgoldsilverBitcoin
So what: Frames fiscal restraint as the one real risk to sound-money assets; its failure is bullish, and continued deficits keep the thesis intact.
“the two trillion they were going to save, which then got amended to one trillion. Kind of turned out to be maybe 100 billion, if that.” ▶ 26:50
Lepard forecasts a third 'big print,' pointing to the pattern of the Fed balance sheet expanding from ~$800 billion to ~$3 trillion after 2008 and from ~$3 trillion to ~$9 trillion (about $5 trillion in 18 months) in 2020, with another expansion required to support growing debt.
prediction🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highFederal ReserveBen BernankeJerome Powelldebt
So what: The repeatable QE pattern is his core reason to hold debasement-insurance assets ahead of the next balance-sheet expansion.
“there's another one coming [laughter] because because the debt keeps growing and you need the money to support the debt” ▶ 56:11
Lepard estimates U.S. AI and data-center capex at roughly $600 billion this year heading toward $1 trillion, which he says is flattering headline economic growth while adding inflationary demand.
data_point🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: mediumAIdata centersUS economy
So what: Signals that reported growth strength is AI-capex-driven and inflationary, undercutting the case for durable Fed hawkishness.
“there's $600 billion this year going to a trillion, you know, that's in the AI buildout and the data center buildout” ▶ 29:23
Lepard views AI as a genuinely great technology but an investment minefield because the eventual winners and economics are still unknown, and cites capital rotating from Bitcoin into AI and SpaceX as a driver of Bitcoin's selloff.
causal🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: mediumAISpaceXBitcoin
So what: Flags AI as crowded 'shiny object' flow and a rotation source out of Bitcoin, a flow that could reverse.
“I think AI is is a great technology, but I also think investment wise, it's a minefield because we don't know how it's going to play out yet” ▶ 18:51
Lepard disputes the official ~3% inflation reading, noting his grocery and insurance bills rise faster, while acknowledging alternative measures like the Dallas Trimmed Mean PCE at 2.3% and 'True Inflation' in the 1-2% range.
data_point🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: mediuminflationDallas Trimmed Mean PCECPI
So what: Highlights measurement ambiguity in inflation data, which he argues the Fed exploits to 'gaslight' markets about price stability.
“Wars has talked about using something called the Dallas TRIM PCI, which is at 2.3 right now, not three” ▶ 57:31
Lepard points to Silicon Valley Bank as evidence policymakers will always backstop the system, citing the BTFP program and Yellen's willingness to guarantee some $17 trillion of bank deposits to stop contagion.
anecdote🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highSilicon Valley BankBTFPJanet YellenFederal Reserve
So what: Reinforces the 'they will always print' base case that underwrites the debasement trade and caps deflationary tail risk.
“I thought Silicon Valley Bank was going to croak the whole system. Boy, was I wrong.” ▶ 47:42
Sound money endgame and the fourth turning 6
Lepard frames the excessive debt-to-GDP imbalance as resolvable only three ways: a 1929-style debt collapse, post-WWII-style inflating out of it, or a structured one-time reset backing the dollar with gold, Bitcoin or a commodity basket.
framework🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highdebt-to-GDPgoldBitcoinUS dollar
So what: A decision tree for the monetary endgame; two of three paths are highly inflationary, favoring hard-money assets.
Lepard argues continual currency dilution is a structural theft from the middle and lower classes while asset-owning borrowers get richer, producing the wealth inequality, K-shaped economy and political anger he links to a 'fourth turning' crisis.
thesis🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highmonetary debasementwealth inequalityfourth turning
So what: Ties monetary policy directly to political instability, implying the crisis that forces a sound-money reset is a matter of when, not if.
“that really represents a theft of the earnings and productivity of of the middle to lower classes” ▶ 33:21
Lepard names Thomas Massie, Warren Davidson, Cynthia Lummis (retiring) and Rand Paul as U.S. politicians who understand sound money and might propose a constitutional path back to it if inflation becomes the dominant political issue.
anecdote🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: mediumThomas MassieWarren DavidsonCynthia LummisRand Paulsound money
So what: Identifies the small political nucleus that could legislate a sound-money transition, a catalyst to watch if inflation dominates politics.
Lepard assigns a real 20-30% probability to U.S. dollar hyperinflation, sketching a scenario where a 2028 'blue team' sweep unleashes uncontrolled money printing and Gresham's law drives people to abandon the fiat currency.
prediction🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: mediumUS dollarhyperinflationGresham's lawStephanie KeltonMMT
So what: A non-trivial tail probability on reserve-currency hyperinflation is his ultimate justification for owning hard money as insurance.
“I don't know what is it, 20% or 30% case. I mean, it's real.” ▶ 44:48
Lepard cites over 50 hyperinflation cases in the past 200 years (Germany, Venezuela, Congo) and the Hjalmar Schacht post-Weimar reset backing money with gold and land as evidence that a sound-money fix reliably ends currency collapse.
framework🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highhyperinflationGermanyVenezuelaCongoHjalmar Schacht
So what: Historical precedent that sound-money resets work implies the eventual policy destination, and the assets that benefit, are predictable.
“in my book I outline there are 50 plus cases of hyperinflation the last you know 200 years” ▶ 45:36
Lepard's mental base case is that a forced return to a sound monetary unit happens sometime in the mid-2030s, though he concedes authorities have proven far better than he expected at 'kicking the can' over 30 years.
prediction🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: lowsound moneyUS dollar
So what: Sets a rough decade-scale horizon for the endgame while flagging that timing has repeatedly disappointed sound-money bears.
“I have a case that says that that kind of happens sometime in the mid 2030s” ▶ 47:17
Portfolio construction and volatility 5
Lepard advises average investors to size Bitcoin as monetary-debasement insurance at roughly 10-20% of net worth, arguing a 20% allocation that goes up 10x becomes 200% of net worth while a wipeout still leaves 80% intact.
framework🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highBitcoingoldportfolio allocation
So what: A concrete position-sizing rule that captures asymmetric upside while surviving Bitcoin's 50%+ drawdowns.
“I think if you don't have 10 or 20% in, you know, you'll wake up in 10 years and regret it. Um, but 20% will do it.” ▶ 51:54
Lepard recommends holding roughly 20% of assets in cash to deploy into liquidity crunches, warning that in a 1929- or 2008/2020-style panic everything correlates to one and only cash holds up before policymakers respond with a liquidity flood.
framework🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highcashBitcoingoldreal estate
So what: Cash is positioned as dry powder to buy forced-liquidation dislocations, the same pattern he says let him buy STRC in the 70s.
“You're going to get a correlation of one event. The only thing that's going to be good is cash.” ▶ 53:15
Lepard frames buying Bitcoin as a 'quiet act of rebellion' and opt-out from the fiat system, citing Jeff Booth's point that mass adoption would collapse the existing monetary system, while conceding adoption remains a small minority.
thesis🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highBitcoinJeff Boothself-sovereignty
So what: Positions Bitcoin adoption as a slow, secular political-economic shift, implying early accumulation ahead of broader opt-out.
“buying Bitcoin is kind of a quiet act of rebellion and saying, "No, I'm not going to save in your system."” ▶ 41:23
Lepard argues the durable way to survive sound-money assets' volatility is a long three-to-ten-year time horizon plus deliberate bet sizing, noting everyone who has held Bitcoin four to five years is net ahead of their entry.
framework🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highBitcoinvolatility
So what: The behavioral edge is time preference and sizing; short-horizon holders get shaken out at exactly the wrong points.
“everyone who's been in it for four or five years is is net ahead of where they bought it” ▶ 10:03
Lepard invokes Warren Buffett-style value discipline, arguing investing is the one arena where people irrationally dislike lower prices and that great investors buy when price falls below fundamental worth.
framework🗣 Lawrence Lepardconviction: highWarren BuffettBitcoin
So what: Reframes the current drawdown as a discount to intrinsic value to be bought, not a signal something is wrong.
“investing is the one area where people don't like cheaper prices” ▶ 0:13

Risk Reversal · Dan Nathan 1 episode(s) · 37 insights

This Is What an AI Unwind Looks Like33 min37 insights2026-07-20
▶ Watch on YouTube
Dan Nathan and Guy Adami argue an AI-trade unwind is underway: memory/semis (Micron, SK Hynix) are cratering, the compute-scarcity bull thesis is breaking as XAI/SpaceX, Meta and others lease out 'excess' compute on short-term contracts, and China's cheaper open-weight models (DeepSeek, Alibaba, Moonshot) are closing the gap. They see margin/valuation risk in Google, Nvidia, Dell, Netflix and Intel, while flagging Apple's contrarian breakout and a building geopolitical oil premium.
Memory/DRAM crash · 4Google margin re-rating · 6Compute-scarcity thesis breaking · 6China closing the AI gap · 4Apple's contrarian breakout · 4Nvidia and component-maker margins · 4Netflix cash-flow miss · 2Oil premium and refiners · 4Earnings previews: Intel, Capital One · 3
Memory/DRAM crash 4
Micron collapsed from an all-time high of $1,255 set around June 25 to a $800 low on Friday, and $800 also marks its prior May all-time high, making it the technical support level to watch.
data_point🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumMicronMU
So what: The prior all-time high at $800 is the line in the sand; a decisive break signals the memory selloff is structural rather than another routine dip.
“a stock that today, and again, today is Friday, traded down to $800. So, so think about that move for a second.” ▶ 1:23
The DRAM ETF grew to roughly $22 billion in AUM (over $25 billion at its highs), an unusually fast rise for a sector ETF, and it round-tripped from ~80 in early-to-mid June to a Friday low of 48.65 before bouncing to 53.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumDRAM ETFSeagateWestern DigitalMicronSamsungSK Hynix
So what: Explosive sector-ETF asset growth into the top is a classic crowding signal; the sharp intraday reversal ('spike bottom') is a tell to watch for whether memory stabilizes or breaks down.
“This ETF guy has 22 billion dollars under management at highs. I think it was over 25. I I can't remember the last time I have seen a sector ETF grow like this.” ▶ 2:27
Seagate, Western Digital, Micron, Samsung and SK Hynix make up roughly 40-45% of the DRAM ETF, and the ETF's Friday action looked like it was attempting a spike bottom while SMH and SOXX were still down 2%.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumSeagateWestern DigitalMicronSamsungSK HynixSMHSOXX
So what: Concentration in five memory names means the ETF is a clean proxy for the memory trade; a confirmed spike bottom would be an early signal the group has washed out.
The hosts have seen roughly 8% daily up-and-down moves in memory names about three times a week for the last three to four weeks, reflecting extreme volatility and indecision in the group.
data_point🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumMicronmemory
So what: 8% swings multiple times a week signal a leadership group losing its footing; heightened realized volatility often precedes a regime change in a crowded trade.
Google margin re-rating 6
A Bloomberg report that Google is pushing out its latest Gemini coding model triggered a 5% straight-line drop and a roughly 10% two-day selloff in the $4 trillion stock, reversing the week's rotation into hyperscalers.
causal🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highGoogleAlphabetGOOGLGeminiBloomberg
So what: A 10% two-day move in a $4T name on a product-delay headline shows how fragile hyperscaler sentiment is; model-cadence slippage is now a market-moving catalyst.
“the stock sold off 5% in a straight line. Okay, as we're recording right now, the stock is off two and a half percent... It was about a 10% two-day sell-off.” ▶ 3:52
Google's gross margins are expected to fall from 71% this year to about 65% next year, with earnings growth decelerating from 12% to 7%, largely because of hundreds of billions in cumulative capex funded partly by equity and debt raises.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highGoogleAlphabetGOOGL
So what: A ~6-point gross-margin compression driven by AI capex is the core bear case: the market may re-rate Google on a deteriorating margin structure even as revenue accelerates.
“Gross margins are expected to go from this year's 71% to about 65%. Think about that. To 65%. Now, what's weighing on that? Capex is a big part of that” ▶ 6:20
Google revenue growth is accelerating to 24% this year with slight deceleration to 22% next year, and the stock trades at about 23.5x both this and next year's earnings, reports Wednesday after the close, with a 7% implied earnings move.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highGoogleAlphabetGOOGL
So what: Accelerating revenue but compressing margins sets up a binary print; the 7% implied move plus a 23.5x multiple frames the risk/reward into Wednesday's report.
Guy Adami argues Google's valuation is still compelling and its search/YouTube moat intact after the pullback from a May all-time high of ~$408 (from a March low of $274) to ~$350, viewing the setup as favorable into earnings.
thesis🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumGoogleAlphabetGOOGLYouTube
So what: The bull case rests on moat durability and a de-risked post-selloff setup; the debate between moat and margin dilution is the key swing factor into the print.
Google's TPUs are competing directly with Nvidia and taking clients, and Google now has five or six products with over two billion monthly active users, supporting the 'vertical AI' bull case built on top of search.
thesis🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumGoogleTPUNvidiaGemini
So what: TPU client wins are a direct threat to Nvidia's demand base and a differentiator for Google, but the open question is whether that distribution translates into profitable monetization.
Dan Nathan cites Ben Thompson's roughly two-year-old warning that AI could be Google's 'Kodak moment' — an existential threat to search — noting it hasn't materialized yet but the risk remains live as moats and valuations can change quickly.
framework🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumGoogleBen ThompsonAI
So what: The 'Kodak moment' frame is a reminder that legacy-tech moats can erode fast; investors should treat Google's search dominance as contingent, not permanent.
“there was a potential for Google to have sort of its Kodak moment and his point was the existential risk and how quickly the ground moves beneath the feet of these legacy companies” ▶ 7:36
Compute-scarcity thesis breaking 6
The core AI bull thesis of compute scarcity is being undermined as Meta and XAI/SpaceX start renting out 'excess' compute — Meta bounced on the news — contradicting the idea that GPUs and data-center capacity are scarce.
contrarian🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highMetaXAISpaceXcompute
So what: If the biggest spenders have surplus compute to lease, the scarcity narrative underpinning AI-infrastructure valuations is false; this is a directly tradeable crack in the bull case.
“Isn't the whole story in this space about scarcity? Right? And so like help me think about that” ▶ 10:11
XAI was losing about $1 billion a month, and SpaceX's ~$250 billion property purchase only pencils because Anthropic and Google each agreed to lease its excess compute for about $1 billion just days before the IPO — but those are three-month, not long-term, contracts.
causal🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highXAISpaceXAnthropicGoogleElon Musk
So what: The valuation depends on short-dated compute leases dressed up as durable demand; if those three-month contracts aren't renewed, the entire property/valuation math collapses.
“the problem with those contracts is they were threemonth outs. Okay, we keep hearing about long-term agreements. That's the thing that sounds so durable... That's the opposite of that” ▶ 9:44
SpaceX's stock, priced at $135 and briefly trading as high as ~$185-$225, has fallen to about $125 and traded very poorly post-IPO, which the hosts warn could weigh heavily on broader market sentiment for AI names.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumSpaceXElon Musk
So what: A high-profile mega-cap IPO breaking below its offer price is a sentiment contagion risk; poor aftermarket action can freeze the pipeline for smaller AI IPOs.
Trillion-dollar AI IPOs must trade well for smaller $10-15 billion mid-cap deals to get done, so SpaceX's weak debut signals a broader closing of the IPO exit window for private AI investors.
thesis🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumSpaceXIPOAI
So what: A frozen exit path pressures private AI valuations and VC markups; watch mega-cap aftermarket performance as a leading indicator for the whole private-market cycle.
Guy Adami points to the Jim Chanos podcast as the succinct bear case, invoking 'watch the fine print' — Elon Musk's admission that the compute contracts are very short-term — as the detail that blows a hole in the scarcity story.
framework🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highJim ChanosElon Muskcompute
So what: Chanos-style skepticism reframes booked 'long-term agreements' as short-dated obligations; scrutinizing contract duration is the analytical edge in this AI cycle.
“a man hears what he wants to hear and disregards the rest. And a lot of people until recently have been disregarding all the rest.” ▶ 11:02
SK Hynix listed a $26.5 billion deal last week, adding fresh supply right in the middle of the DRAM/memory drawdown.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumSK Hynix
So what: A large new listing during a memory selloff adds supply and sentiment pressure to an already-crowded, declining group.
China closing the AI gap 4
Per Torsten Slok of Apollo, the Epoch Capabilities Index shows open-weight (Chinese) models now trail closed-weight US frontier models by only about four months, with the gap steadily narrowing.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highTorsten SlokApolloEpochChinaAI models
So what: A shrinking four-month capability gap undercuts the premium paid for US frontier labs' hundred-billion-dollar spend; commoditization risk is real and measurable.
“the openweight models uh trail the closed weight frontier models... they're trailing about four months. This gap is getting less and less.” ▶ 12:34
China is building competitive AI far more cheaply — DeepSeek raised $7 billion at a $52 billion valuation and Alibaba announced $50 billion of capex over three years — while US labs spend hundreds of billions, and China is doing it without Nvidia's most advanced chips.
contrarian🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highDeepSeekAlibabaChinaNvidia
So what: If China matches capability on a fraction of the capex and without top Nvidia chips, it pressures both US AI ROI assumptions and Nvidia's China demand thesis.
“our companies are battling each other with trillions of dollars in capex and the Chinese are doing something very differently on much less.” ▶ 13:38
Chinese customers are declining to buy Nvidia's advanced chips even after the president cleared Nvidia to sell into China, signaling China's deliberate pivot toward domestic, lower-cost AI development.
causal🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumNvidiaChina
So what: Voluntary refusal of Nvidia chips despite export approval is a bearish demand signal for Nvidia and a bullish one for Chinese self-sufficiency.
“the Chinese aren't buying them.” ▶ 13:32
A Bloomberg headline that China's Moonshot unveiled an AI model narrowing the gap with US firms hit US AI names on Friday, weighing on Meta, Google, Amazon and Microsoft down the stack.
causal🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highMoonshotChinaMetaGoogleAmazonMicrosoftBloomberg
So what: Chinese model releases are now direct catalysts for US mega-cap AI weakness; competitive-parity headlines are a recurring downside trigger to monitor.
“China's moonshot unveils AI model that narrows gap with US firms. So this is one of the reasons why we're seeing weakness today.” ▶ 13:51
Apple's contrarian breakout 4
Apple broke out to the upside — the opposite of the rest of tech — on Chinese approval to work with Baidu and Alibaba, with money rotating out of semis and memory into Apple as it is now rewarded, rather than punished, for not spending on AI.
thesis🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumAppleAAPLBaiduAlibaba
So what: Apple is trading as the defensive AI-capex haven; the narrative flip from 'punished for not spending' to 'rewarded for not spending' explains the rotation and may reverse if AI sentiment recovers.
“there was a period of time where Apple is being punished for not spending. I think now they're being rewarded for not spending.” ▶ 14:51
Apple trades at roughly 33.5-34x next year's earnings with expected earnings growth of only ~10% and sales growth of ~9%, a growth-company multiple on what is arguably now a value company, into an earnings release with its 200-day moving average near $275.
data_point🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highAppleAAPL
So what: A 34x multiple on high-single-digit growth is a valuation mismatch; the setup looks poor into earnings, making Apple a candidate to fade after its defensive run.
“when Apple was a growth company, it was trading probably in the low teens... now that it's probably some sort of value company, it's trading at a growth company multiple. So, something's a skew here.” ▶ 15:28
Apple's China iPhone sales reaccelerated more than 20% last quarter (China is ~20% of revenue), but memory-driven price increases plus a weak Chinese economy, a fall Siri-with-AI relaunch, and a lawsuit against OpenAI over departing hardware staff make Dan Nathan skeptical of the rally.
thesis🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumAppleAAPLChinaSiriOpenAI
So what: If Apple must raise prices into a weak China amid product/AI execution issues, the reacceleration may not last; the breakout could be a rotation artifact rather than a fundamental turn.
“This thing seems like a mess to me. I'm just going to be really like clear on the product front. And I just don't get the enthusiasm about it.” ▶ 16:35
Apple's gross margin was ~47% last fiscal year and is expected to be ~48%, with a mix shift toward services and AI services seen as the main positive driver.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumAppleAAPL
So what: Services-led margin expansion is the one clear bullish lever for Apple, but it is modest and may not justify a 34x multiple.
Nvidia and component-maker margins 4
Nvidia trading at only ~18x next year's earnings (below a market multiple, in the high teens) is a warning, not a bargain, because the market is signaling it does not believe Nvidia's ~74% gross margins will last.
contrarian🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highNvidiaNVDA
So what: A low multiple on peak margins is a value trap; the market is pricing margin erosion, so cheapness alone is not a buy signal for Nvidia.
“Nvidia the point that I've been making it's either a very good thing that they're trading at 18 times the next year's numbers or a very bad thing. I obviously think it's the latter” ▶ 18:23
Per Jim Chanos, Dell's gross margins have fallen from 22% to about 17.5% over the last year and a half and are likely to go lower if component pricing stays high, illustrating how AI hardware economics squeeze the server assemblers below the chipmaker.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: highDellDELLNvidiaMicronJim Chanos
So what: Component suppliers and assemblers absorb margin pain from high Nvidia/memory pricing; Chanos argues they shouldn't trade at a higher multiple than Nvidia itself.
“So Dell's gross margins have gone from 22% down to about 17 and a half% over the last year and a half. And they're probably going to go lower” ▶ 17:36
Dell topped out around 470 and now trades sub-400, at roughly 21.5x this year and 18x next — a premium to Nvidia — and has become a crowded name partly because the president has repeatedly mentioned it, a tailwind that could reverse.
data_point🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumDellDELLNvidia
So what: Dell paying a premium to Nvidia on shrinking margins is a relative-value short setup; political attention adds a two-sided catalyst risk.
Guy Adami's overarching warning is not to be seduced by AI hardware valuations that look cheap, because Nvidia's margins will inevitably start to decrease regardless of what Jensen Huang says, and cheap multiples can quickly look expensive.
framework🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highNvidiaNVDAJensen HuangDell
So what: On peak-margin, peak-cycle names a low multiple is a trap; investors should discount forward margins rather than trust management's durability claims.
“don't be seduced by valuations that seem um interesting because... they can seem very expensive, especially with a name like Nvidia where there's just an inevitability that margins are going to start to decrease” ▶ 19:14
Netflix cash-flow miss 2
Netflix fell 9% because free cash flow came in around $1.54 billion versus the ~$2.75 billion expected, signaling it is spending far more than the market wants on content and buying growth despite in-line EPS and sales.
causal🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highNetflixNFLX
So what: The free-cash-flow miss, not the P&L, is what broke the stock; content-spend intensity and lack of organic growth are the metrics that now matter for Netflix.
“free cash flow is expected to be $2.75 billion. It came in I believe at 1.54 something like that pretty significant” ▶ 21:23
Netflix has round-tripped back to its 2021 all-time high near $68 (after a prior run to ~$135), but with the earnings gap now below that level there is no technical support, making it a 'no touch' despite the flush.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumNetflixNFLX
So what: With support broken and a gap below, Netflix has no clear technical floor; a two-to-three-times-normal-volume flush day is the only reason to even look at it.
“if I draw a horizontal line from that 2021 high... the gap is actually below that... So, there's no there's really no support from technical standpoint.” ▶ 23:12
Oil premium and refiners 4
WTI crude at ~$81.25 (Brent ~$86.50) has bounced ~22% off a multi-month low near $67.50, but Guy Adami says the bounce is not commensurate with tight fundamentals — SPR and gasoline reserves are down to 'tag ends' and nobody is talking about it.
thesis🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumWTIBrentcrude oilSPR
So what: Depleted strategic and gasoline reserves are an underappreciated bullish supply backdrop; the equities are supported anywhere in this crude range even without a strong price view.
“we are down to tag ends in terms of the SPR, in terms of gasoline reserves, it's not good. Nobody's talking about it.” ▶ 25:39
Guy Adami is bullish refiners and energy equities — Valero made an all-time high Friday, alongside strength in PSX and Marathon Petroleum, and he expects Exxon's downtrend since March to break to the upside, with ConocoPhillips, Chevron and OIH also buys unless crude collapses.
thesis🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highValeroPSXMarathon PetroleumExxonConocoPhillipsChevronOIH
So what: The trade is long energy equities rather than the commodity; refiner crack-spread dynamics and a supportive crude range keep these names working absent a crude crash.
“unless you think crude is going to collapse which I don't think you got to stay low these equities Dan in my opinion.” ▶ 26:31
With the Middle East ceasefire over and bombing accelerating for six days, Guy Adami expects a geopolitical premium to build into oil, even though the VIX (at 18), gold and the bond market are not yet pricing that risk.
prediction🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: mediumcrude oilVIXgoldMiddle East
So what: A disconnect between rising geopolitical risk and complacent VIX/gold/bond markets is a potential asymmetric setup to hedge or position for a premium re-rating.
“even with the VIX up today at 18 does not take that into consideration nor does the gold market and by the way neither does the bond market.” ▶ 28:38
The hosts frame the Middle East conflict as a 'calendar game' tied to the November 4 midterm election, with adversaries trying to wait out the administration while the US tries to bomb them into submission, and warn winning may require boots on the ground into the fall.
framework🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: mediumMiddle EastIRGCmidterm election
So what: An election-timed conflict raises the odds of escalation into the fall; markets pricing no premium leaves oil and volatility exposed to headline risk.
“it's crazy to think that the IRGC and these folks are really they probably have a calendar up and they're like they looking at election day on November 4th” ▶ 27:18
Earnings previews: Intel, Capital One 3
Intel has fallen 35% from an all-time high of $142 on June 30 to $93 in under a month, with theoretical technical support near its prior $80 post-earnings gap and the 200-day moving average around $64, but Guy Adami says the valuation makes zero sense and doubts Thursday's report changes that.
thesis🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highIntelINTC
So what: Despite a violent 35% drop into technical support, the fundamental valuation concern remains, so bounces into $80/$64 are levels to fade rather than buy.
“the stock has gone from 142 down to 93 bucks. It's a pretty significant move in a very short period of time... the valuation makes zero sense.” ▶ 29:59
Capital One (Tuesday earnings) is the key read on the lower-K consumer, and Guy Adami expects rising delinquencies across credit cards, student loans, autos and mortgages to force commentary the market won't like, in contrast to the upper-K American Express consumer.
prediction🗣 Guy Adamiconviction: highCapital OneCOFAmerican ExpressAXP
So what: Capital One is a proxy for consumer credit stress; a K-shaped-economy read with worsening delinquencies is a macro warning even though its valuation and uptrend still look reasonable.
“if what I'm reading is true in terms of credit card delinquency rates, in terms of student loans, autos, even mortgage delinquencies, there's no way they're going to say anything that the market's going to like” ▶ 31:51
Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley gave back all of their gains after making all-time highs following bank earnings, a sign the post-earnings enthusiasm in financials faded quickly.
data_point🗣 Dan Nathanconviction: lowGoldman SachsMorgan Stanley
So what: Bank leaders reversing all-time-high breakouts suggests broad risk appetite is deteriorating even outside the AI complex.

0xResearch 1 episode(s) · 37 insights

CLARITY, Base Vs Robinhood & Bitcoin Bottoming?56 min37 insights2026-07-22
▶ Watch on YouTube
Blockworks Research analysts (host Luke with guests Carlos and Ryan) argue the Clarity Act is a ceiling-remover but not a driver of new crypto highs, that Robinhood chain's launchpad wars (Pawns winning) and the FOMO trading app represent the real growth, that Base is losing its identity to Coinbase while Robinhood out-executes on RWAs, and that Bitcoin is in a 'bottoming zone' as strategy's selling derisks a blowup while ETH and L1/L2 tokens are structurally disfavored versus the application layer.
Clarity Act market impact · 5Robinhood chain launchpad wars · 10FOMO app owns the user · 3Base losing identity to Coinbase · 4Robinhood chain monetization vs network revenue · 4Bitcoin bottoming zone · 7ETH and L1/L2 tokens disfavored · 4
Clarity Act market impact 5
President Trump agreed to an ethics provision in the Clarity Act, removing the final major hurdle to advancing the crypto market-structure bill toward a Senate vote before recess.
data_point🗣 Lukeconviction: highClarity ActDonald TrumpThe BlockUS Senate
So what: A near-term Senate vote catalyst exists, but the window is tight before recess so timing risk remains.
“Donald Trump has agreed to an ethics provision in the Clarity Act, removing the final major hurdle to advancing the crypto market structure bill towards a Senate vote” ▶ 2:25
Polymarket odds on the Clarity Act passing barely moved despite crypto rallying on the ethics-provision news, suggesting a possible (though small-size) spread trade between the prediction market and spot crypto.
contrarian🗣 Ryanconviction: mediumPolymarketClarity ActBitcoin
So what: Divergence between prediction-market odds and crypto price action flags a mispricing worth watching, but illiquidity caps the trade.
“I'd rather trust the signal that crypto is sending rather than the poly market” ▶ 3:44
Crypto has never rallied structurally on legislation, so a bull case for crypto prices resting on the Clarity Act is unconvincing and won't drive new all-time highs, though it may help put in a bottom.
thesis🗣 Ryanconviction: highClarity Act
So what: Fade positioning that treats Clarity Act passage as a path to new highs; treat it as bottom-support, not upside fuel.
“if crypto needs to be anointed by the high priest in Washington, like it's probably not that actually good of an investment” ▶ 4:30
The real Clarity Act beneficiaries are US-entrenched crypto equities at the TradFi/fintech intersection like Coinbase (up 11%) and Circle (up 6%), not the long-tail of DeFi.
thesis🗣 Carlosconviction: highCoinbaseCircleCOIN
So what: Express a Clarity Act view through regulated equities (COIN, Circle) rather than DeFi tokens.
“coin up 11%, circle up 6%. I think these are two names that clearly can benefit from clarity passing” ▶ 5:11
Securitize rallied 12% on the Clarity news since Clarity benefits an RWA issuer more than most projects, but its post-IPO cap table may carry share overhangs from extra SPAC shares hitting the market.
thesis🗣 Ryanconviction: mediumSecuritizeClarity ActRWA
So what: Securitize is a leveraged Clarity play among RWA issuers, but scrutinize potential unlock/overhang risk before buying.
“clarity does help an RWA issuer probably more than most projects” ▶ 8:03
Robinhood chain launchpad wars 10
Robinhood chain's first weeks show ETH-stablecoin swaps and memecoins making up ~97% of spot volume while tokenized assets it was designed for are a rounding error at ~0.6%.
data_point🗣 Lukeconviction: highRobinhood chainmemecoinstokenized assetsBlockworks Research
So what: Memecoins, not tokenized equities, are the actual onboarding engine for the chain in its early phase.
“it's E stablecoin and memes um doing 97% of the volume with with tokenized assets. Still lagging” ▶ 9:39
Robinhood chain is strategically significant because Robinhood is the biggest retail trading app in America bringing a massive non-crypto user base on-chain, unlike Coinbase's Base whose users are already in crypto.
thesis🗣 Ryanconviction: highRobinhood chainRobinhoodCoinbaseBase
So what: The distribution edge means winning launchpad/app positioning on Robinhood chain is a nine-figure-plus opportunity.
“they're the biggest retail trading app in America. That's obviously like the golden grail of customers right there for crypto” ▶ 11:51
Launchpad Noxa did over $7 million revenue in a week (~$1M/day) on Robinhood chain before internal team fighting caused it to pause all new deployments and effectively shut down.
data_point🗣 Ryanconviction: highNoxaRobinhood chain
So what: Demonstrates the scale of launchpad revenue up for grabs and how quickly leadership can shift on the chain.
“in a week they did like over 7 million revenue. So they were doing like making a million dollars a day” ▶ 10:42
Pawns (a fork of Noxa) is consolidating as the winning Robinhood chain launchpad with a fair-launch token, no VC overhang, and 80% of platform revenue used to buy back and burn the token.
thesis🗣 Ryanconviction: highPawnsNoxaRobinhood chainHyperliquid
So what: Pawns is Ryan's high-conviction pick for the launchpad winner and a community-aligned buyback/burn token to watch.
“they're using 80% of platform revenues to buy back and burn the token” ▶ 13:27
Conventional wisdom that a launchpad token caps the tokens on its platform is wrong; a community-aligned token is itself a marketing mechanism that cultivates a loyal user base, which is why Pawns is winning where pump.fun spurned its community.
framework🗣 Ryanconviction: highPawnspump.funPUMP
So what: Reframes launchpad token design: alignment and community loyalty beat the old 'no token / avoid ceiling' logic.
“the token in of itself is a is a marketing mechanism” ▶ 13:06
Because rotational traders chase 'beta' coins down the ladder rather than top-blasting runners, buying coins launched on Pawns generates revenue for Pawns and strengthens its fundamentals, unlike fragmented liquidity around a single token.
framework🗣 Ryanconviction: mediumPawns
So what: The reflexive flywheel between trader rotation and platform revenue is a durable moat for the launchpad.
“if you're now buying a coin launch on ponds, like you're generating revenue for ponds and making its fundamentals stronger” ▶ 14:38
Robinhood founder Vlad following the Pawns dev was read by traders as a signal that Pawns is the winner, though Bonk's newly launched launchpad could temporarily grab share like Bonk Fun did from Pump Fun last summer.
anecdote🗣 Ryanconviction: mediumPawnsVlad TenevBonkBonk FunPump Fun
So what: Watch for a Bonk-style temporary share grab, but Ryan expects Pawns' community-first position to hold.
“if I was a betting man, and I guess I am, I would say Pawns is probably the winner” ▶ 14:55
Robinhood chain memecoin activity has so far been additive rather than cannibalistic to Solana/pump.fun, with pump.fun revenue, volume, and token launches roughly flat rather than down through its unlock cliffs.
data_point🗣 Carlosconviction: mediumRobinhood chainSolanapump.funPUMP
So what: For now Robinhood chain growth is not a short-Solana thesis; both ecosystems can coexist in the current environment.
“for now the Robin Hood chain activity has been additive rather than taking away from Pomp and Sena trenches” ▶ 16:31
Solana traders are described as 'cockroaches' who survive anything and show ecosystem loyalty, making the scenario where Robinhood chain actually steals Solana market share a lower-probability but non-zero risk.
thesis🗣 Ryanconviction: mediumSolanaRobinhood chain

“Salana traders seem to be cockroaches and will survive literally anything and they come back and keep trading” ▶ 18:05
Memecoins are the greatest onboarding tool for retail onto a new chain, as seen historically with Solana, Ethereum, and Base's AI-agent memecoin season, and now driving Robinhood chain's early volume.
framework🗣 Carlosconviction: highmemecoinsSolanaEthereumBaseRobinhood chain

“they're definitely like the greatest on boarding tool for retail uh when it comes to having a new chain” ▶ 19:18
FOMO app owns the user 3
The FOMO trading app is generating almost as much revenue as the Robinhood chain sequencer itself, with daily relay revenue peaking after it began supporting Robinhood chain on July 10th.
data_point🗣 Carlosconviction: highFOMORobinhood chain
So what: The application/relay layer is capturing value comparable to the chain infrastructure itself - a key value-accrual signal.
“FOMO is doing almost as much revenue as Robin Hood chain itself like the sequencer just from supporting that chain” ▶ 23:27
FOMO abstracts away the underlying chain so users become loyal to the app rather than any chain, meaning whichever chain FOMO adds gets an immediate volume boost - 'who owns the user, it's starting to become FOMO.'
thesis🗣 Ryanconviction: highFOMORobinhood chain
So what: Chain-agnostic app distribution is becoming the control point in crypto; owning the user beats owning the chain.
“There's a significant portion of crypto users now that are not loyal to any chain. They're just loyal to FOMO” ▶ 25:26
FOMO is characterized as one of the best apps in crypto and the first to successfully merge social and trading where friend.tech and Zora failed, with over $1M weekly revenue and ~$100K/day from its relay, likened to Phantom last cycle.
thesis🗣 Ryanconviction: highFOMOPhantomfriend.techZora
So what: The social+trading thesis is finally executing; a FOMO token launch would be a sought-after exposure if it ever happens.
“I'll go a step further and say this is like one of the best apps in crypto” ▶ 23:55
Base losing identity to Coinbase 4
Base is losing its independent identity and increasingly becoming backend infrastructure for Coinbase, which is good for Coinbase but bad for Base builders who saw it as a separate decentralized ecosystem.
thesis🗣 Ryanconviction: highBaseCoinbaseCOIN
So what: Reassess theses that value Base as an independent ecosystem; its value is consolidating into Coinbase's stack.
“base is going to lose its own identity. It's going to lose its community and it's going to just increasely seen as like backend info for Coinbase” ▶ 40:21
Base has spurned the degenerate traders who generated the majority of its revenue, and its pivot to tokenized equities puts it in direct competition with Robinhood chain where Coinbase is unlikely to out-execute Robinhood on RWAs.
contrarian🗣 Ryanconviction: highBaseCoinbaseRobinhood chainRWA
So what: Bearish signal for Base positioning; Robinhood's equity-market pedigree makes it the favorite in the RWA/tokenized-equity race.
“majority of BAS's revenue came from degenerate traders... Bass constantly has spurned them at every turn” ▶ 36:21
Base is structurally misaligned with Coinbase because Base wants to build its own decentralized ecosystem while Coinbase wants to absorb what works into its own walled garden, evidenced by the redundant separate Coinbase app and Base app.
framework🗣 Ryanconviction: highBaseCoinbase
So what: The internal misalignment is a strategic weakness; contrast with Robinhood chain's tighter alignment to Robinhood's goals.
“Bass almost does not want the same things Coinbase wants” ▶ 37:50
Coinbase founder Brian Armstrong (Jesse) capitulating and admitting the Base creator-coin/Zora thesis was wrong, while Kobe taking charge of all Coinbase trading products is viewed as bullish for product innovation like a potential HIP3 perps integration.
anecdote🗣 Carlosconviction: mediumCoinbaseBaseZoraKobeHIP3
So what: Leadership change on trading products is a catalyst to watch for Coinbase moving beyond its 'laggard in innovation' reputation.
“it's bullish for Coinbase that Kobe is now in charge of all the trading products” ▶ 34:38
Robinhood chain monetization vs network revenue 4
Robinhood chain network (sequencer) revenue will trend downward over time, but Robinhood can monetize far more through ecosystem partnerships since it owns the users and distribution that apps normally must be bribed with grants to attract.
thesis🗣 Ryanconviction: highRobinhood chainRobinhoodHood
So what: Model Robinhood chain value via app-layer partnership fees, not sequencer revenue - the standard chain-launch playbook is inverted here.
“Robin and Chain has all the users and they have the distribution” ▶ 30:31
Robinhood takes a 50% fee split with Lighter on all fees from Lighter's Robinhood-chain instance, and Morpho is rumored to have paid Robinhood for main-app distribution of its vaults - deals worth more than network revenue.
data_point🗣 Ryanconviction: mediumRobinhood chainLighterMorphoRobinhood
So what: These partnership economics, if replicated, could make Robinhood chain a nine-figure revenue line and an upside surprise for HOOD equity.
“Robin Hood has a 50% fee split with lighter on all fees generated by the the Robin Hood instance” ▶ 30:54
Equity sellside analysts likely mismodel Robinhood chain by comparing it to Base sequencer revenue (~$20-50M business line), missing that if fees accrue to the application layer via ecosystem monetization it could become a nine-figure revenue line.
contrarian🗣 Ryanconviction: mediumRobinhood chainRobinhoodHoodBase
So what: A potential mispricing in HOOD equity if the market anchors to a Base-sequencer comp instead of app-layer monetization.
≈ “if you have the view that Robin Hood can like monetize through the ecosystem and the application layer is now where the majority of fees acrue... that's where you probably get an upside surprise” paraphrase
JP Morgan sellside research on Coinbase estimated that if Base launched a token it could be a $12-30 billion opportunity given the multiples L2 tokens trade at relative to revenue, and a similar dynamic is theoretically in play for Robinhood chain.
data_point🗣 Lukeconviction: mediumJP MorganCoinbaseBaseRobinhood chain
So what: Frames the optionality value of a potential Base or Robinhood chain token, though neither has signaled a token launch.
“JP Morgan's writing sellside equity research on Coinbase saying if Bass launches a token that could be a 12 to30 billion opportunity” ▶ 29:20
Bitcoin bottoming zone 7
Strategy sold just over $200 million in Bitcoin Monday morning and Bitcoin rallied in the face of it, reframing what was seen as bad news as good news as strategy 'walks itself out of the hole' of stretch-financed purchases.
data_point🗣 Lukeconviction: highStrategyMicroStrategyBitcoinSTRK
So what: Strategy shifting from forced-hole-digging buyer to seller is being absorbed constructively - a sentiment inflection.
“what sounds like bad news is now good news” ▶ 42:18
Bitcoin traded two weeks below its 200-week moving average - historically a reliable bottoming indicator - and has now reclaimed it around $66,000.
data_point🗣 Carlosconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: The 200-week MA reclaim at ~$66K is a technical bottoming signal to anchor risk levels around.
“it traded for two weeks below its 200 week moving average which has historically been a good indicator of like Bitcoin bottoming and now it is above it like 66,000” ▶ 43:23
Four consecutive days of Bitcoin ETF inflows are a more constructive signal than strategy's activity because they reflect genuine institutional demand rather than a single player like Saylor.
thesis🗣 Carlosconviction: highBitcoinETFStrategyMichael Saylor
So what: Track ETF flows as the higher-quality demand signal; a few days of inflows can more than offset strategy's outflows.
“we've seen four consecutive days of ETF flows” ▶ 43:56
With Bitcoin realized price at $53K representing only modest downside and most of the bear market behind in price and time, this is a 'bottoming zone' rather than a confirmed bottom.
framework🗣 Lukeconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: Realized price at $53K is the reference downside level; positioning for a bottoming range rather than a V-reversal.
“I'm not necessarily calling bottom, but I I would be calling a bottoming zone here” ▶ 45:31
Strategy selling Bitcoin for cash derisks a spectacular blowup (a tail probability estimated at ~0.1-0.5%), making this bottom likely characterized by apathy rather than the peak chaos and crime of the 2022-2023 cycle.
thesis🗣 Ryanconviction: mediumStrategyBitcoinMichael Saylor
So what: The removal of blowup tail risk supports price action; expect an apathy-driven bottom, not a capitulation-crime event.
“as strategy is selling off Bitcoin for cash, like the chance of a spectacular blow up is decreasing” ▶ 46:15
Strategy's dividend coverage now extends roughly two years, and if Bitcoin is at the same price in two years there would be severe issues with the entire Bitcoin thesis, not just Saylor.
framework🗣 Ryanconviction: mediumStrategyBitcoinMichael Saylor
So what: Two-year runway means strategy has time to manage its balance sheet; the real bear case would be BTC going nowhere for two years.
“if Bitcoin is still the same price as it is now in two years, there's probably like severe issues with the Bitcoin thesis” ▶ 47:16
The ETF product is becoming a more important and capitalized vehicle for Bitcoin inflows and outflows than strategy, so a few days of positive ETF flows are enough to take the other side of strategy's selling.
thesis🗣 Lukeconviction: highBitcoinETFStrategy
So what: The marginal Bitcoin price-setter is shifting from strategy to ETFs - a structurally healthier demand base.
“a few days of positive flows is more than enough to take the other side of strategies outflows” ▶ 48:36
ETH and L1/L2 tokens disfavored 4
ETH just follows Bitcoin with no independent rally case, and value accrual from Robinhood chain to Ethereum is extremely small since ETH is used as money only for memecoin pairs while tokenized stocks will trade against dollars, not ETH.
thesis🗣 Ryanconviction: highEthereumETHBitcoinRobinhood chain
So what: If bullish Robinhood chain, buy HOOD or Uniswap not ETH; if bullish crypto broadly, buy Bitcoin - ETH offers no differentiated exposure.
“if you're bullish on Robin Hood chain I don't think you buy ETH um you buy Hood or you buy unis swap” ▶ 50:28
The ETH bull case has collapsed into a circular thesis of 'how much will Bitmine buy,' mirroring how Bitcoin's case degraded into 'Saylor will buy all the Bitcoin,' which doesn't work as a durable investment thesis.
contrarian🗣 Carlosconviction: highEthereumETHBitmineBitcoinMichael Saylor
So what: Discount single-buyer treasury narratives; a thesis dependent on one entity's purchases is fragile and reflexive.
“the bull case right now is just how much is Bitmine going to buy which at the end of the day is just a circular thesis” ▶ 51:34
ETH sits at the low of a wide, volatile six-year range, and while a bearish thesis exists on price-to-rev and competition, animal spirits plus the Tom Lee gigabull thesis of institutions staking ETH for tokenized-asset issuance could send it back toward the high of the range.
thesis🗣 Lukeconviction: mediumEthereumETHTom Lee
So what: At range lows, ETH is a skewed risk/reward for TA traders betting on an animal-spirits reversal, even if fundamentals lag.
“I also still wouldn't fade the impact that animal spirits can have on expectations” ▶ 53:02
L1 and L2 tokens (ETH, SOL, L2s) are structurally disfavored because the application layer is winning revenue generation; the only crypto asset with a durable monetary bid is Bitcoin, with Zcash as a possible secondary interest.
thesis🗣 Ryanconviction: highBitcoinZcashEthereumSolanaL2 tokens

“The only like crypto asset that I think that has like a durable sustaining monetary bid is Bitcoin. Maybe you can make the case for Zcash” ▶ 54:15

The Meb Faber Show 1 episode(s) · 37 insights

Meb Faber’s Mid-Year Market Review50 min37 insights2026-07-20
▶ Watch on YouTube
In a mid-year webinar, Meb Faber argues US stocks are historically expensive (CAPE ~42, dividend yield ~1%) and destined for low-single-digit returns over 5-7 years, while foreign, emerging, small-cap value, real assets and T-bills offer far better risk/reward. He details Cambria's shareholder-yield, endowment-replication, trend and hedging funds and flags two non-consensus positions: allocating up to 50% to trend, and holding only T-bills in fixed income until spreads compensate for risk.
US valuation gravity · 5Rotation into foreign, EM and value · 7Shareholder yield fund construction · 4T-bills over credit · 3Trend following as core diversifier · 4Endowment replication and hedged structures · 6Taxable-account and structural innovation · 4Private markets and IPO supply · 1Investor psychology and hubris · 3
US valuation gravity 5
US equities are trading at a Shiller CAPE ratio of roughly 42 with a dividend yield of about 1%, which Meb Faber flags as unambiguously expensive across every valuation metric (CAPE, forward PE, trailing PE, price/cash flow, dividend yield).
data_point🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highS&P 500CAPE ratioUS equities
So what: A starting valuation this high is a structural headwind for US index returns; investors should temper S&P 500 return expectations and look for cheaper exposures.
“We're at 42 and change. You know US dividend yield is 1%. So there's no question US stocks are expensive.” ▶ 8:10
Faber says no market that has ended a year at a CAPE ratio of 40 has ever produced above-average returns over the following decade, giving expensive markets a historical batting average of zero for beating the average.
data_point🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highCAPE ratioUS equities
So what: Frames elevated CAPE as gravity/weight on the next 10 years of returns rather than a crash signal, arguing for lower US-equity allocation now.
“Not once have we found in history that the a market that ends the year at a cape ratio of 40 has above average returns the next decade. So the batting average historically has been zero” ▶ 8:46
Faber warns there is no ceiling on valuation, citing that following Japan's historical path would mean a double from current US levels and that South Korea's market roughly tripled in the past year.
framework🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumJapanSouth KoreaUS equities
So what: Cautions against shorting or fully exiting expensive markets on valuation alone, since overvaluation can extend dramatically before mean-reverting.
“there's no ceiling on valuation. And so, you know, we we look at the example of Japan and say, "Hey, you know, this could if if we followed the track of Japan, this that's a double from here."” ▶ 8:19
Faber argues sentiment is not yet euphoric despite high valuations, pointing to University of Michigan consumer sentiment near all-time lows and AAII and Investors Intelligence readings that are not euphoric, meaning the final euphoric blow-off top has not occurred.
contrarian🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumUniversity of Michigan consumer sentimentAAIIInvestors Intelligence
So what: Suggests the bull market could still have a euphoric top left, so bearish valuation calls need not be imminent; watch sentiment for the blow-off signal.
“the sentiment is universally not euphoric. Michigan sentiment is like all-time lows. AI not that euphoric. Investors intelligence not that euphoric.” ▶ 24:41
Faber expects the US stock market to perform poorly over the next five to seven years, pegging returns at low single digits and firmly below 15%.
prediction🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highS&P 500US equities
So what: A concrete low-return forecast that argues for diversifying away from US-cap-weighted equities into cheaper asset classes.
“How do I think the stock market will do in the next five to seven years? Poorly. Um lower than 15% for sure. Expectations, you know, I would peg it low single digits.” ▶ 45:02
Rotation into foreign, EM and value 7
Faber contends the value rotation into foreign and emerging markets is not over, since many foreign markets still trade at low-teens CAPE valuations versus roughly 42 for the US, even after Taiwan and South Korea have surged.
thesis🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highemerging marketsforeign developed marketsTaiwanSouth KoreaCAPE ratio
So what: Argues investors who feel they missed the run still have a large valuation gap to exploit by adding foreign and EM exposure.
“many of these foreign markets are still teens, low teens valuations relative to the US. I'm talking cape ratio at 42” ▶ 32:41
Faber recommends a default global equity allocation of 60% US, 30% foreign developed and 10% emerging markets as a starting point, versus the roughly two-thirds US Bogle index weighting.
framework🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highJack BogleUS equitiesforeign developed marketsemerging markets
So what: A concrete allocation blueprint; simply moving to index weights would materially cut most investors' US overexposure.
“60% US, 30% foreign developed, 10% emerging. That's the starting point.” ▶ 14:44
Faber identifies the two most common flaws in traditional portfolios as being far too concentrated in highly-valued US large-cap stocks and holding very little foreign, emerging-market or real-asset exposure (commodities, commodity equities, TIPS and REITs).
framework🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highUS large capsemerging marketscommoditiesTIPSREITs
So what: A checklist for repositioning: break the US market-cap tilt and add real assets to improve expected 1-5 year outcomes.
“the two biggest, you know, kind of, uh, problems when we see traditional portfolios, one is they're way too US focused... And then the second is very few people own real assets.” ▶ 13:16
Faber notes US small-cap value is having a monster year in 2026, an outperformance almost no one anticipated, signaling that value is rotating within the US market and not only abroad.
data_point🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumUS small-cap value
So what: Points to a broadening rotation away from mega-cap growth; small-cap value may continue to lead as the cap-weight tilt unwinds.
“Small cap having a monster year uh uh which no one said that in forever, right?” ▶ 13:00
Faber predicts explosive flows into long-neglected asset classes like emerging and foreign markets as advisors shift from having sold them to fearing they will miss another 20-30% gain, reframing the theme as moving from a bear market in diversification to a bull market in diversification.
prediction🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumemerging marketsforeign developed markets
So what: Anticipates a FOMO-driven flow surge into cheap non-US assets, especially once the S&P stalls; early positioning captures the re-rating.
“then you might see some pretty explosive flows into these asset classs that have been long uh you know long forgotten about put in the dumpster bin of allocations.” ▶ 12:06
Faber argues equal-weighted indexing will beat cap-weighted indexing over the next decade because the Achilles heel of cap weighting is valuation when markets get extreme, as they are now.
prediction🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highequal-weight indexcap-weight indexS&P 500
So what: Favors equal-weight over cap-weight S&P exposure to avoid concentration in the most overvalued mega-caps.
“Equal weighted over cap weighted. cap weighted the the the Achilles heel of cap weighting is valuation when things go crazy like right now uh I think evil weight is going to stomp cap weight for the next decade” ▶ 46:21
Faber invokes Jack Bogle's 1999-2000 shift from 75% to 25% stocks as a template for today, noting the same conditions now hold: expensive stocks, bonds yielding 4%, and attractively valued foreign, emerging and small-value equities.
thesis🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumJack BogleVanguardbondsforeign marketsemerging markets
So what: Uses the 'world's greatest market timer' framing to justify trimming US equity risk when competing assets finally offer real yield and value.
“Jack's out here saying, "Hey, I moved my allocation from 75% stocks to 25% stocks." And to me, we call him the world's greatest market timer.” ▶ 15:14
Shareholder yield fund construction 4
Cambria's shareholder-yield ETFs SYLD (US), FYLD (foreign) and EYLD (emerging) now have 10-year track records, share the same methodology and similar valuation and cash-flow characteristics, yet produce very different sector exposures.
data_point🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highSYLDFYLDEYLDCambria
So what: Shows that identical value screens abroad yield different sector bets, so owning all three diversifies factor exposure rather than duplicating it.
“if you compare all these funds, they have similar characteristics, but totally different sector exposures.” ▶ 11:09
Faber notes the foreign-developed shareholder-yield fund is maximally long energy at roughly a 30% position while the emerging-market fund carries a huge tech weighting and the US fund has almost no tech, upending the assumption that value investing avoids technology.
data_point🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highFYLDEYLDSYLDenergy sectortechnology sector
So what: Investors can get tech and energy exposure through value screens abroad; sector outcomes depend on geography, not the value label.
“the foreign developed max long energy. I mean this is like a 30% position the uh emerging fund huge tech position whereas in the US it has almost no tech.” ▶ 11:16
Faber says Cambria avoids value traps by running a final relative-strength/momentum sort on the shortlist of shareholder-yield candidates rather than on both entry and exit, a process that historically added about 1% per year in simulated returns.
framework🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumCambriamomentumrelative strengthshareholder yield
So what: A concrete, low-turnover way to screen out cheap-and-falling stocks; the ~1% annual edge is a repeatable factor overlay.
“using relative strength adds about a percent a year in you know hypothetical returns” ▶ 18:07
Faber reveals the biggest winners inside Cambria's FYLD, EYLD and GVAL value funds have been AI and semiconductor-adjacent companies that doubled, tripled or quadrupled, forcing the funds to continually trim so no single stock exceeds 10-20% of the portfolio.
anecdote🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumFYLDEYLDGVALAIsemiconductors
So what: Even value funds captured the AI/semi run via cheap foreign names; the forced trimming is a built-in profit-taking discipline.
“the big investments we've had in those funds that have screamed higher have been AI semiconductor adjacent companies.” ▶ 34:15
T-bills over credit 3
Faber's answer for fixed-income yield is T-bills, arguing that investing further out the curve only pays when yields offer a margin of safety above the risk-free rate, and that Cambria's TYLD fund remains 100% in T-bills because no bond sleeve currently offers a wide enough spread.
contrarian🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highT-billsTYLDCambriayield curve
So what: Argues against reaching for duration or credit now; hold T-bills until spreads compensate for risk.
“where should I look for yield on the fixed income side and my answer to that is T- billills.” ▶ 25:59
Faber warns corporate and high-yield credit spreads are in the top decile or top 1% of tightness historically, meaning small increases in volatility could blow spreads out by percentage points, so portfolios stuffed with agg, corporates or high yield are poorly compensated.
causal🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highcorporate bondshigh yieldBloomberg Aggcredit spreads
So what: Flags credit as an asymmetric risk; underweight IG/HY and hold T-bills to avoid spread widening losses.
“corporates in high yield are like top decile or top 1% of spread historically and if we know anything about that... those spreads blow out percentage points” ▶ 27:28
Faber attributes today's tight credit spreads to price-insensitive institutional buyers who, after years of near-zero yields, are eager to buy corporate bonds simply because they now yield around 5%, regardless of the margin of safety.
causal🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumcorporate bondsinstitutional investors
So what: Explains why spreads stay tight and why the setup is fragile; a supply of yield-chasing buyers can reverse quickly.
“I think it's a lot of price insensitive buyers.” ▶ 28:21
Trend following as core diversifier 4
Faber holds the non-consensus view that up to 50% of a portfolio can be allocated to trend following, versus the 10-20% that even trend believers typically use, valuing it as a diversifier that performs when other assets fail.
contrarian🗣 Meb Faberconviction: hightrend followingmanaged futures
So what: A far larger trend allocation than industry norm; frames managed futures as a strategic, not fringe, portfolio pillar.
“One is how much of a role trend plays in the portfolio. Longtime followers will know that my comment is 50%. You could argue up to 50. No one on the planet does that.” ▶ 25:16
Faber highlights that managed futures is one of the few strategies that can short bonds and is currently short bonds, having been a key diversifier in 2022 when both stocks and bonds were wrecked.
framework🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highmanaged futuresbondsTrinity
So what: Managed futures offers negative correlation to bonds in a rising-rate/inflation regime; its current short-bond stance is a hedge worth watching.
“It's one of the few things that can be short bonds. So when everything got wrecked in 2022, uh it was one of the few uh diversifiers that was short bonds. It's currently short bonds today.” ▶ 25:44
Faber describes Cambria's Trinity (TRTY) strategy as half a globally diversified buy-and-hold of stocks, bonds and real assets (none market-cap weighted) and half momentum and trend, and says it holds most of his own public money.
framework🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highTrinityTRTYCambriamomentumtrend
So what: A skin-in-the-game blueprint blending non-cap-weighted global assets with trend for both statistical and psychological diversification.
“the trinity concept is a half of the allocation is a global buy and hold of the world asset classes... And then the other half of Trinity is momentum and trend.” ▶ 19:07
Faber argues cap-weighted market indexing like the S&P 500 is itself the world's ultimate trend-following algorithm, since holdings are sized by price times shares outstanding, so investors own more as stocks rise and less as they fall.
framework🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highS&P 500cap-weight indextrend following
So what: Reframes passive indexing as an unmanaged trend strategy, undercutting objections to explicit trend following on narrative grounds.
≈ “S&P and market cap indexing is the world's ultimate trend following index... you own mo more. The... algorithm sizing is you own more as it goes up and you own less as it goes down.” paraphrase
Endowment replication and hedged structures 6
Cambria's Endowment ETF (ENDW) replicates Yale/Harvard-style endowment allocations using a global factor-based portfolio with about 40% leverage (140% gross exposure) at an all-in cost near 25 basis points, and now has a one-year-plus track record Faber intends to publicly benchmark against Calpers, Harvard and Bridgewater.
data_point🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highENDWCambriaYale endowmentCalpersHarvardBridgewater
So what: Offers a sub-30bp liquid, tax-efficient alternative to expensive endowment/private-equity complexity; the public scoreboard is a catalyst to watch.
“we think you can replicate the majority of what these great endowments do with a global factor-based allocation but also with a little leverage. So ENDW can is like 40% leverage.” ▶ 20:50
Faber argues the low-cost endowment-replication approach gets little coverage because an entire ecosystem of consultants, private-equity advisors and newsletters profits from institutional complexity and is not incentivized to acknowledge a cheap ETF can match it.
causal🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumENDWendowmentsprivate equityconsultants
So what: Structural conflict of interest keeps fees high in institutional allocation; a disruptor product could gain share as results are published.
“there's an entire ecosystem... hundreds of thousands of positions, private equity consultants, family office, email newsletters, endowment magazines. There's a whole world ecosystem that profits on this complexity.” ▶ 21:46
Faber describes Cambria's VAMO ETF as a fund that buys US value-and-trend stocks and dynamically shorts broad S&P futures from 0 to 100% (half by valuation, half by trend), currently about half hedged given expensive-but-rising markets, and removes hedges after a market decline.
framework🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highVAMOCambriaS&P 500 futures
So what: A tool for reluctant bulls to stay invested with a variable hedge that lifts after crashes rather than staying market-neutral into a recovery.
≈ “Vammo, which is buys a portfolio of of US stocks with good value and trend and then it shorts broad-based S&P futures from anywhere from 0 to 100%... So, that fund is half hedged.” paraphrase
Faber describes Cambria's TAIL fund as an insurance-like vehicle buying puts on the US stock market coupled with 10-year Treasuries, which is expected to lose money over time but can protect a portfolio during a tail event.
framework🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumTAILCambria10-year Treasuriesput options
So what: A defined-cost hedge that investors typically add tactically as markets start to roll over; useful for tail-risk protection.
“Tail is a inverse style fund. It's buying puts on the US stock market coupled with 10-year bonds... it's probably going to lose money like an insurance vehicle, but some years... it it can save your hide” ▶ 44:25
Faber says Cambria's global REIT fund is up about 12% year-to-date, a surprise strong year for a value-tilted global real-estate strategy that he had not expected.
data_point🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumglobal REITsCambriareal assets
So what: Global value-tilted REITs are quietly outperforming; real-estate exposure is delivering within the under-owned real-assets bucket.
“would he expect REITs global REITs to be up 12%. I would have said no. Uh but here we are. REITs are having a really phenomenal year” ▶ 14:10
Faber says gold plays both a strategic and tactical role in a portfolio and that it is hard to argue against gold improving risk-adjusted investor returns over time.
thesis🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumgoldreal assets
So what: Supports a persistent gold allocation as a risk-adjusted return enhancer alongside other real assets.
“gold. I think yeah, it it definitely plays a role strategic as well as tactical in a portfolio. Uh it's hard to argue against that uh gold improves the risk investor returns over time.” ▶ 47:14
Taxable-account and structural innovation 4
Faber predicts a revolution in taxable-account asset location, with investors splitting between growth-oriented holders who want to avoid dividends and their taxes and income-oriented holders who want them; Cambria's TAX ETF targets low-to-no-yield US stocks to serve the former.
prediction🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumTAXCambriataxable accountsdividends
So what: Signals a growing product category around tax-aware equity exposure; low-dividend ETFs become more valuable if the S&P dividend yield rises from all-time lows.
“I think you're going to see a revolution in taxable accounts on asset location... We launched TAX, which is an ETF in the US that avoids high dividend companies” ▶ 39:52
Faber says Cambria has completed five 351 exchange conversions totaling nearly a billion dollars in assets, with another (a global stock fund) coming in the fall, and offers custom 351s for advisors or institutions with large concentrated-stock positions.
data_point🗣 Meb Faberconviction: high351 exchangeCambriaconcentrated stock
So what: 351 conversions are a growing tax-deferral tool for concentrated-stock holders; a live channel for advisors seeking custom ETF wrappers.
“351s which we haven't even talked about today. We've now done five of these almost a billion in assets.” ▶ 38:34
Faber calls a low-cost farmland interval fund a billion-dollar idea, noting farmland has historically been hard to access with only two or three REITs and platforms like Farmland Partners and AcreTrader offering fractionalized ownership; he recently sold his own farmland because it became too large a position.
thesis🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumfarmlandFarmland PartnersAcreTraderinterval fundREITs
So what: Highlights an underserved real-asset niche and a potential product/investment opportunity in accessible farmland vehicles.
“if I was a younger not doing what I was doing now, I would certainly start an interval fund based on farmland. I think that's a billion dollar idea.” ▶ 38:09
Faber recommends holding Bitcoin only as its market-portfolio weight, currently around half a percent, and notes investors already gain crypto exposure through broad equity indices as crypto-adjacent companies rise within them.
thesis🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumBitcoincryptoS&P 500
So what: A market-cap-weighted, low-conviction crypto stance; investors get incidental crypto exposure via equity indices without overweighting.
“own it as a percentage of the market portfolio. Uh, which today what half percent or something.” ▶ 33:51
Private markets and IPO supply 1
Faber warns investors underestimate how large the biggest private US companies have become, arguing the 'big three' if they come to market would be larger than the entire energy sector, which could trigger a significant IPO cycle and equity supply.
prediction🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumprivate companiesIPO marketenergy sector
So what: A wave of mega-IPOs would add substantial equity supply and could weigh on valuations; watch for the IPO cycle as a supply signal.
“the the big three if they come to market will be larger than the entire energy sector, which is astonishing to me” ▶ 32:17
Investor psychology and hubris 3
Faber warns that public displays of hubris tend to precede sharp reversals, citing Kathy Wood mocking value and buy-and-hold investors near the peak of ARK and pointing to Michael Saylor's recent Bitcoin-yield and AI video claims as a similar red flag.
contrarian🗣 Meb Faberconviction: mediumCathie WoodARK InvestMichael SaylorBitcoin
So what: Treats promoter hubris as a sentiment top-signal; extreme bragging around ARK-style and Bitcoin narratives is a caution flag.
