“You guys remember when Kathy Wood was making fun of value investors and buy and hold investors right before like it was like the peak of Arc... the market gods do not reward that.” ▶ 35:38
Faber argues the belief that US stocks are expensive is now a consensus view, with nearly every advisor holding heavily-valued S&P market-cap positions while confident they can exit before the market rolls over, echoing the 'wonderful ball' metaphor where none of the clocks have hands.
contrarian🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highS&P 500financial advisors
So what: Warns that the crowded 'I'll get out in time' trade rarely works; the crowded exit is itself the risk when the market turns.
“almost every single one thinks that they can make a move before it rolls over... none of the clocks have hands.” ▶ 23:54
Faber frames buying the dip in US equities as a 250-year phenomenon, using the long history of American equity ownership to argue that even severe drawdowns like the Great Depression and Global Financial Crisis barely register on a multi-century chart and are followed by strong long-run returns.
framework🗣 Meb Faberconviction: highUS equitiesGreat DepressionGlobal Financial Crisis
So what: A long-horizon behavioral anchor for staying invested and buying downturns, even while near-term US return expectations are low.
“buy the dip has been going on for 250 years.” ▶ 3:38

Adam Taggart | Thoughtful Money 1 episode(s) · 36 insights

SPECIAL REPORT: Re-Escalation Of War With Iran Spinning Out Of Control? | Ryan Bohl85 min36 insights2026-07-21
▶ Watch on YouTube
RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst Ryan Bohl argues the renewed US-Iran war has narrowed to a military-to-military battle over control of the Strait of Hormuz: freedom of navigation versus an Iranian tolling regime. He sees Iran holding the near-term upper hand as US munitions run low, expects elevated-and-sticky oil prices with fat tail risk from supply destruction, and frames a prolonged conflict as a long-run accelerant of the world's move off hydrocarbons and Gulf oil.
Hormuz control as the new war objective · 4US munitions and naval shortfall · 5GCC alignment and leverage · 3Oil price outlook and supply-destruction tail · 6Prolonged war as an anti-hydrocarbon accelerant · 3Iran's near-term edge vs long-term trap · 6Iran's fractured decision-making · 3Israel, Trump and the international flotilla question · 4Second- and third-order commodity effects · 2
Hormuz control as the new war objective 4
The war re-escalated specifically over control of the Strait of Hormuz: Iran resumed attacking civilian ships that sailed outside its 'pre-approved' lines, forcing a contest between US-backed freedom of navigation and an Iranian tolling/pre-approved-route regime.
thesis🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: highIranStrait of HormuzUnited Statesinternational shipping
So what: The trade is no longer a regime-change tail risk but a persistent shipping-toll/insurance premium on everything transiting Hormuz; watch tanker-attack cadence as the escalation signal.
“We've entered into a battle for uh influence over Hormuz. Whose rules does international shipping have to live by? Is it freedom of navigation as the US is pushing for? Or is it Iran's tolling system” ▶ 0:00
The June MOU that paused fighting was a non-binding memorandum whose contentious Article 5 deliberately left the Hormuz question unresolved, so its collapse was structural rather than accidental.
causal🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: highIranUnited StatesStrait of HormuzMOU
So what: Any future US-Iran 'deal' headline should be discounted as a shooting-pause, not a resolution, keeping a standing risk premium on Gulf energy.
An Iranian toll on Hormuz shipping of roughly $1-2 million per vessel would be economically absorbable and simply passed to consumers, meaning the base-case outcome is permanently higher energy costs rather than a supply cutoff.
data_point🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumIranStrait of HormuzChinaenergy prices
So what: Frames the likely steady-state as a structural cost-push on oil/LNG, not a spike-and-revert, favoring a persistent energy-inflation lean.
Trump's remark about imposing a 20% US toll on Hormuz undermines the global freedom-of-navigation norm and gives cover to Turkey to keep raising Dardanelles tolls and to other chokepoint holders to monetize passage.
causal🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumTrumpTurkeyDardanellesStrait of HormuzStrait of MalaccaSuez Canal
So what: A norm breakdown at chokepoints is a slow, global tax on seaborne trade; watch Turkey's Montreux-era Dardanelles tolling as a leading indicator.
US munitions and naval shortfall 5
The US Navy cannot escort Hormuz traffic (roughly 150 ships per day pre-war) because it lacks the vessels and because Iran presents a multi-threat mix of sea mines, fast boats, shahed drones, cruise missiles and long-range missiles that at volume get through.
data_point🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: highUS NavyIranStrait of Hormuzshahed drones
So what: The 'we can keep the strait open' narrative is operationally weak; tanker owners/insurers will price disruption, not protection.
“it's about 150 ships a day pre-war that went through. We don't have 150 vessels to to guard uh all of those ships” ▶ 16:05
US air-defense interceptors like THAAD and Patriot take years to replace and are being rationed for high-value assets such as aircraft carriers, so a Qatari LNG tanker may get no protection against a cheap shahed drone.
causal🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: highTHAADPatriotQatarLNGaircraft carriers
So what: Interceptor scarcity means merchant/energy shipping bears the risk directly; a bullish signal for war-risk insurance costs and Gulf LNG disruption.
The US munitions problem is a planning shortage, not an immediate one: stockpiles were shifted from the homeland to the Gulf while home reserves stay depleted, and naval planners are self-rationing to preserve munitions for a possible Taiwan/China conflict as soon as next year.
framework🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: highUnited StatesChinaTaiwan
So what: China contingency planning caps how much ordnance the US will expend on Iran, structurally limiting US ability to force Hormuz open.
US missile production competes directly with the semiconductor supply chain, so THAADs and Patriots are contending with cell phones, gaming platforms and cars for chips, forcing an unmade guns-versus-butter choice.
framework🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumTHAADPatriotsemiconductorsUnited States
So what: Defense-vs-consumer chip contention is a structural bottleneck for the whole missile-defense buildout thesis; watch NDAA language for civilian-to-military production mandates.
“missiles are in the chips competition competition space. So, every cell phone, gaming platform, cars, etc. are competing with our THADs and our patriots” ▶ 22:06
The reflexive munitions buildout is happening abroad, not in the US: Ukraine is scaling drones to thousands per day and selling to Gulf Arab states, and Volkswagen is repurposing troubled car plants for munitions for Israel, while US political will lags.
data_point🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumUkraineVolkswagenIsraelGulf Arab statesdrones
So what: Points to non-US defense/drone suppliers (Ukraine, European industrial converters) as the beneficiaries of Gulf air-defense demand.
GCC alignment and leverage 3
No GCC monarchy is ideologically aligned with Iran, but their threat perceptions diverge: Qatar accommodates (its gas fields were hit in March), Bahrain treats Iran as existential, Saudi is a reluctant bystander, and even hawkish UAE is now a powerless bystander fearing missiles on its cities and nuclear plant.
framework🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: highQatarBahrainSaudi ArabiaUAEGCCIran
So what: GCC disunity means no cohesive regional counterweight; each will hedge toward Iran if US resolve fades, undermining any 'Gulf coalition' trade thesis.
If US political will collapses, Gulf states have no military leverage over Iran (limited missiles, only US-supplied fighter jets) and will diplomatically oppose but practically accommodate an Iranian Hormuz tolling regime, passing costs to buyers.
prediction🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: highSaudi ArabiaGCCIranUnited StatesStrait of Hormuz
So what: The pivotal variable for oil is US staying power; a US drawdown flips Gulf producers into cost-passers and cements a Hormuz premium.
Gulf leaders carry living memory of great-power abandonment (Abu Dhabi's MBZ recalls Britain quitting in 1968, after which Iran seized three UAE islands), so a US withdrawal is a scenario they quietly track.
anecdote🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumMohammed bin ZayedUAEBritainIran
So what: Explains why Gulf states will pre-emptively appease Iran rather than bet on US permanence; a structural bias toward accommodation.
Oil price outlook and supply-destruction tail 6
China is the main reason gasoline is not $10 a gallon, having used its command economy to force down oil demand and consumption, and whether it keeps doing so is the single biggest swing factor for oil prices.
causal🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: highChinaoilgasoline
So what: Chinese demand restraint is the key marginal buyer signal; a Chinese pivot back to importing oil is the top upside catalyst for crude.
“The big story of why gas isn't $10 a gallon in the United States is China. they're the the counter measures they took to control energy demand and consumption is a huge part of why the crisis didn't get worse” ▶ 34:30
Base-case oil is elevated prices with a slow climb-down if things stabilize, with sharp upside if China resumes oil imports, SPRs hit a critical floor, or supply destruction occurs in Russian or Gulf infrastructure.
prediction🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumoilChinaSPRRussiaGulf Arab states
So what: A clear three-trigger checklist for going long oil volatility; monitor Chinese import data, SPR levels, and infrastructure-strike reports.
Destruction of the East-West pipeline or Gulf refineries could take Saudi oil offline for 3-5 years, forcing oil traders to price durable supply loss and triggering a genuine energy crisis rather than a transient spike.
prediction🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumEast-West pipelineSaudi Arabiaoilrefineries
So what: Physical supply destruction is the fat-tail scenario that reprices crude permanently higher; Iran is preserving these targets as escalation rungs.
Refining, not crude, is the tight part of the market: Ukrainian strikes on Russian refining capacity keep gasoline and diesel elevated, pushing importers like India to buy US product at high prices, which is why pump prices lag oil-price declines.
causal🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: highRussiaUkraineIndiaUnited Statesgasolinedieselrefining
So what: Crack spreads / refined-product margins are the better expression of this conflict than crude itself; watch Russian refinery strikes as the driver.
Iranian oil already on the water under the MOU (including Iranian barrels being delivered) creates a multi-week lag before disruption shows up in prices, mirroring the war's opening phase.
data_point🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumIranoil
So what: Near-term price calm can mislead; the lag means positioning ahead of the delayed physical tightening has an edge.
Bohl expects Hormuz to remain disrupted until at least early next year and possibly longer, rejecting a quick snap-back to normal.
prediction🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumStrait of HormuzIran
So what: A multi-quarter disruption horizon supports staying long the energy risk premium rather than fading each de-escalation headline.
“my kind of outlook is that we're looking at hormones disrupted until at least early next year and possibly longer” ▶ 41:05
Prolonged war as an anti-hydrocarbon accelerant 3
The longer the war runs, the more it acts as a 'green new war' that permanently destroys hydrocarbon demand by proving oil is too volatile to rely on, shrinking Iran's (and to a lesser extent Saudi/UAE) post-war oil market share.
thesis🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumIranSaudi ArabiaUAEoilrenewable energyChinaEuropean Union
So what: Long-duration Gulf conflict is a structural tailwind for energy-diversification and nuclear/renewables investment and a long-run headwind for Gulf oil market share.
“Trump is now waging a war, the green new war uh with Iran because it is really hammering uh that demand for hydrocarbons in the long run and creating a great deal of incentives for alternatives” ▶ 39:59
Buyers will attach a durable geopolitical risk premium to Persian Gulf and Iranian oil and preferentially source from the Americas and Canada, analogous to post-Covid reshoring accepting higher cost for supply security.
thesis🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumIranPersian GulfAmericasCanadaoil
So what: Structurally favors Western Hemisphere crude producers and a persistent Brent-quality premium for non-Gulf barrels.
A durable, multi-year GCC infrastructure push to bypass Hormuz is now underway (Fujairah expansion, Saudi-to-Turkey rail, revived Iraq/Syria pipelines), though it stays costlier than Hormuz and does not fully remove Iran's leverage since Iran can strike pipelines with missiles and drones.
data_point🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumFujairahSaudi ArabiaTurkeyIraqSyriaStrait of HormuzIran
So what: A multi-year capex cycle in Gulf bypass infrastructure (ports, rail, pipelines) is a durable spending theme even as it fails to eliminate the chokepoint risk.
Iran's near-term edge vs long-term trap 6
On a three-month horizon Iran holds the upper hand: its missiles hit targets more often as US interceptor stocks fall, US bases face their worst threat since Kuwait in 1991, US political will is weak, and the NDAA cannot even clear procedural steps amid Democratic opposition to war funding.
thesis🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: highIranUnited StatesNDAAKuwait
So what: Near-term momentum favors continued Iranian pressure and higher energy risk; the stalled NDAA is a concrete signal of eroding US resolve.
Iran's regime risks becoming addicted to confrontation because attacks on the 'Great Satan' rally the population and keep the opposition off the streets, with southern Iranians now angrier at Trump than at their own government over strikes on water and power.
causal🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumIranIRGCTrump
So what: Rally-around-the-flag dynamics mean US escalation may prolong, not shorten, the conflict, extending the energy-disruption horizon.
Iran could 'win the battle of Hormuz but lose the Cold War': securing tolling rights while its war economy and severed trade links leave a system that may fracture in 2-5 years via a protest going septic, echoing the sudden collapse of Assad's Syria.
framework🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumIranStrait of HormuzSyriaAssad
So what: Frames Iranian tactical wins as long-run regime fragility; a multi-year setup for eventual regime-change repricing of Iranian oil back into markets.
“The Iranians could win the battle of Hormuz, but their political system might not survive the pressures and the costs of that campaign in the long run” ▶ 46:27
Air campaigns cannot cause an Iranian uprising because bombing disorganizes the opposition as much as the regime; historically (Iraq, Libya) uprisings only succeed when they begin before the air campaign and need a pause in hostilities to catalyze.
framework🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: highIranIraqLibyaIRGC
So what: A maximum-pressure bombing escalation is unlikely to topple the regime near-term; it mainly raises energy-supply-destruction risk without the regime-change payoff.
As long as Iran's elite stays united in suppressing revolts and no US ground intervention gives an uprising fire, the regime can persist for another generation, comparable to Venezuela and North Korea outlasting expectations.
framework🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumIranIRGCVenezuelaNorth Korea
So what: Tempers bets on imminent Iranian collapse; Iran-linked risk is likely a persistent feature, not a soon-to-resolve event.
Iran's economy is deteriorating sharply, with inflation running around 50% before the war and now reportedly over 100%, a collapsing currency, compromised trade, and a worsening water-management crisis.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumIraninflation
So what: Quantifies the internal pressure that could eventually crack the regime, though Bohl cautions a near-term uprising is unlikely.
Iran's fractured decision-making 3
Iran's national security is driven by IRGC hardliners (who launched the late-June civilian shipping attacks), while President Pezeshkian and figures like Ghalibaf voice dissent but do not run the show.
framework🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: highIRGCPezeshkianGhalibafKhameneiIran
So what: Hardliner control means shipping attacks are policy, not accident; diplomacy signals from Iran's civilian side carry little market weight.
Iran's habit of striking militarily hours after a diplomatic agreement is a deliberate consensus mechanism (governed by informal 'wasta' influence) that lets hardliners fire missiles while diplomats negotiate, not a command-and-control breakdown.
framework🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumIranIRGCwasta
So what: Investors should expect diplomacy and violence to coexist by design, so ceasefire headlines are unreliable de-risking signals.
Israeli assassination of Ali Larijani, whom many Washington insiders viewed as conducive to diplomacy, hardened Iran's government and reduced negotiable interlocutors, contributing to US reluctance to greenlight further Israeli strikes.
causal🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumAli LarijaniIsraelIranUnited States
So what: Decapitation of moderates lengthens the conflict by removing diplomatic off-ramps, supporting a longer risk-premium regime.
Israel, Trump and the international flotilla question 4
Israel is a restrained bystander 'chomping at the bit' to rejoin the war, with a Netanyahu government needing a foreign-policy win before this year's elections, but the US is withholding a greenlight over limited operational value, escalation risk, munition drain, and Israel's continued push for regime change the US has abandoned.
thesis🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: highIsraelNetanyahuUnited StatesIran
So what: A US greenlight for Israel is the key escalation trigger to a wider regional war; its absence is currently capping the conflict box.
Trump is pursuing an Iran legacy win by 'swinging for the fences' and believes the midterms can be secured via the Save America Act and gerrymandering, judging that de-escalating on Iran's terms would damage his midterm standing as much as continuing the war.
thesis🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumTrumpIranSave America ActUS midterms
So what: Trump's legacy motive plus domestic-vote workarounds reduce the odds of a face-saving climbdown, biasing toward prolonged conflict.
A multinational escort flotilla to keep Hormuz open is unlikely because navies are conserving hulls (China for a Taiwan campaign, UK/France for irreplaceable carriers), a deep US-China/Europe trust deficit blocks coordination, and for China a $1-2M toll is cheaper than an open-ended deployment potentially fighting Iran with China-supplied weapons.
framework🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumChinaUnited KingdomFranceIndiaChinaStrait of Hormuz
So what: Rules out a fast multilateral fix for Hormuz, keeping the disruption premium in place longer than a coalition-hope narrative implies.
If Hormuz disruption runs another year, China, India and Russia are likely to apply real political pressure on Iran to moderate, since China is also burning through its reserves and cannot suppress its own oil demand indefinitely.
prediction🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumChinaIndiaRussiaIranStrait of Hormuz
So what: The eventual circuit-breaker on Iran is its own patrons, not the West; watch Chinese reserve levels and diplomatic posture as the moderation signal.
Second- and third-order commodity effects 2
Beyond oil, the conflict is squeezing helium, nitrates (used in semiconductor manufacturing), copper, aluminum and steel, driven both by blocked Gulf exports and by countries like China withholding these inputs from the international market.
data_point🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: mediumheliumnitratessemiconductorscopperaluminumsteelChina
So what: Supply-chain-critical materials, not just crude, are exposed; RANE reports clients are commissioning maps of the affected commodity list.
Import-dependent countries will bear the brunt of the supply tightening, and their resulting political instability (e.g., Sri Lanka disrupting textiles) is the third-order channel through which the conflict ripples into further supply-chain disruption.
framework🗣 Ryan Bohl (RANE Middle East geopolitical analyst)conviction: lowSri Lankatextilescommodity importers
So what: Fragile net-importers are the watch list for contagion; instability there can cascade into unexpected sector disruptions.

Alpha Exchange · Dean Curnutt 1 episode(s) · 36 insights

Franklin Parlamis, Founder & CIO, Aequim Alternative Investments71 min36 insights2026-07-21
▶ Watch on YouTube
Franklin Parlamis, CIO of convertible-arbitrage fund Aequim, walks through the credit-vs-equity-vol arbitrage at the heart of converts and warns that today's tight credit spreads plus very high single-name implied vols (AI/data-center names, Oracle, CoreWeave) make the trade far more speculative than the wide-spread, high-vol setup of 2009. He flags contingent-option credit risk, an unexplained shift to inverse skew, and argues the way to trade an uncertain vol regime is a portfolio of asymmetric bets rather than a directional call on whether single-name vol is permanently higher.
Correlation-justified leverage (Russia 1998) · 2GFC convert unwind and the 'machine' · 4Live to fight another day · 2Credit-vs-vol arbitrage mechanics · 4AI / data-center convert supply · 3Contingent options and credit-vol correlation risk · 6Asset floor and management optionality · 2Inverse skew regime · 3Mandatory convertibles and gamma dynamics · 3Black-Scholes critique and custom forward distributions · 2Convert community as alpha and forward view · 5
Correlation-justified leverage (Russia 1998) 2
In the 1998 Russian default, hedge funds that bought high-yield ruble GKOs and hedged into dollars via currency forwards were secretly relying on an anti-correlation (ruble devaluation offsetting bond losses); the trade blew up because the counterparty banks providing the currency hedges went bankrupt.
causal🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highRussiaGKOshedge fundsrublecurrency forwards
So what: A 'hedge' that quietly depends on a correlation assumption is really leverage; counterparty solvency can be the hidden factor that turns a hedged book into a directional blowup.
Arbitrage traders justify leverage by an assumed correlation or anti-correlation between two related assets, and the more confidence they have in that structure the bigger the position gets, so even a small breakdown in the correlation can blow up the volatility of the pair.
framework🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: higharbitrageleveragecorrelation
So what: Position size scales with correlation confidence; stress-test the pair's vol for correlation breaks, not just the level mispricing.
GFC convert unwind and the 'machine' 4
Convertible arbitrage depends on an intact market 'machine' — the ability to rehypothecate long convertibles and to borrow stock; both broke in the 2008 GFC, forcing leveraged arb players who cared about the arbitrage (not the floor value) to liquidate.
causal🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highconvertible arbitrageGFCrehypothecationstock borrowhedge funds
So what: Convert arb has multiple single points of failure (financing, stock loan, credit, equity/rate vol); when leveraged plumbing breaks the asset class can dislocate regardless of fundamentals.
“It was the machine broke. And when the machine breaks and the machine has leverage on it, you get that kind of behavior.” ▶ 8:21
Managers who performed well into the 2008 stress were psychologically encouraged to step into more risk as competitors failed, but September-October 2008 was mayhem where only liquidity mattered — a 'winner's curse' where being right earlier just meant owning more at the true crisis low.
anecdote🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highCitadel2008liquidity
So what: Outperforming into a crisis can bait you into adding risk right before the final flush; survival depends on liquidity, not on having been right.
Pine River (under Brian Taylor) treated the money as investor capital and returned it on demand in late 2008 even at humiliating realized losses, delevering to get 'on sides,' and then had a strong 2009 — whereas managers who gated simply marked positions down slowly instead of aggressively.
anecdote🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highPine River CapitalBrian Taylor2009gating
So what: Right-sizing at the bottom, even at ugly prints, positioned funds to profit in the 2009 recovery; gating deferred but did not avoid the losses.
Convertible bonds are a 'dilettante's discipline' sitting at the intersection of rates, equity vol, credit, credit vol and financing — you don't need to be great at any one thing but many contributing factors means many things can go wrong at once.
framework🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: mediumconvertible bondsratescreditvolatility
So what: The multi-disciplinary nature is both the edge and the tail risk: a confluence of several markets deteriorating (as in the GFC) hits converts hardest.
“converts are a dilotance discipline” ▶ 10:39
Live to fight another day 2
The core survival rule of arbitrage investing is to treat your own judgment as just one more variable that can be wrong; if you trade with that humility you will always size to survive, because the ability to stay in the game is the single most important thing.
framework🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: higharbitragerisk managementposition sizing
So what: Size positions so that being wrong doesn't force you out; staying solvent is a precondition to capturing any edge.
“the ability to stay in is the literal most important thing um in the game.” ▶ 13:27
Even at the GFC lows there was a real downside case (collapse of the entire financial system); markets mostly resolve to the upside but that outcome was not predestined, so any 'I know this is too cheap' conviction should trigger a hubris check.
contrarian🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highfinancial crisisGFC
So what: Hindsight makes crisis lows look obvious; guard against sizing as if a cheap price guarantees the good outcome.
Credit-vs-vol arbitrage mechanics 4
Taking credit risk (owning a high-yield bond) is historically the richest form of short volatility to sell — a better money-making short-vol expression than any tail-selling strategy in listed options — because high-yield returns have proven to be a durable short-vol premium.
thesis🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highhigh yield bondscredit riskshort volatilitylisted options
So what: Frame credit exposure as short vol; the convert arb sells that rich credit vol and buys cheap vol via the embedded convertible option.
The 'surplus vol' you pay in a convert depends entirely on how wide the credit spread is: at a 1,200-over credit, buying 80 vol leaves modest surplus vol because credit earnings pay down your Vega, but at a 100-over credit, buying 80 vol means roughly 70 of the 80 points is surplus vol you must realize yourself.
framework🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highcredit spreadsimplied volatilityVegaconvertible bonds
So what: Today's tight spreads mean the vol you buy in converts is almost all surplus vol that must be realized through gamma trading, not subsidized by credit carry — a much lower-quality trade than in 2009.
“if a credit's 100 over, like many many credits we're trading today are, and you're buying 80 V, 70 of the 80 is the surplus vault, right?” ▶ 0:12
Credit spreads are mostly an expression of risk premium rather than of true default likelihood — high-yield prices do not imply companies will actually default at the implied rate; you are being paid a significant premium simply for the act of taking credit risk.
framework🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highcredit spreadshigh yieldrisk premiumdefault
So what: When credit spreads are wide, risk premia are wide across all asset classes simultaneously, which is why wide-spread regimes are the best entry points for converts and for straightforward risk alike.
“credit spreads I think are mostly an expression of risk premium as opposed to an expression of of of true default likelihood” ▶ 21:58
For long-dated volatility (e.g. a five-year call, which is what trades in converts) the risk-premium component historically dominates the Vega effect, so a vol spike that should raise implied from 40 to 45-50 can leave the long-dated option trading around 37 or 35 as its risk premium widens — the opposite of short-dated options, which trade rich.
contrarian🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highlong-dated volatilityVIXS&P putsconvertible bondsrisk premium
So what: You can buy long-dated convert vol at a discount to forward expected realized vol even during vol spikes, whereas short-dated listed options stay rich; the two ends of the term structure behave oppositely.
“the risk premium component will has at least historically tended to dominate the Vega effect” ▶ 23:30
AI / data-center convert supply 3
AI and data-center builders (e.g. CoreWeave) have become the aggressive convertible issuers because a multi-year buildout with no revenue for years can't service much 9% fixed debt but can issue 2% convertibles, a large interest saving they achieve by selling their stock at a premium through the option market.
data_point🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highCoreWeavedata centersAIconvertible bonds
So what: The current convert supply is concentrated in pre-cash-flow AI/data-center names, so the asset class's risk profile is tied to whether the AI buildout pays off.
A higher interest-rate environment drives more convertible issuance because the zero coupon floor makes the savings versus straight debt larger (an issuer saving ~6% at 5% rates vs ~2% at 1% rates), and a bigger convert asset class carries more liquidity and more distant tail risk than a small one with few players.
framework🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: mediuminterest ratesconvertible issuanceliquidityBerkshire Hathaway
So what: Higher rates are structurally bullish for convert supply, opportunity set and liquidity — a positive for arbitrageurs relative to the low-rate 2021 regime.
The AI/data-center issuers are companies with roughly two-year track records and 100-vol stocks that the market is still using to figure out whether the AI thesis works, producing extreme short-term realized vol but no long history to anchor five-year option pricing.
data_point🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: mediumdata centersAIrealized volatility
So what: There is no reliable base rate for these names' long-run vol, so five-year convert options are being priced on thin, unstable observables.
Contingent options and credit-vol correlation risk 6
The long-dated option embedded in a convertible is contingent on the issuer not going bankrupt — it is struck at the conversion price, so if the issuer defaults you never actually owned the option you thought you had; converts only reliably 'work' in upside volatility because rallies never force you to test the credit hypothesis.
causal🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highconvertible bondscredit riskbankruptcyvolatility
So what: The seemingly clean long-vol payoff of a convert is actually a credit-contingent option; a downside test of these AI issuers could break the arb precisely when you most want the vol.
“converts always work in upside volatility because you're not being forced to test your credit hypothesis” ▶ 34:36
A selloff in the data-center space — which Parlamis says feels like it may be starting ('physics happening a little bit right now') — would finally test whether these issuers are creditworthy and is where the current convertible arbitrages could break down.
prediction🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: mediumdata centersAIconvertible arbitragecredit
So what: Watch for the first real drawdown in AI/data-center names as the trigger that stress-tests convert credit assumptions and unwinds crowded arb positions.
Managers are being asked to price five-year convertible vol off one-and-a-half-year observables like 2028 LEAPs, while the entire listed option market covers only roughly 2-5% of the Vega sold into the market by the convertible bonds — nowhere near enough to be the dominant hedging venue.
data_point🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: mediumLEAPslisted optionsVegaconvertible bonds
So what: The Vega supplied by converts dwarfs listed-option liquidity, so hedging is imperfect and the forward-vol pricing is closer to guesswork than to a clean executable arbitrage.
Forward realized volatility for these 80-to-120-vol names is not smooth and should be modeled like the jump component of a jump-diffusion equity model — regimes displace discontinuously (a name is either 90 vol or 50 vol, not gliding between them).
framework🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: mediumrealized volatilityjump diffusionconvertible bonds
So what: Pricing these converts on smooth vol-path assumptions understates gap risk; the real exposure is to discrete regime jumps in single-name vol.
If this cycle ends with established low-vol investment-grade issuers (Parlamis uses Microsoft as a hypothetical) absorbing 80-to-100-vol startups for stock, the back-end Vega of convert arbitrage is impaired — but today's very tight credit spreads leave no room for the offsetting credit tightening that would normally cushion that Vega hit.
causal🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: mediumMicrosoftOraclemergersVegacredit spreads
So what: Stock-for-stock M&A by IG acquirers is a hidden risk to convert vol books, and the usual credit-spread offset is unavailable when spreads are already tight — an asymmetric downside.
The old world of reliably 25-30 vol 'low-vol' tech leaders no longer exists — Oracle is now roughly a 55-vol stock after deliberately changing its business plan — so managers must guess where a name's vol truly sits rather than rely on ten years of historical realized vol.
data_point🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: mediumOracleMicrosoftNASDAQvolatility
So what: Historical vol anchors are broken for tech; even mega-caps can re-rate to high-vol names, undermining assumptions baked into existing convert positions.
Asset floor and management optionality 2
A convertible is good or bad depending on whether the issuer's legacy business provides an 'asset floor' that buffers credit and equity on the downside; a single-drug phase-three biotech convert is a binary bet with no credit floor, whereas a biotech with three cash-flowing drugs chasing a fourth has a floor that protects recovery value.
framework🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highbiotechconvertible bondscreditasset floor
So what: The key screen for a convert is downside recovery value: prefer issuers whose existing business floors the credit, and apply the same asset-floor test to Oracle's legacy business as it pivots.
In straight credit you are effectively short a 'management put' — management can re-lever or make acquisitions that break a credit thesis that looked fine on EBITDA coverage — while in convertible space you are 'straddling management' because the equity option upside makes management's discretion more symmetric.
framework🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highcreditconvertible bondsmanagementEBITDA
So what: Judgments about management capital-allocation behavior are central to convert selection; the equity-linked payoff partly offsets the short-management-put risk that plagues pure credit.
Inverse skew regime 3
Traditional equity skew (vol rises as the stock falls) has clearly changed to an environment where stocks rise and vol rises with them, flattening skews; Parlamis admits he has no clean explanation but says the shift is unmistakable.
contrarian🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: mediumskewvolatilityequity options
So what: The breakdown of traditional skew changes how converts and mandatory skew profiles should be priced and hedged; up-vol behavior is now the prototypical pattern.
Parlamis's macro hypothesis for inverse skew: if the system defaults on debt, skew should be traditional (issuers approaching the 'sucking sound of bankruptcy' get more volatile down), but if the government monetizes debt and risks inflation, printing to prevent defaults removes downside default risk while adding upside/inflation risk, producing inverse skew.
causal🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: exploratoryskewdebt monetizationinflationgovernment policybankruptcy
So what: If non-standard monetary tools are driving inverse skew, the regime persists as long as the policy backstop does — a structural, not transient, change in the shape of vol surfaces.
In a bubble, skew should be inverse because the upside is effectively infinite while the downside is bounded at zero; consistent with this, inverse skew is already visible in listed-option arbitrage but convertible bonds still retain some traditional skew (though maybe not worth paying for).
framework🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: mediumskewbubblelisted optionsconvertible bonds
So what: A residual traditional skew in converts versus inverse skew in listed options is itself a potential relative-value opportunity, and a bubble read argues the convert skew is unlikely to be worth much.
Mandatory convertibles and gamma dynamics 3
Oracle was the first big mandatory convertible; a mandatory pays dividends for three years then mandatorily converts to stock, with a 'flat zone' that returns par within a ~25% range, a minimum share count to the upside and a maximum to the downside — making it effectively long a call at one strike and short a put at a lower strike (a pure skew instrument).
framework🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highOraclemandatory convertiblesskewoptions
So what: Mandatories are a distinct discipline from ordinary converts — a stock forward plus a concentrated skew position — and, with a 3-year-into-2-year LEAP-like term, are more directly arbitrageable against listed options.
Because mandatories concentrate large long and short gamma at specific strikes, the position itself can move the underlying: when the market is long too much gamma at a strike, realized vol dampens there as everyone monetizes gamma, and when short too much, vol is enhanced at that strike.
causal🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highmandatory convertiblesgammavolatility
So what: Size a mandatory against the stock's average daily volume and issuer size; the stock's price action near key strikes may be gamma-driven rather than fundamental information.
To trade mandatory convertibles correctly you must sometimes disbelieve the stock's gamma-driven move and hold through it — which, in conventional terms, means you effectively have to be a 'bad risk manager' in the traditional sense.
contrarian🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: mediummandatory convertiblesgammarisk management
So what: Standard stop-loss discipline can be exactly wrong around concentrated-gamma strikes; the edge comes from fading gamma-induced moves rather than respecting them.
“you have to basically be a bad risk manager to trade in the traditional sense to trade mandatory conversals correctly.” ▶ 50:29
Black-Scholes critique and custom forward distributions 2
For a three-year 60-vol name there should be essentially no skew between the 80%- and 100%-moneyness options, because such a stock's true forward distribution is bimodal (like a roulette bet that ends at 0 or 200, not a bell curve centered up 15%) — and skew is only worth anything if the stock lingers near a strike, which these names won't.
contrarian🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highBlack-Scholesskewvolatilityforward distribution
So what: Arguing over the 'right' skew on a high-vol name is largely wasted effort; a bimodal forward view says that skew carries almost no Vega by expiry, so don't overpay or overhedge it.
Parlamis advocates abandoning the Black-Scholes stochastic treatment for high-vol names in favor of pricing options in dollar/break-even terms and drawing custom forward probability distributions — a 'lava lamp' redistribution tool (essentially a Monte Carlo valuation) that he says AI could now easily build.
framework🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: mediumBlack-ScholesMonte Carloforward distributionAI
So what: A pragmatic scenario/break-even valuation of long-dated options is likely to beat arguing implied-vol skew levels; the tooling gap is now trivial to close with AI.
Convert community as alpha and forward view 5
Aequim views alpha as coming partly from being embedded in the tight-knit convert community's flow and positioning — connected to dealers and even competitors — pursuing 'Pareto-optimal' trading where you make money without taking it from a counterparty, contrasting sharply with the 'creditor-on-creditor violence' (uptiering) now infecting credit and distressed markets.
thesis🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: mediumAequim Alternative Investmentsconvertible bondscredit marketsdealers
So what: Relationship-driven flow and a collaborative convert culture are a real information edge; the predatory uptiering dynamic in distressed credit is a structural negative for that space.
“creditor on creditor violence” ▶ 1:05:20
Trading volatility forces humility — you must assume you are the stupidest person in the room on every single trade and can't hold too many strong views, or you won't last — which Parlamis frames as both a survival trait and the reason the convert community stays collaborative.
framework🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: highvolatilityrisk managementconvertible bonds
So what: Persistent humility and view-minimization is the behavioral edge in vol trading; overconfidence is the fastest route out of the game.
“you have to assume that you're the stupidest person in the room on every single trade.” ▶ 1:04:49
There is an open debate in converts — veterans complain implied vols are higher than anything ever paid, while the next generation argues a paradigm shift has made single-name vol permanently higher — and a five-year shift is effectively permanent for most careers, with converts stuck in a positive feedback loop where 'flow begets profit begets flows.'
thesis🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: exploratoryconvertible bondsimplied volatilitysingle-name volatility
So what: Whether elevated single-name vol is permanent is the central unresolved question; the self-reinforcing flow-profit-flow loop can sustain rich vol far longer than fundamentals justify.
“the next generation suggests that we have experienced a paradigm shift where single name uh volatility has moved permanently higher” ▶ 1:06:10
Parlamis's answer to not knowing whether single-name vol is permanently higher is to refuse the binary and instead build a portfolio of asymmetric trades — each roughly 'make two, lose one' in both possible worlds — so the book profits regardless of which regime prevails, the hallmark of a true arbitrage portfolio.
framework🗣 Franklin Parlamisconviction: higharbitrageconvertible bondsPolymarketKalshi
So what: Market-neutral positioning means constructing asymmetric bets against each prevailing macro call rather than making a directional vol-regime forecast — a repeatable way to trade radical uncertainty.
The host (Dean Curnutt) argues the accelerating pace of change in markets makes long-convexity products like convertibles attractive, but stresses that nothing is a win at any price — you still have to buy the convexity at the right price.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: mediumconvertible bondsconvexity
So what: Long-convexity exposure is a favored structural stance amid faster market change, but valuation discipline still governs whether converts are actually a good buy.

What Bitcoin Did · Peter McCormack 1 episode(s) · 35 insights

An Economist’s Warning: Massive Inflation Is Coming | Charles Goodhart61 min35 insights2026-07-21
▶ Watch on YouTube
Economist Charles Goodhart argues that Western fiscal positions (UK, US, France) have become unsustainable, and because politicians won't cut spending or raise the right taxes, central banks will lose the ability to hold their inflation targets — making 'massive inflation' at some undated point almost certain. He frames the 1990-2020 low-inflation era as a temporary, demographically-driven fluke that is now reversing, and prescribes shifting taxation from labor/profits toward land and property.
Fiscal dominance and the inflation warning · 6End of the disinflation era and China's demographic reversal · 5Shifting tax from labor to land · 3Interest rates, asset prices and housing · 3Democracy's short-termism and pension politics · 5Inequality and the forgotten worker · 4UK decline, Germany and mismeasured growth · 5Demographics, immigration and what to watch · 4
Fiscal dominance and the inflation warning 6
Because politicians have failed to address a severely worsening fiscal position, central banks will find it very difficult to maintain their inflation targets and monetary control — the thesis of Goodhart's book 'The Unanchored Central Banker'.
thesis🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highcentral banksThe Unanchored Central BankerCharles Goodhart
So what: Signals structurally higher and less-controllable inflation ahead; favors real assets and inflation hedges over long-duration nominal bonds.
“because the politicians have not dealt with uh the worsening severely worsening fiscal position. It's going to be very difficult for central banks to maintain uh infl the inflation target and monetary control.” ▶ 13:15
When governments keep paying out more than they raise in tax, the country effectively goes fiscally bankrupt and the almost-certain outcome is massive inflation at some stage, with or without a financial crisis.
prediction🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highinflationsovereign debtfiscal policy
So what: Base case is inflationary debt debasement rather than default; position for currency debasement and rising long-term inflation risk premia.
“the country effectually goes fiscally bankrupt and the outcome outcome is almost certainly massive inflation at some stage with or possibly without a financial crisis” ▶ 0:00
The UK, the United States and France are all in the same unsustainable fiscal position with the same political inability to impose restraint, so the problem is not idiosyncratic to Britain.
thesis🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highUKUnited StatesFrance
So what: A synchronized developed-market fiscal problem argues for gold and hard assets over rotating between fiat currencies as a hedge.
“the United States and France to name but two others are very much in the same kind of boat as we are with the same kind of political problems” ▶ 16:50
There is a doom-loop between central bank and government: raising rates to hit the inflation target increases debt interest on the existing massive government debt, worsening the fiscal position and inviting more political pressure to cut rates.
causal🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highcentral banksinterest ratesgovernment debt
So what: Explains why authorities may tolerate above-target inflation; steepeners and inflation-linked exposure can express the tension between fiscal need and price stability.
“the moment it raises interest rates, it raises the debt interest on the existing massive debt that the government has to pay and makes the government's fiscal position even worse.” ▶ 17:31
President Trump continually pressures the Federal Reserve to lower interest rates specifically to make it easier to sustain a huge and growing US deficit.
causal🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highDonald TrumpFederal ReserveUS deficit
So what: Political capture of monetary policy raises the risk of the Fed easing into inflation; watch Fed independence as a regime-shift signal.
“President Trump spends his life uh telling the Federal Reserve Board to lower interest rates in order to make it easier for him to maintain a huge and growing deficit in the US.” ▶ 17:50
US national debt has reached roughly 39.5 trillion dollars, up from about 35 trillion not long ago, with neither party willing to impose restraint — Republicans spending on the military and Democrats on social welfare.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumUS debtRepublicansDemocratsDonald TrumpJoe Biden
So what: Rapidly compounding debt with no political brake underpins the structural case for debasement and higher term premia.
“39.5 trillion I believe it's hit now. Is that correct, Con? And it seems it wasn't so long ago it was 35 trillion.” ▶ 18:06
End of the disinflation era and China's demographic reversal 5
The low-inflation period from roughly 1990 to 2020 was a temporary, fortunate accident driven by a labor supply surge — China and Eastern Europe entering world trade plus women entering the workforce — not by central-bank brilliance.
framework🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highChinaEastern Europecentral banksglobalization
So what: If the disinflation was demographic rather than institutional, its reversal means structurally higher inflation regardless of central-bank credibility.
“It was a temporary fortunate period. But people didn't realize how temporary and how fortunate it was. partly because they thought it was all due to the brilliance and expertise of the central banks” ▶ 22:42
From 1990 onward the prices of internationally traded goods fell on average by about 1 to 1.5 percent per annum, letting economies grow while shifting production from industry to services.
data_point🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: hightraded goodsglobalizationChina
So what: The tailwind that suppressed goods inflation for three decades is fading; goods disinflation can no longer offset services inflation.
“the prices of goods which are internationally traded were going down on average by about 1 to 1 and a half% peranom.” ▶ 21:46
China's demographic collapse is extreme — birth rates around 0.5 to 0.7 mean there will soon be fewer workers than old people, ending its role as a global disinflationary force.
data_point🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highChinaSouth KoreaEast Asiademographics
So what: The world's cheap-labor supplier is aging out; expect Chinese wage and price pressure to become inflationary for the West.
“the birth rate is below one something between 0.5 and 0.7 uh and that means that there will be fewer workers than old old people in the not tooistant future.” ▶ 24:00
China's huge current account surplus will flip to a current account deficit as its aging population dissaves — high savings today reflect a weak welfare safety net, but retirees will draw those savings down.
prediction🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highChinacurrent accountsavings ratio
So what: A shift from Chinese surplus to deficit removes a major global buyer of Western debt and pushes global real interest rates up.
“the current huge uh Chinese current account surplus is going to shift to becoming a current account deficit in the not tooistant future.” ▶ 24:48
As labor becomes scarce relative to capital, power shifts to workers who will demand higher wages, and unless taxation shifts off labor they will also be taxed much more heavily.
prediction🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highlaborwagesdemographics
So what: A structural reversal of the capital-over-labor trade of the last 40 years; wage-driven inflation and margin pressure become the regime.
Shifting tax from labor to land 3
Goodhart argues taxation should move away from incomes and profits toward assets, particularly land and property, because land is immobile and cannot be relocated abroad the way a wealth tax base can.
framework🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highland value taxproperty taxwealth tax
So what: A policy drift toward land/property taxation would hit real-estate valuations while a mobile wealth tax would drive capital flight — watch which route governments take.
“it ought to shift away from uh taxing incomes and profits to taxing assets particularly land and property.” ▶ 25:44
Taxing land actually encourages more productive use of it and can enhance growth, unlike a wealth tax which the wealthy avoid by going abroad.
causal🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highland value taxwealth taxproductivity
So what: Land taxation is the growth-friendlier fiscal fix; a broad wealth tax is likely to fail on capital flight.
“the great advantage of taxing land is it's immobile and if anything if you tax land it actually encourages people to make better use of land. So if anything it it enhances growth” ▶ 45:49
Rachel Reeves reduced the UK deficit through fiscal drag — freezing income-tax thresholds against inflation — which pushes ordinary working people into higher brackets, is very unpopular and is a disincentive to work.
causal🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highRachel ReevesUK Treasuryincome taxfiscal drag
So what: Stealth tax rises via frozen thresholds are the UK's default fiscal tool; they erode disposable income and labor incentives without headline rate hikes.
“what Rachel Reeves did uh was try to reduce the scale of the deficit by not shifting the thresholds at which you pay income tax to take account of inflation.” ▶ 26:26
Interest rates, asset prices and housing 3
The dramatic rise in asset and house values is largely mechanical: as interest rates fell, the discount rate on future asset returns fell, so present asset values rose.
framework🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highinterest ratesasset priceshousing
So what: Implies that a sustained higher-rate regime should compress asset valuations, reversing decades of financial-asset and housing appreciation.
“as interest rates decline, asset values rise because the the determination of the asset value is effectively what you expect with the asset to bring in terms of returns discounted by the interest rate.” ▶ 28:49
UK house prices have risen dramatically faster than real incomes, driving the house-price-to-income ratio sharply higher and locking young workers out of ownership.
data_point🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highUK housinghouse-price-to-income ratio
So what: Housing affordability stress is a durable political and demographic driver, feeding both socialist-leaning politics and lower birth rates.
“the increase in the value of housing has been dramatically faster than the increase in in real incomes.” ▶ 27:27
Falling birth and marriage rates are linked to young people being unable to afford homes and living with their parents, because people do not marry and have children while living with parents.
causal🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: mediumbirth ratehousingdemographics
So what: Housing unaffordability compounds the dependency-ratio crisis by suppressing births, worsening the long-run fiscal math.
“you don't marry and don't have children if you're living with your parents.” ▶ 28:20
Democracy's short-termism and pension politics 5
Democracy structurally blocks long-term economic planning because the next election is always near, creating a misalignment between what wins power and what the country needs.
framework🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highdemocracyfiscal policy
So what: Explains why fiscal correction is unlikely pre-emptively; investors should expect reform only after a forcing crisis, not ahead of one.
“So democracy is the block to any kind of long-term economic planning.” ▶ 0:17
Countries only restore their fiscal positions after going through a major financial crisis — Goodhart cites Iceland, Ireland and Greece as the examples.
framework🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highIcelandIrelandGreecefiscal reform
So what: Implies the UK/US path to fiscal repair runs through a crisis first; positions that pay off in a fiscal/inflation crisis are the relevant hedge.
“if you look at countries that have actually managed to restore their overall fiscal position, you will find that these are countries which have been through a major crisis... Iceland, Ireland and Greece.” ▶ 14:59
The UK triple lock on pensions gives the old an ever-increasing share of GDP and is economically indefensible and unaffordable as the elderly grow as a share of the population, yet politicians including Andy Burnham and Nigel Farage vow to keep it.
thesis🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: hightriple lockUK pensionsAndy BurnhamNigel Farage
So what: Entrenched pension commitments are a hard-to-cut driver of rising spending; their political untouchability signals fiscal drift will continue.
“the triple lock uh on any decent logical piece of economics is is indefensible.” ▶ 38:16
Politicians protect unaffordable pension promises because they fear losing the old vote — the old vote and the young do not.
causal🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highpensionsvotingdemographics
So what: The gerontocratic voter base means spending cuts fall on the young and workers, reinforcing intergenerational fiscal transfer.
≈ “they're scared of losing your vote. And yeah, and the old vote and the young don't.” paraphrase
Political polarization is rising sharply from the old centrist Butskellite consensus, and at both extremes there is a growing sense that a loss to the other side is existential and cannot be allowed.
thesis🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: mediumpolitical polarizationRab ButlerHugh GaitskellDonald TrumpZohran Mamdani
So what: Wider policy swings between governments raise regime and tail risk, arguing for portfolio robustness to sharp policy reversals.
“could lead to um a feeling at both extremes that if we lose to the other extreme, it is so awful that we simply cannot allow it to happen.” ▶ 35:47
Inequality and the forgotten worker 4
World inequality has actually fallen: Chinese poverty dropped from around 80 percent living in poverty in 1970-75 to under 1 percent now, even as inequality within the West rose from the same labor-supply shock.
contrarian🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highChinaVietnamThailandIndiaglobal inequality
So what: Counters the popular narrative of rising global inequality; the real story is a redistribution from Western labor to Asian labor and Western capital.
“if you go back to 197075 the end of Mong and all that something like 80% of the Chinese were living in poverty... now less than 1% of the Chinese are living in poverty” ▶ 29:59
The same offshoring of Western manufacturing that raised Asian living standards depressed Western labor and rewarded capital owners, fueling the 'forgotten worker' backlash behind MAGA support for Trump and Marine Le Pen's support in France.
causal🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highMAGADonald TrumpMarine Le Penblue-collar workersglobalization
So what: Populist political risk is rooted in a real economic mechanism, so the anti-globalization/protectionist policy trend is likely durable.
“That was really what fueled the make America great again the MAGA support for Trump. there was a feeling that the bluecollar workers in the US had been forgotten.” ▶ 31:53
White-collar and graduate workers now face a similar displacement, with AI potentially doing to the graduate elite what globalization did to factory workers.
prediction🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: exploratoryAIgraduate laborwhite-collar workers
So what: Suggests the next populist wave and labor-market disruption hits professional services; sectors reliant on graduate labor face structural pressure.
“I question whether AI is going to do for the graduate elite what globalism did for factory workers.” ▶ 32:52
Conditions for young university leavers are already considerably worse than they otherwise would be because of AI, on top of unaffordable housing.
thesis🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: mediumAIgraduate employmenthousing
So what: Weakening entry-level graduate hiring is an early, observable AI-displacement signal worth tracking in labor data.
“the conditions for young university levers at the moment are considerably worse than they would otherwise have been because of AI.” ▶ 33:15
UK decline, Germany and mismeasured growth 5
Britain's imperial dominance was an accident of naval power plus being the birthplace of the industrial revolution, and its decline became inevitable once air power supplanted naval power and scale shifted advantage to larger nations like the US, China and next India — with World War I the turning point.
framework🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highUKUnited StatesChinaIndiaWorld War I
So what: Frames UK underperformance as structural and irreversible at the imperial level, tempering any 'British renaissance' investment thesis.
“when air power came to dominate naval power uh the um powers went to the countries in larger scale.” ▶ 3:10
London remains the world's largest international financial hub and the UK runs a very large services surplus, which keeps the economy afloat despite manufacturing decline.
thesis🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highLondonfinancial servicesUK services surplus
So what: UK's remaining comparative advantage is financial and legal services, not goods; policy that damages the City is disproportionately costly.
“we have a great strength with the uh the role of London as an international financial hub. It's the the largest international financial hub in the world” ▶ 7:17
Germany's powerhouse business model — importing cheap Russian energy and exporting machinery and cars to China — has broken because both inputs are gone, moving Germany from Europe's driving force to its tail end while southern Europe like Spain does relatively better.
causal🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highGermanyRussiaChinaSpainsouthern Europe
So what: A structurally broken German export model argues against German industrials and favors a relative rotation toward Spain/southern Europe.
“You imported cheap energy from Russia and you exported machinery and cars to China. Both of those have gone and that means they're without their standard business model.” ▶ 54:45
Much of the measured productivity boom of the 1950s-1980s was an illusion: as married women in the workforce rose from about 25 percent to 75 percent by 2020, unpaid home and childcare work that GDP never counted shifted into measured GDP.
contrarian🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highGDPproductivityfemale labor participation
So what: If past productivity growth was partly a measurement artifact, expectations of returning to that growth rate are misplaced; policy anchored to it will disappoint.
“the proportion of married women working in the workforce was by 25%. By the time you actually marital age women, by the time you get to about 2020, it's 75%.” ▶ 49:09
GDP should never be equated with welfare or happiness: on a broad welfare, family and health basis Europe is at least as good as and probably better than the US, despite the US being far ahead on GDP alone.
framework🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highGDPwelfareEuropeUnited States
So what: Cautions against using headline GDP gaps to judge relative prosperity or to justify policy; welfare and GDP can diverge sharply.
“you should you should never believe that GDP equates with welfare and happiness it doesn't” ▶ 50:56
Demographics, immigration and what to watch 4
Working-age immigration improves the UK's worsening dependency ratio because immigrants are almost all of working age, and the government's move to ban carer immigration visas threatens social-care provision for an aging population.
causal🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highimmigrationdependency ratiosocial careZimbabweSouth Africa
So what: Restricting working-age migration worsens the fiscal dependency math and care capacity — a self-inflicted structural cost worth tracking in policy.
“the immigrants that we get are virtually all of working age and so they make the ratio of of workers to dependent better than otherwise.” ▶ 9:37
A combination of AI and robotics offsetting fewer workers is the main plausible source of hope against demographic decline, but nobody knows if it will materialize, and until it does the trajectory is downward.
prediction🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: exploratoryAIroboticsproductivitydemographics
So what: AI-driven productivity is the swing factor that could avert the demographic/fiscal crisis; its realization is the key variable to monitor.
“a combination of AI and robotics will do it for us. And you can't exclude that... But the problem is we don't know.” ▶ 48:14
The roughly 70 years to 2020 were probably the best in growth, living standards and conditions the UK and Western world have ever seen, and many of the conditions that produced that period are now over.
thesis🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highWestern worldUKliving standards
So what: Sets a sober baseline: the next several decades are expected to be materially harder, arguing against extrapolating post-war return assumptions.
“those were probably the best years in terms of growth, living standards, conditions that not only the UK, but effectively the Western world has ever seen.” ▶ 56:28
The next 30 to 40 years will be very difficult as the birth rate falls and life expectancy rises, pushing the dependency ratio up until it stabilizes.
prediction🗣 Charles Goodhartconviction: highdependency ratiodemographicslife expectancy
So what: A multi-decade demographic headwind underpins the structural case for higher taxes, higher inflation and slower growth.
“the next 30 to 40 years are going to be very difficult because as the birth rate goes down and it expectation of life goes up, the dependency ratio increases.” ▶ 55:49

80,000 Hours Podcast 1 episode(s) · 34 insights

What AI insiders say off the record | Jasmine Sun66 min34 insights2026-07-21
▶ Watch on YouTube
Writer Jasmine Sun reports on the off-the-record culture of frontier-AI insiders: a quiet consensus that mass knowledge-work displacement is imminent, techno-determinism and equity-grabbing that funnel scarce talent into a few labs, a niche 'successionist' indifference to human control, and a rising cross-partisan 'AI populist' backlash that she expects to shape the 2026 midterms and 2028 campaigns. She argues idiosyncratic beliefs of a few builders are load-bearing, PR can no longer win the public back without legible economic redistribution, and policy/philanthropy Overton windows are expanding fast.
Insiders' private consensus on displacement and doom · 3Why they build anyway: determinism and equity · 3Successionist-lite: indifference to human control · 2Few builders, load-bearing idiosyncratic beliefs · 4'Doomer' as a status slur · 4AI populism and public backlash · 4Political trajectory into 2026-2028 · 2Why AI PR fails and the insularity gap · 5China contrast · 3Media coverage of AI · 2Opportunity: expanding Overton window · 2
Insiders' private consensus on displacement and doom 3
Across political and ideological lines, frontier-AI-lab insiders share a 'quiet consensus' that mass knowledge-work job displacement will hit within the next few years, falling hardest on young people, even among those who disagree about the long-run outcome.
thesis🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: highAI labsknowledge worklabor market
So what: A near-term labor-disruption view is baseline among the people building the tech, supporting a bearish stance on entry-level white-collar employment and firms exposed to it.
≈ “the vast majority of people I know in the AI industry believe that in the near term, in the next few years, we are going to see mass job displacement” paraphrase
It is 'not an uncommon belief' among frontier-lab researchers to assign roughly a 10% chance that the AI they are building kills everybody, and they keep building anyway.
data_point🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: mediumAI labsexistential risk
So what: The people closest to the technology price meaningful tail risk yet proceed, a signal that competitive/financial incentives override stated risk estimates.
≈ “a 10% chance of killing everybody or something like that — which is also not an uncommon belief from researchers at the labs” paraphrase
Almost every frontier-lab researcher Sun interviewed off the record said they had no idea how a normal, non-elite 17-year-old should prepare for the AI future.
anecdote🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: highAI labseducationlabor market
So what: The builders' inability to chart a path for median workers underscores the depth of uncertainty about post-AGI labor demand.
≈ “I have no idea what I’d tell that 17-year-old. It’s a really scary time. I don’t think there’s going to be a lot of jobs for them left.” paraphrase
Why they build anyway: determinism and equity 3
Techno-determinism drives lab researchers: they reason that if their lab (e.g. Anthropic) exits the race, OpenAI, xAI and Google will keep building anyway, so it is better to stay in and shape the outcome.
framework🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: highAnthropicOpenAIxAIGoogle
So what: The 'if I don't, they will' logic means no single lab will unilaterally slow down, keeping the capabilities race intact for investors and competitors.
≈ “there is a sense of, “If I don’t do it, they’re going to do it.”” paraphrase
Many insiders who expect huge disruption are consciously buying equity/a 'stake' in frontier AI as personal insurance against ending up in a future underclass.
causal🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: mediumOpenAIAI labsequity
So what: Reveals that insider talent flows are partly a hedge trade on AI winners, reinforcing concentration of upside among a small group.
≈ “I might as well get my bag and make sure that I have a stake in the future.” paraphrase
The belief that you need literal equity in frontier AI to have economic leverage is draining talent from the independent, non-corporate ecosystem into a few labs, with Berkeley CS PhD students graduating early and curating dissertations to get lab jobs.
causal🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: highAI labsUC Berkeleyacademia
So what: Talent concentration into a handful of labs starves independent research, policy and journalism, raising the strategic value of the few firms that capture it.
≈ “half of her cohort has decided to graduate early... in order to get jobs at AI labs. And they’ve actually curated their PhD dissertations for research that would be most likely to get them a job at an AI lab.” paraphrase
Successionist-lite: indifference to human control 2
A niche but influential minority in the AI community express a 'successionist-lite' preference for a world where superintelligent AIs, rather than humans, make most societal decisions.
contrarian🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: mediumAI communitysuperintelligence
So what: Even a small faction indifferent to human control makes safety consensus and policy coordination harder, a governance risk investors in the sector should track.
≈ “frankly, I’m more worried about what if the monkeys are still running the show?” paraphrase
At least one very influential AI-scene figure told Sun he cares more about what AIs do with each other in 1,000 years than what happens to humans next year.
anecdote🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: mediumAI community
So what: Illustrates how far some elite worldviews diverge from human-welfare framing, complicating any assumption that lab leaders share the public's priorities.
≈ “I’m a lot more interested in what the AIs are going to do with each other 1,000 years from now than I am in what’s going to happen to humans next year.” paraphrase
Few builders, load-bearing idiosyncratic beliefs 4
Because frontier-AI talent is so scarce (commanding ~$100M pay packages), the idiosyncratic personal beliefs of a few researchers are 'load bearing' for major decisions about what gets built, who it is sold to, and how it diffuses.
framework🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: highAI labstalent
So what: With so few decisive actors, understanding key individuals' philosophies is predictive of firm behavior, useful for anticipating product, sales and safety choices.
≈ “personal-idiosyncratic belief systems can be quite load bearing for really big decisions” paraphrase
Anthropic's existence is a bet that a single lab with the best models can make principled choices from Dario Amodei's beliefs, e.g. refusing to sell Mythos for mass surveillance or autonomous robots, and declining Chinese-government customers despite market incentives.
thesis🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: highAnthropicDario AmodeiMythosChina
So what: Anthropic may forgo revenue segments (surveillance, defense-adjacent robotics, China) on principle, a differentiator that shapes its addressable market versus rivals.
≈ “we don’t want Mythos to be used for mass surveillance or for autonomous robots right now. We actually don’t want to sell to Chinese companies and the Chinese government” paraphrase
DeepMind, arguably the first serious AGI lab, could not raise money until Luke Nosek of Founders Fund backed it on personal conviction, while Peter Thiel and other VCs called funding a team with no business plan crazy.
anecdote🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: mediumDeepMindFounders FundLuke NosekPeter ThielDemis Hassabis
So what: History shows frontier-AI bets hinged on a single conviction investor, a reminder that today's category-defining firms often looked un-fundable at inception.
≈ “no venture capitalist was willing to back a bunch of these weirdo scientists who insisted that you could build superintelligence in 2010” paraphrase
The early internet's enduring open, cross-border architecture was set by the personal convictions of builders like Vint Cerf that it should run on open protocols.
anecdote🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: mediuminternetVint CerfDARPAARPANET
So what: Founder values become durable architectural lock-in, so the norms frontier-AI leaders set now may persist for decades.
'Doomer' as a status slur 4
In classic VC/startup Silicon Valley, 'doomer' has become the lowest-status label you can wear, weaponized by accelerationists to paint anyone favoring slowing or regulating AI as anti-progress and low-agency.
framework🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: highSilicon Valleyeffective accelerationismVC
So what: Status dynamics steer impressionable young talent away from safety and toward capabilities, shaping the labor supply that determines who wins.
≈ “the word ‘doomer’ has become the lowest status label that you can wear in Silicon Valley” paraphrase
The tech right, led by figures like Marc Andreessen and David Sacks, has effectively tied 'doomerism' and AI safety to 'woke' identity politics and censorship to discredit regulation on the American right.
causal🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: highMarc AndreessenDavid SacksAI safetyRepublican Party
So what: Framing safety as 'woke' lowers the odds of federal AI regulation from a Republican-leaning government, a tailwind for capability-focused firms.
≈ “to be interested in AI safety is the same as being a ‘woke regulator’ who wants Facebook to censor all non-liberal content” paraphrase
A rising 'vice signaling' culture makes edgy anti-restraint startups cool, e.g. Mechanize ('automate and replace all human labour') and Cluely ('cheat on everything').
data_point🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: mediumMechanizeCluelySilicon Valley
So what: Provocation-as-branding is a fundable go-to-market posture now, signaling how far the cultural center has shifted from caution.
Technical AI-safety work retains status, funding and professional ecosystems (with pioneers like Geoff Hinton raising the alarm), but independent journalism, policy and academic AI-safety roles are under-supported and stigmatized as 'shill' or 'EA' work.
thesis🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: highGeoff HintonAI safetyjournalismeffective altruism
So what: The independent scrutiny ecosystem is being defunded and delegitimized, reducing external checks on the labs.
AI populism and public backlash 4
A new cross-issue 'AI populist' coalition (labour unions, social conservatives, environmentalists, states-rights advocates) is forming in Washington to push measures like data-centre moratoriums and to fight federal preemption.
thesis🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: highWashington DClabor unionsdata centersfederal preemption
So what: Bipartisan anti-AI coalitions raise regulatory and permitting risk for data-centre build-outs and the compute supply chain.
AI populists' opposition is driven less by AI's technical risks than by a pre-existing backlash against billionaires and corporate concentration of power that hurts ordinary workers.
framework🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: highAI populismcorporate power
So what: Because the grievance is distributional, better products alone will not defuse it, changing what it takes to earn a social license to operate.
≈ “a small number of wealthy and powerful corporate elites are concentrating control in society at the cost of everybody else” paraphrase
AI is currently propping up US GDP growth, with the St. Louis Fed cited as attributing 39% of 2025 growth to data centres and AI.
data_point🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: highSt. Louis FedUS GDPdata centers
So what: Heavy macro dependence on AI/data-centre capex means a slowdown or bubble deflation would drag headline growth, a key macro fragility.
≈ “St. Louis Fed said that 39% of growth in 2025 came from data centres and AI” paraphrase
With no mediating institutions (white-collar workers are largely un-unionized) to bargain over AI adoption, public anger is finding diffuse outlets like cancellation witch-hunts and stochastic political violence, including attacks on Sam Altman's house.
causal🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: highSam Altmanlabor unionswhite-collar workers
So what: Absent bargaining channels, backlash becomes volatile and unpredictable, a reputational and physical-security risk premium for AI leaders.
“public sentiment will find an outlet” ▶ 39:59
Political trajectory into 2026-2028 2
Sun expects AI populism and AI's public salience to rise sharply through the 2026 midterms and 2028 primaries, with candidates like Mark Kelly, Ro Khanna and Josh Hawley already launching bold AI action plans.
prediction🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: highMark KellyRo KhannaJosh Hawley2028 election
So what: AI regulation is set to become a marquee campaign issue on both left and right, raising policy-risk uncertainty for the sector into 2028.
AI has become the new all-purpose political justification ('because AI'), with politicians citing it to advance agendas they already held, e.g. Elizabeth Warren on taxing companies or social conservatives on limiting kids' tech access.
causal🗣 Jasmine Sunconviction: highElizabeth WarrenUS Congress
So what: Expect AI-labeled legislation that is really about other goals, making the regulatory agenda broad, unfocused and hard to predict.
≈ “For Elizabeth Warren it’s, “I want to tax the companies and we should do it because of AI.”” paraphrase
Why AI PR fails and the insularity gap 5
