“it limits op return. Uh there's still ways to put a lot of non-monetary data into the blockchain especially uh through the more encryption route” ▶ 48:17
Alden values Bitcoin's strong resistance to change as central to its sound-money property and is wary of rushing any soft fork like BIP 110, noting prior aggressive attempts to make Bitcoin more expressive hit a wall.
framework🗣 Lyn Aldenconviction: highBitcoinBIP 110
So what: Her change-resistance framework predicts protocol conservatism will keep winning; relevant to handicapping any consensus-change bet.
“if Bitcoin was easy to change um you know that that kind of makes its sound money aspect uh reduced” ▶ 49:32
On the quantum threat, Alden treats Satoshi's coins (roughly 5% of supply) plus lost coins as a volatility event rather than existential, and sees the bigger risk in Bitcoin's slow ability to migrate coins to new quantum-resistant address types given limited throughput.
framework🗣 Lyn Aldenconviction: mediumBitcoinquantum computingSatoshi
So what: Separates a manageable vol risk from the real bottleneck—migration speed—implying change should be enacted earlier than later.
“I view Satoshi's coins as less of a concern than most because we're talking about 5% of coins roughly speaking... I view that as a volatility event more so than existential” ▶ 51:13
Alden argues the worst response to quantum risk is overestimating the threat and rushing an immature signature scheme, since quantum-resilient signatures are still evolving and hardware progress typically lags theory; she doesn't view it as a near-term (5-10 year) risk.
contrarian🗣 Lyn Aldenconviction: mediumBitcoinquantum computing
So what: Pushes back against quantum bulls' urgency; signals a longer runway before quantum meaningfully threatens Bitcoin.
“putting in one that is maybe not up to par in a number of years just just to get one in there uh could be worse could be worse than doing nothing at all”

Latent Space 1 episode(s) · 30 insights

Causal Models Need Causal Data - Xaira’s X-Cell model (Bo Wang & Ci Chu)90 min30 insights2026-07-21
▶ Watch on YouTube
Xaira Therapeutics' Bo Wang and Xi (Ci) Chu explain X-Cell, a 4.9B-parameter diffusion-language 'virtual cell' model trained on in-house genome-wide perturb-seq causal data. Core thesis: descriptive/observational single-cell data is fundamentally underpowered to learn causality, so the moat is generating high-quality causal data at scale, which lets X-Cell beat linear baselines and generalize to unseen cell types, activated T cells, and primary human cells.
Xaira's three-platform, end-to-end AI drug strategy · 4Causal data over descriptive data (the core thesis) · 4Industrializing perturb-seq data generation (the wet-lab moat) · 3X-Cell diffusion architecture and biological priors · 5Out-of-context generalization results (the proof points) · 5Platform roadmap and multimodal expansion · 4Wishlist: protein and temporal measurement bottlenecks · 2Industry vs academia and open science · 3
Xaira's three-platform, end-to-end AI drug strategy 4
Xaira Therapeutics is built around three integrated AI platforms: protein design (spun out of co-founder David Baker's UW group), a virtual-cell/foundation-model-of-biology platform (X-Cell), and a nascent patient-representation model to predict which patients respond to which therapeutics.
framework🗣 Xi Chu (SVP, AI-enabled discovery, Xaira)conviction: highXaira TherapeuticsDavid BakerUniversity of WashingtonX-Cellprotein design
So what: Maps where an AI-native biotech is deploying capital; the patient-representation layer is the still-unbuilt piece and the eventual clinical value driver to watch.
“There are three main AI platforms that we're building here.” ▶ 3:34
Xaira positions itself as one of very few AI-native drug-discovery companies working end-to-end across the entire pipeline (target ID, protein design, small molecules, through phase 1-3 trials), backed by a roughly $1 billion round.
thesis🗣 Bo Wang (SVP & Head of Biomedical AI, Xaira)conviction: highXaira Therapeutics
So what: Frames Xaira as a full-stack platform bet rather than a point solution; the scale of funding underwrites the wet-lab data generation that is its differentiator.
“what sets Xaira different is not just the 1 billion around” ▶ 4:51
Xaira's stated differentiator is connecting its three AI models to each other rather than running them independently — e.g. designing virtual-cell models to surface targets that are easier for the protein-engineering models, and linking cellular causal models to patient representations for clinical utility.
framework🗣 Bo Wangconviction: highXaira Therapeuticsvirtual cellpatient representation
So what: The integration thesis is the claimed edge over single-model competitors; watch whether cross-model handoffs actually yield differentiated targets.
≈ “we always aim to connect three AI models together instead of letting them work individually by their own” paraphrase
The overarching goal is to compress drug-discovery cycle time and raise success rates, converting what has been 'artisanal trial and error' into an engineering discipline.
thesis🗣 Xi Chuconviction: mediumXaira Therapeuticsdrug discovery
So what: Standard AI-bio ambition; the credible near-term proof point is the perturbation model, not yet clinical timelines.
≈ “transform what is used to be artisanal trial and error in the past into more and more into an engineering discipline” paraphrase
Causal data over descriptive data (the core thesis) 4
The binding constraint in biology AI is data, not algorithms: protein design advanced fastest because of 70+ years of high-quality curated structural data (PDB) plus genome sequence data, while virtual-cell and clinical domains lack comparably massive high-quality data.
causal🗣 Xi Chuconviction: highPDBAlphaFoldprotein designvirtual cell
So what: If the bottleneck is data, the moat accrues to whoever industrializes high-quality data generation, not to model architecture alone.
“it's mainly a data limitation issue” ▶ 9:59
Descriptive/observational single-cell datasets (e.g. Cell x Gene, 33M+ cells of mostly healthy donors) are fundamentally underpowered to learn causality because correlation data can be fit by N possible causal structures, which is why models trained on them do not yet outperform linear baselines on perturbation/counterfactual tasks.
contrarian🗣 Xi Chuconviction: highCell x GenescGPTlinear modelscounterfactual
So what: Directly undercuts the value of observational-data foundation models; the whole episode's title thesis and a screen for which AI-bio approaches are worth funding.
≈ “these models that are trained on descriptive data do not yet outperform linear models on causal tasks, perturbational tasks, or what we call counterfactual tasks” paraphrase
Xaira's answer is generating causal data at scale via perturb-seq (pooled CRISPR-Cas9 perturbation combined with single-cell RNA-seq) to build 2D perturbation-by-gene-expression datasets, explicitly analogous to how PDB structural data powered AlphaFold.
framework🗣 Xi Chuconviction: highperturb-seqCRISPR-Cas9single-cell RNA-seqPDBAlphaFold
So what: Identifies perturb-seq scale as the concrete data asset underpinning the model; the '2D dataset like PDB' framing is the pitch for why this could unlock an AlphaFold-style step change.
“not that different than the type of PDB data that trained alphaphone models” ▶ 27:26
Patient-level data is the hardest input to obtain — requiring high-quality patient samples matched to clinical annotation to bridge molecular data to drug response — and Xaira is only beginning to enter this area.
thesis🗣 Xi Chuconviction: mediumpatient representationclinical data
So what: Flags the patient-representation platform as early and data-constrained; near-term clinical read-throughs should be discounted accordingly.
≈ “getting access to high quality patient samples is difficult in itself” paraphrase
Industrializing perturb-seq data generation (the wet-lab moat) 3
Genome-wide perturb-seq requires handling tens to hundreds of millions of cells across a ~14-hour day, whereas academic protocols were built for fresh cells over 1-2 hours; Xaira industrialized the workflow with chemical fixation to lock cell state up front and run time-shifted, batch-effect-free operations.
data_point🗣 Xi Chuconviction: highperturb-seqchemical fixationbatch effect
So what: This operational engineering — not the algorithm — is the hard-to-replicate moat; data quality gates directly determine model quality.
≈ “we lock the state of the cells in at the beginning of this experiment” paraphrase
Xaira released the Pisces dataset of 16 cell types across 25 million (post-QC) cells with genome-wide perturbation — described as the world's largest perturb-seq release — following an earlier dataset ('XLS Orion') from two cell lines (a colorectal cancer line and a hematopoietic line).
data_point🗣 Xi Chu / Bo Wangconviction: highPisces datasetXLS Orionperturb-seq
So what: Concrete, open-released data asset; its scale and 16-context breadth are the empirical basis for X-Cell's generalization claims.
“16 different cell types across 25 million cells” ▶ 50:59
Xaira optimizes for information content ('bits per dollar') and biological-context diversity over raw cell/read counts — e.g. a pan-differentiation experiment turning iPSCs into 10 cell types in one experiment with genome-scale perturbation, a 'library-on-library' design.
framework🗣 Xi Chuconviction: highiPSCgenome-scale perturbationbits per dollar
So what: A data-strategy heuristic distinct from brute-force scaling; context diversity is what enables out-of-distribution generalization.
“It's about bits per dollar in information content.” ▶ 33:11
X-Cell diffusion architecture and biological priors 5
X-Cell is Xaira's first virtual-cell model, a roughly 4.9-billion-parameter diffusion language model (not autoregressive) that predicts cellular response to genetic perturbations and is extensible to drug/chemical perturbations.
data_point🗣 Bo Wangconviction: highX-Celldiffusion language modelgenetic perturbation
So what: Anchors the technical bet: a generative diffusion decoder over full transcriptomes rather than a representation-only encoder.
“four point nine billion” ▶ 53:33
Bo Wang's Toronto lab published scGPT (an autoregressive GPT-like single-cell foundation model) about three years ago, ~4 months after ChatGPT, and X-Cell deliberately switches from autoregressive to diffusion because diffusion avoids assuming an arbitrary gene order and fits sparse single-cell RNA-seq better.
framework🗣 Bo Wangconviction: highscGPTChatGPTdiffusion language modelautoregressive
So what: Architectural rationale investors can track: diffusion-over-autoregressive is a testable design choice with reported gains on hard generalization tasks.
“you can think of autoregressive training as typing... But diffusion language model, you can treat it as editing” ▶ 42:55
X-Cell incorporates five types of biological priors — literature/GenePT-style LLM embeddings, protein-protein interaction networks, DepMap cancer essential-gene data, morphology, and cell-type embeddings — fused via cross-attention, and the learned prior weights add interpretability about which knowledge matters per cell type.
framework🗣 Bo Wangconviction: mediumX-CellGenePTPPI networksDepMapcross-attention
So what: The multi-prior conditioning is a differentiator versus single-prior models, but its marginal value is cell-type dependent (see contribution ranking).
≈ “Excel, to my knowledge, is one of the first models in trying to incorporate extremely diverse sets of biological priors” paraphrase
Bo Wang ranks the drivers of X-Cell's performance as, in order: quality and scale of the data first, then architecture (autoregressive-to-diffusion switch), then prior knowledge, which is only marginally and condition-specifically helpful.
data_point🗣 Bo Wangconviction: highX-Celldiffusionbiological priors
So what: An explicit ablation ordering: reinforces that the durable edge is proprietary data, not the architectural or prior cleverness competitors can copy.
≈ “my order would be the quality among scale of the data sets and then the architecture and then the prior knowledge” paraphrase
Priors are baked into X-Cell as learnable parameters and are not required at inference, but supplying them at inference offers additional in-context-learning-style steering of predictions.
framework🗣 Bo Wangconviction: mediumX-Cellin-context learningbiological priors
So what: Means the model is deployable without curated priors, lowering usage friction while leaving an upside lever for power users.
Out-of-context generalization results (the proof points) 5
Trained only on resting (non-activated) regulatory T cells, X-Cell accurately predicted perturbation effects in activated T cells it had never seen — including known TCR-complex biology and putative novel inactivators — beating a linear baseline that simply transposes resting-state perturbation deltas onto the active state.
data_point🗣 Xi Chuconviction: highX-CellT cellsTCR complexlinear baseline
So what: The headline validation that a virtual-cell model can predict out-of-context biology, the capability that would justify replacing some wet-lab screens.
≈ “critically, X-Cell has not seen active cell T cells. And it's able to make accurate prediction” paraphrase
In the 10-cell-type iPSC differentiation experiment, X-Cell was tested on a held-out cell type it never trained on and predicted accurately across thousands of genes and thousands of perturbations.
data_point🗣 Xi Chuconviction: highX-CelliPSCheld-out cell type
So what: Second independent generalization demonstration; strengthens the case that the model captures transferable causal structure, not memorized context.
≈ “The model made very good prediction across thousands of genes, thousands of perturbations in that unseen cell type.” paraphrase
Trained on a single immortalized T-cell line, X-Cell generalized to primary T cells harvested from multiple human donors, matching a recently published primary-T-cell perturb-seq from Alex Marson's lab.
data_point🗣 Xi Chuconviction: highX-Cellprimary T cellsAlex Marson labcell lines
So what: Validates the whole strategy of training on cheap, scalable immortalized cells to predict expensive, hard-to-screen real human cells.
≈ “the Excel trained on just one T cell cell line is able to make predictions across multiple donors from primary T cell experiments” paraphrase
The perturbation-prediction field lacks consistent benchmarks; common MAE metrics on small Replogle datasets are unreliable because the average cell profile can achieve lower MAE than even technical replicates, so Xaira emphasizes Pearson-delta (similarity of predicted vs ground-truth expression changes), which is hard to game.
contrarian🗣 Bo Wang / Xi Chuconviction: highMAEReplogle datasetPearson deltatechnical replicates
So what: A caution for evaluating any virtual-cell claims: benchmark choice can make weak models look strong, so scrutinize whether competitors report change-based metrics.
≈ “the average profile of cells has lower MAEs even than technical replicates, which are considered off-ground truth” paraphrase
Linear models fail entirely to extend to unseen cell types, which is precisely where nonlinear foundation models trained on causal data are expected to win.
causal🗣 Bo Wangconviction: highlinear modelsfoundation modelsgeneralization
So what: Defines the specific regime (out-of-distribution generalization) where the expensive model earns its keep versus a cheap baseline.
≈ “linear model totally failed to extend to unseen cell types” paraphrase
Platform roadmap and multimodal expansion 4
Xaira is expanding its data platform along three axes: multimodal readouts beyond transcriptomics, combinatorial/pathway perturbations beyond single-gene knockdowns, and more complex systems (primary cells, organoids, in-vivo perturbation) beyond cell-line monocultures.
framework🗣 Xi Chuconviction: highXaira Therapeuticsorganoidsin vivo perturbationmultimodal
So what: Signals the next data-generation investments and where model capability (and competitive lead) will extend next.
“we're expanding the platform along all of those axes” ▶ 1:09:04
Although trained on single-gene perturbations, X-Cell can already predict combinatorial perturbations in silico by perturbing two gene tokens simultaneously, useful for hypothesis generation despite limited accuracy without combinatorial training data.
prediction🗣 Bo Wangconviction: mediumX-Cellcombinatorial perturbationPPI networks
So what: Extends the model's utility to combination-effect screening in silico, a valuable capability for combination-therapy discovery even before dedicated data exists.
≈ “you can actually predict combinatorial perturbations just on the model in only silico” paraphrase
Bo Wang predicts future virtual-cell models will integrate multiple modalities — spatial single-cell RNA-seq, ATAC-seq, proteomics, and H&E imaging — and the next version of X-Cell will infer spatial-aware representations that its current release lacks.
prediction🗣 Bo Wangconviction: mediumX-Cellspatial transcriptomicsATAC-seqH&E imagingscGPT spatial
So what: Roadmap signal; spatial context is deemed crucial for cancer/immuno-oncology applications where cell-cell interactions drive clinical response.
≈ “the next version of Excel will be able to infer the spatial-aware representations for different cells” paraphrase
The ultimate vision is to transfer causal prediction 'rightward' along biology's multi-scale spectrum (cell lines to tissues to animals to human patients) to eventually select which patients respond to which drugs — motivated by 90% of diseases having no cure and phase-3 trial success rates as low as 5-10%.
thesis🗣 Bo Wangconviction: highvirtual cellclinical trialspatient selection
So what: Frames the total addressable prize (patient-response prediction) and quantifies the failure rate the model aims to attack; a long-dated bet, not near-term.
“the success rate of phase three trials is as low as five to 10%” ▶ 1:00:38
Wishlist: protein and temporal measurement bottlenecks 2
Xi Chu's top wished-for breakthrough is high-throughput single-cell (or spatial) protein measurement at genomics scale — capturing abundance, post-translational modifications, localization, and conformational states — since RNA only foreshadows the proteins that are the functional units of the cell.
prediction🗣 Xi Chuconviction: highprotein sequencingproteomicssingle cell
So what: Points to where the next high-value data-generation technology (and investment opportunity) lies; whoever cracks scalable single-cell proteomics unlocks the next model generation.
≈ “if I can do that, I would incorporate that technology in a heart beat” paraphrase
Bo Wang's top wished-for breakthrough is sequencing technology that can measure the same cells at multiple time points without killing them, to capture temporal dynamics — arguing current virtual-cell models are entirely static and that longitudinal single-cell measurement would be the 'real' virtual cell.
prediction🗣 Bo Wangconviction: highsequencing technologytemporal dynamicsvirtual cell
So what: Flags a hard measurement limit (sequencing kills the cell) whose solution would redefine the field; a north-star for tool-company innovation.
“To me, that is a real virtual cell.” ▶ 1:27:26
Industry vs academia and open science 3
Major biology-AI breakthroughs increasingly come from industry (e.g. AlphaFold) because of GPU/resource concentration, faculty are leaving universities for industry resources, and the first question students now ask a prospective advisor is 'how many GPUs do you have?'
causal🗣 Bo Wangconviction: highAlphaFoldacademiaGPUs
So what: Structural signal that compute-intensive bio-AI value is migrating to well-funded private labs like Xaira, favoring industry over academic incumbents.
≈ “The first question they often ask is, How many GPUs do you have?” paraphrase
Xaira is committed to open science, open-sourcing its datasets and model weights (available on Bo Wang's lab GitHub), reasoning that virtual cell is too early a field to benefit from withholding, mirroring how open PDB and AlphaFold accelerated protein science.
thesis🗣 Bo Wangconviction: highXaira Therapeuticsopen sciencePDBAlphaFoldscGPT
So what: The open-release strategy seeds ecosystem standards and adoption (scGPT is already widely used in pharma), a distribution/influence play rather than IP hoarding.
≈ “it doesn't help to withhold certain data sets or certain models because it's so early” paraphrase
In the agentic-AI era coding is commoditized ('everybody can code') so the scarce differentiator is research taste to avoid 'burning tokens without purpose,' and the researcher's time has shifted from mostly coding to mostly debugging AI-generated code.
framework🗣 Bo Wangconviction: mediumagentic AIresearch taste
So what: A workflow read on how AI reshapes R&D productivity: judgment and validation, not code volume, become the scarce inputs — relevant to how labs and startups staff.
≈ “It's more important to have a right taste about your project so that you don't just burn tokens without purpose.” paraphrase

Anthony Pompliano 1 episode(s) · 29 insights

The Investor Betting BILLIONS on AI52 min29 insights2026-07-20
▶ Watch on YouTube
Union Square Ventures GP Nick Grossman lays out USV's 'Rebel Alliance' thesis: AI is too big to be monopolized by two or three frontier labs, so durable non-lab companies will emerge at the application, middleware, model and energy layers, with value increasingly driven by multi-agent orchestration and multi-model routing. He and host Anthony Pompliano dig into the 'AI kill zone', agentic investing, data monetization, and the geopolitical and social trade-offs of the buildout.
Rebel Alliance thesis vs lab monopoly · 5AI kill zone and specialization · 3Multi-model routing and token cost · 3Multi-agent architecture in production · 3Agentic investing and autonomous capital · 6Vertical integration and data monetization · 3Model philosophy and geopolitics · 3Societal impact and human augmentation · 3
Rebel Alliance thesis vs lab monopoly 5
USV's 'Rebel Alliance' thesis holds that AI is too large an opportunity to be dominated by two or three companies and is instead forming a massive ecosystem of consumer-facing agents (AI doctors, financial advisors) and infrastructure agents embedded under the hood of every product.
thesis🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: highUnion Square VenturesRebel AllianceAI agents
So what: Frames USV's investing posture against the consensus bet that AI value collapses into a few frontier labs; look for durable non-lab winners at multiple layers.
“the Rebel Alliance is our view that uh AI is too big an opportunity to be dominated by one or two or three companies and that it's actually forming into a massive ecosystem” ▶ 0:50
Grossman concedes real network effects exist in AI model training that have compounded advantage to the big labs, but argues the countervailing forces are strong competition among frontier models, open-source models improving in lockstep, and data value accruing to the application layer rather than only the model layer.
framework🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: highfrontier modelsopen source modelsapplication layer
So what: Identifies where non-lab value can accrue; the bet that data advantage disperses across layers is the crux of whether app-layer startups are investable.
“there's going to be an unassalable gravity into the big AI companies” ▶ 2:47
Grossman rejects Peter Thiel's 'competition is for losers' monopoly framing as the end state for AI, arguing the space will look more like the fabric of computing and apps than a single collapse into one or two apps like ChatGPT or Claude.
contrarian🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: highPeter ThielChatGPTClaude
So what: A directional disagreement with the dominant Silicon Valley monopoly playbook; underpins a diversified multi-layer venture strategy.
“if you talk like Peter Teal he'd be like competitions for losers you know the I want to find a monopoly” ▶ 2:06
Grossman expects that within five years there will be multiple massive public AI companies in health, legal, finance and industrials that are not the big labs, plus a large category of 'middleware' companies handling memory, harnesses, orchestration and payments in the agentic stack.
prediction🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: mediummiddlewareagentic stackhealth AIlegal AIfinance AI
So what: Concrete five-year map of where new public-market winners could emerge outside the frontier labs; middleware is called out as 'just getting going'.
“Five years from now, I think we will see a number of massive public companies in the um in the in the consumer AI or the business AI space, whether that's health, legal, finance that aren't the big labs” ▶ 36:03
USV also invests at the energy layer as a cross-cutting input to all AI, and has been doing so for six or seven years, viewing the big labs and their models as the infrastructure of the 'next internet' to be built around.
thesis🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: highenergyUnion Square VenturesAI infrastructure
So what: Signals energy as a durable AI-adjacent investment vector; USV positions itself around rather than against the labs.
“we're also investing at the energy layer um because energy is an input you know across the board for for all AI” ▶ 43:20
AI kill zone and specialization 3
Grossman calls the competitive threat from frontier labs the 'AI kill zone' and argues companies closest to the models' core skills — language, media, law, code, natively digital domains — are the most exposed, while specialization and real-world connectivity build defensibility even inside those areas.
framework🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: mediumAI kill zonelawcodemedia
So what: A map of which AI startups are most vulnerable to lab encroachment versus which can defend via domain depth; a screening lens for early-stage risk.
“we refer to it as the kill zone, the AI kill zone” ▶ 4:42
Grossman's bet is that frontier labs, however capable, cannot be best at every use case, so domain expertise, defensibility and connectivity to the real world let specialized companies pull products in the right direction against general-purpose lab offerings.
thesis🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: highfrontier labsspecialized applications
So what: Core rationale for backing vertical AI apps over horizontal lab products; expertise at the edges is the moat.
“the idea of being the best at every use case is is too much too much to chew” ▶ 5:42
Pompliano observes that when OpenAI launched a product competing with his AI CFO Sylvia, it lacked core investor-facing functionality despite being exactly what an engineer would build from available connectivity tools like Plaid and SnapTrade, illustrating that vertical AI success depends on solving customer-pain problems, not just engineering ones.
anecdote🗣 Anthony Pompliano, host and builder of Sylviaconviction: mediumOpenAISylviaPlaidSnapTrade
So what: Real operator evidence that lab entrants can miss domain-specific needs, supporting the specialization defensibility thesis.
Multi-model routing and token cost 3
Building a robot that patrols a factory requires general reasoning, spatial reasoning and local mobility across cloud and on-device models, leading Grossman to conclude the world is going to be 'multimodel' with most use cases combining general-purpose and specialized models.
framework🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: highroboticsmultimodelspecialized models
So what: Undercuts a single-model-wins narrative and supports orchestration/routing tooling as an investable layer.
“the world is going to be multimodel and most use cases are going to be multimodel a combination of general purpose and specialized” ▶ 8:31
Pompliano says his company was one of the fastest-growing Claude customers by percentage and was spending more on compute than a large public company, prompting an internal task force to drive token costs down as CEOs increasingly flag compute cost as too expensive.
data_point🗣 Anthony Pompliano, hostconviction: highClaudecompute costtokens
So what: Signals that AI inference spend is becoming a board-level cost line, driving demand for cost-optimization and routing solutions.
“you're spending more than us like a really big public company and I was like oh that's a problem” ▶ 9:00
Grossman says intelligent model routing optimizes both cost and quality, citing a report where an open-source coding harness running on a mix of models outperformed Claude Code and Opus, and predicting far more sophisticated agent-and-model orchestration as workloads move from prototype to production.
data_point🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: highmodel routingopen source harnessClaude CodeOpus
So what: Reframes routing as a performance lever, not just cost savings; orchestration/harness startups become more valuable as production deployment scales.
“when you can intelligently use a mix of models. You can not only optimize your cost, you can improve your performance uh by using the best model for the best task” ▶ 10:25
Multi-agent architecture in production 3
USV built its own internal VC platform over nine months in which every deal, company, idea and person has an attached agent, orchestrated in software so thousands of agents operate under the hood rather than a single chatbot.
anecdote🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: highUnion Square Venturesmulti-agent systemsCRM
So what: A concrete production example of the multi-agent future; demonstrates internal tooling built cheaply and fast, a template for enterprise agent adoption.
“every uh deal, company, idea, uh potentially person in our platform has an agent attached to it and they're all orchestrated through software” ▶ 11:40
In USV's system a main 'deal analyst' agent lives in email and messaging like a teammate, while sub-agents dedicated to each deal run overnight — listening to Twitter, web searching, reading notes — and push their work into a shared context memory layer for other agents to use.
framework🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: highdeal analyst agentsub-agentsshared context memory
So what: Illustrates the emerging architecture (orchestrator + specialized sub-agents + shared memory) that middleware startups will need to serve.
“that agent wakes up and goes out and and listens to Twitter and listen and does web searching” ▶ 12:41
Grossman argues AI is shifting from a chat-based call-and-response paradigm to a trigger-based proactive paradigm, where agents fire off timers or events (a meeting note, an inbound email) and can execute longer-running, more autonomous tasks as they grow more capable.
prediction🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: hightrigger-based agentscoding agentsproactive AI
So what: A directional shift in product design; watch for tooling that supports event-driven, long-running agent workflows.
“we're moving from um a kind of a chatbased call response paradigm to more of a proact to like a trigger-based paradigm” ▶ 14:49
Agentic investing and autonomous capital 6
Grossman argues venture investing is too human and episodic to fully automate, but follow-on dollars that fill the edges of rounds — the last few hundred thousand or million — could be deployed by a 'pool of capital agent' following parameters and acting autonomously, and a USV portfolio company is pursuing this.
prediction🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: mediumventure capitalfollow-on capitalagentic VC
So what: Points to a near-term wedge for automated capital in venture — parameter-driven follow-on allocation triggered by tier-one lead signals.
“one area where I could totally see more automated uh capital coming into venture deals is like filling in the edges of venture rounds” ▶ 19:19
Grossman says the first fleet of investing agents traded crypto because those markets are programmatic and accessible, and that agents with wallets making payments as autonomous financial actors will proliferate fastest in the most open, API- or crypto-rail-accessible markets.
thesis🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: mediumcryptoautonomous agentsagent walletsprediction markets
So what: Frames crypto and prediction markets as the natural first venue for autonomous agent trading — a watch area for agent-native financial infrastructure.
“the places where that's going to be the easiest are the markets that are the most open and accessible either via API or because they're, you know, on crypto rails” ▶ 18:02
Both Grossman and Pompliano report that naive prompting to pick a winning stock has produced few success stories, while the working cases combine a human's original idea or industry view with AI to find and express it — mirroring how great investors process complex data into a thesis.
causal🗣 Anthony Pompliano and Nick Grossmanconviction: mediumagentic tradingstock screening
So what: Tempers hype around fully autonomous stock-picking; the durable edge remains human-idea-plus-AI-execution, not pure model prompting.
“really what he's using is again it's kind of his idea that he's then using to find the idea” ▶ 21:09
Grossman knows a team using agents to trade frontier and prediction markets and obscure corners where retail dominates, betting agents can spot patterns before big institutions arrive and arb them out, and says those traders are making some money at early scale.
anecdote🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: lowfrontier marketsprediction marketsagentic trading
So what: Suggests dislocated, retail-heavy markets are where agentic alpha currently exists, before institutional arbitrage compresses it.
“corners of prediction markets and other kind of obscure places where maybe there's some early dislocations” ▶ 22:14
Pompliano relays Jordi Visser's idea that because AI leaders like Jensen Huang give roughly five interviews a week, feeding those transcripts into models to predict beneficiary companies is 'scary accurate,' citing that a Gavin Baker mention of trainium as an H2 trend led a model to pick Marvell, which rose about 100% in 60 days.
anecdote🗣 Anthony Pompliano, host, relaying Jordi Visserconviction: mediumJordi VisserJensen HuangGavin BakertrainiumMarvell
So what: A replicable AI research workflow — mine executive interview transcripts for downstream beneficiaries — with a concrete example of a large move.
“If you take those and you take the transcripts and you put them into the systems and you say ... what are the companies that are going to benefit over the next 12 months” ▶ 23:51
Grossman suggests a lab could develop a frontier model and not sell it as an API or consumer app but internalize it as a trading fund, noting SSI is rumored to have done something like this, and that internalizing superintelligence could prove a better business model than the ones familiar today.
contrarian🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: lowSSIsuperintelligencefrontier models
So what: A non-obvious risk/opportunity: the best models may never reach the market if labs monetize them internally, changing how AI value is captured.
“you could in theory develop a frontier model and not sell it and just turn it into a trading fund” ▶ 26:33
Vertical integration and data monetization 3
Grossman notes vertical integration is running in both directions: application-layer companies with unique data are moving down into training models — citing Revolut training a foundation model and the Robinhood chain — while big labs move up into the application layer.
thesis🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: mediumRevolutRobinhoodfoundation modelsapplication layer
So what: Signals that scaled app companies with proprietary data are a new class of model builders; the model layer is not the labs' exclusive territory.
“revolute getting into the model game was not something I think a lot of people were thinking about before they announced that that's like the opposite direction going down from the application layer” ▶ 27:25
Grossman says early-stage founders using enterprise plans with the best data-retention policies and parallelizing across models focus mostly on capability and user experience, and that structural data-protection worries surface mainly at scale, which is why the concern is loudest from the biggest companies.
causal🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: mediumdata retentionenterprise plansPalantirAlex Karp
So what: Reframes the data-privacy debate as a scale-stage concern; app-layer companies uniquely see a data stack that never reaches the model.
“there's a whole stack of data that never reaches the actual model itself right which application layer companies can can uniquely see and learn from” ▶ 29:23
Grossman argues selling data to the labs works for some categories — UGC companies like Reddit and Stack Overflow, and data-generation firms like Scale AI — but is prone to commoditization, whereas a USV portfolio company using a patented DNA-synthesis chemical process to generate biomodel training data is a more defensible, specialized example.
framework🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: mediumRedditStack OverflowScale AIDNA synthesisbiomodels
So what: A defensibility test for data businesses: patented, specialized data generation beats commodity labeling; USV is broadly less excited about selling data to labs.
“that's an example to me that feels more defensible and like specialized um versus just sort of doing work out in the world” ▶ 33:58
Model philosophy and geopolitics 3
Grossman treats a model's philosophy or personality — beyond performance, cost and data security — as a fourth vector of competition, noting Western models embody capitalism and risk-taking while Eastern models may lean more socialist, so identical capabilities can still yield different weights and answers.
framework🗣 Nick Grossman and Anthony Pomplianoconviction: mediummodel personalityChinaUnited Statesopen source models
So what: An underexplored differentiation axis for model selection, especially in high-stakes domains like finance, therapy and healthcare.
USV portfolio company Cradle runs nuanced model challenges to tease out model personalities and published a report on which models lie the most, finding one model lied most in its first test — the kind of trait not captured by standard software-engineering benchmarks focused on coding ability.
data_point🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: mediumCradlemodel benchmarksGrok
So what: As agents take on non-coding tasks, behavioral/trust benchmarks become a new evaluation category and potential investable niche.
“which models lie to you the most” ▶ 39:35
Grossman expects a clearer picture of the model layer within five years and questions whether it will be just Anthropic plus Chinese open-source models or whether US open models can emerge, noting USV has invested in a project attempting decentralized, open model training.
prediction🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: mediumAnthropicChinese open source modelsdecentralized model training
So what: Flags US open-source and decentralized training as a strategic gap and a USV bet against a two-pole (closed-US / open-China) model landscape.
“is it open anthropic plus some open source Chinese models and that's it or can we develop open models here in the US” ▶ 37:06
Societal impact and human augmentation 3
Grossman says he is equal parts excited and scared about AI, arguing that like the steam engine, automobile and internet, AI will unleash waves of unforeseen second-order implications — data centers as the biggest new energy consumers straining the grid, and large-scale job displacement raising unanswered questions about how humanity shares the gains.
framework🗣 Nick Grossman, GP at Union Square Venturesconviction: highdata centersenergy gridjob displacementautomobileinternet
So what: Grounds the buildout's macro risks — grid strain and labor disruption — as investable and political flashpoints, with an ownership-model question left open.
“I am honestly like equal parts excited and scared” ▶ 47:34
Pompliano argues the underexplored AI opportunity is augmenting the patient or consumer rather than only the professional — recounting that AI correctly caught his wife's doctor being wrong — creating a consumer-protection angle distinct from the job-displacement narrative.
thesis🗣 Anthony Pompliano, hostconviction: mediumhealthcare AIconsumer protectionAI augmentation
So what: Points to consumer-side 'personal superpowers' products (e.g. verify-your-doctor, car-buying advisors) as a distinct AI market beyond replacing professionals.
“there's like a displacement argument, but there's also like a consumer protection argument” ▶ 48:36
Both note Neuralink has reportedly performed 26 brain implants, and frame future neural implants as 'the new steroids' that could give mental-performance advantages and eventually require regulation in competitive settings.
data_point🗣 Anthony Pompliano and Nick Grossmanconviction: lowNeuralinkneural implantshuman augmentation
So what: A speculative but concrete data point on brain-computer-interface adoption and a framing (enhanced-games/regulation) for how augmentation markets could develop.
“Neuralink supposedly has done 26 of them” ▶ 50:10

This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis 1 episode(s) · 29 insights

An AI that watches your every click may be the future of work | E231441 min29 insights2026-07-20
▶ Watch on YouTube
Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of intent-aware endpoint security startup INT (int.ai, ex-Microsoft founders), pitches an on-device AI agent that reasons about user and agent behavior in real time to stop policy violations before they happen; the company just left stealth with a $100M round targeting Global 2000/Fortune 500. A second segment features David Im of Sume Labs, building an orchestration/router layer over multiple video-gen models to one-shot production-ready marketing videos.
Prevention-first endpoint security thesis · 5Embeddings-on-edge architecture · 4Rollout mechanics and the 'work model' · 3Platform expansion beyond security · 3Monitoring, privacy and data ownership · 4Funding, go-to-market and competitive positioning · 5Sume Labs: video-model orchestration · 5
Prevention-first endpoint security thesis 5
INT (int.ai) has built an on-device AI agent for endpoints (laptops, phones) that assesses in real time whether a user is about to violate corporate policy or do something risky, and stops the action before it occurs.
thesis🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highINTint.aiendpoint securityAI agents
So what: A prevention-at-the-endpoint approach is a category shift from reactive detection; watch whether it can displace incumbent EDR budget.
“we wanted to meet them in that moment, look at the work that was taking place and then make an assessment as to whether or not they were going to violate corporate policy or do something they shouldn't and effectively stop that from happening” ▶ 2:54
Dixon argues the security industry has 'largely given up' on prevention and become reactive—waiting for breaches then troubleshooting—and INT's whole thesis is to restore prevention using new reasoning/embedding models at the point of work.
contrarian🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highsecurity industryreasoning modelsprevention
So what: Frames a contrarian positioning against the detection-and-response consensus that dominates security spend.
“we've largely given up uh prevention inside of security, right? We're very reactive. We wait for the bad thing to occur” ▶ 2:16
Most corporate breaches come from well-intentioned employees making mistakes—clicking things, emailing financial data to the wrong person, sharing credentials across chat—not primarily from sophisticated adversaries.
causal🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highinsider riskhuman errorbreaches
So what: If human error is the dominant breach vector, tools that intercept benign mistakes address a larger surface than adversary-focused defenses.
“a lot of the breaches occur because people are wellincclined trying to do their jobs, but they make a mistake” ▶ 2:32
AI adoption creates a new risk surface: non-technical 'citizen developers' pushed by leadership to solve problems with AI can delete system artifacts, pull sensitive context into models, and accidentally leak it outside the company.
causal🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highcitizen developersAI toolsdata leakage
So what: Enterprise AI rollouts expand the attack/error surface, creating demand for behavioral guardrails around AI usage.
“ones that don't have a technical background who are being encouraged by their leadership to you know solve problems with AI” ▶ 4:34
Developers inside enterprises may run ~20 different AI agents simultaneously across roles, multiplying the same leakage and mistake risks at machine scale.
data_point🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: mediumdevelopersAI agentsenterprise
So what: Agent proliferation per employee signals a fast-growing need to monitor agent (not just human) behavior.
“they might have like 20 different agents running to go and perform uh various roles across the enterprise” ▶ 5:02
Embeddings-on-edge architecture 4
INT relies on optimized embedding models rather than heavy reasoning models, letting it make sub-second decisions on CPUs without GPUs at the endpoint—the performance property that makes real-time prevention feasible.
framework🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highembedding modelsCPUGPUvector databases
So what: CPU-only sub-second inference lowers deployment cost and latency, a technical moat for real-time endpoint intervention.
“Embedding models are great because they're very performant. They don't require like GPUs. They can operate on CPUs and when it comes to decision-m they can make decisions in sub-second time frames” ▶ 12:10
INT built a single architecture that runs identically on the endpoint or entirely on the back end, so customers with regulatory constraints can choose where compute happens without a separate product.
framework🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highINTendpointbackendregulatory compliance
So what: One deployable architecture across edge and cloud reduces engineering fragmentation and widens the addressable regulated-customer base.
“it can run completely on the back end or it can run on the endpoint itself but it's the same architecture” ▶ 9:32
The advance that makes INT possible today versus a couple of years ago is embeddings—the ability to translate semantically meaningful words into high-dimensional numeric representations computers can act on.
causal🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highembeddingslarge language modelsdimensionality
So what: Ties the product's timing directly to the LLM/embedding wave—an enabling-technology inflection for behavioral security.
“the advantage that we have in in things like embeddings the ability to take semantic words like things that we understand have meaning and translate them into something that a computer can understand” ▶ 7:36
INT uses published open models plus optional cloud-provider or customer-brought models for reasoning, but controls and self-optimizes its own embedding models to run performantly on either edge or backend.
framework🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: mediumopen modelscloud service provider modelsembedding models
So what: Owning the embedding layer while renting frontier reasoning keeps costs down and controls the core IP.
Rollout mechanics and the 'work model' 3
With just a corporate policy plus a list of sanctioned software, INT can surface policy violations—like unsanctioned AI, remote-access, or other tools—within 'hour one' of deployment.
data_point🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highcorporate policysanctioned softwareunsanctioned AI
So what: Fast time-to-value with minimal configuration lowers the barrier to enterprise pilots.
“if I understand what like the allowed software is I can immediately tell you people that are using other software be it AI remote access or something else that is not sanctioned for them” ▶ 6:50
INT deploys in a baseline/observability mode first, using a roughly two-week burn-in to establish what is normal per user, per department, and per cohort before it starts intervening—avoiding annoying users with premature prompts.
framework🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highbaselinesburn-in perioduser behavior
So what: A learn-then-enforce rollout reduces false-positive friction, a common failure mode for behavioral security tools.
“there's typically a burn-in period where you might have two weeks to establish baselines of what's normal” ▶ 13:31
Dixon frames INT as building an 'organization work model,' analogous to the world models that made self-driving cars predictive instead of overfit to hand-coded rules—shifting security from static rules to anticipatory behavioral understanding.
framework🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highworld modelsself-driving carsorganization work model
So what: The world-model framing hints at a far larger 'work observability' platform ambition beyond security.
“world models were like super advantageous to cars, right? Because prior to a world model existing that encapsulated the environment, we were trying to tell the car like when to stay in the lanes” ▶ 14:52
Platform expansion beyond security 3
Dixon positions the accumulated behavioral context as a 'semantic substrate for security' with broad applicability: closing benign true-positive SOC tickets, adding context to DLP events, surfacing insider risk, and identifying employees who need training.
thesis🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highsemantic substrateSOCDLPinsider risk
So what: A single behavioral data layer that spans SOC, DLP, and insider-risk use cases implies a multi-product land-and-expand roadmap.
“One of the ones that I was playing around with was like the semantic substrate for security” ▶ 16:32
Beyond security there is a productivity angle: understanding what employees do all day lets a company identify mundane, monotonous, or risky work that AI agents could take over, then keep those agents 'on the rails.'
thesis🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: mediumproductivityAI agentsprocess distillation
So what: The dataset becomes a map for automating work—potentially far more valuable than the security use case alone.
“there's a productivity angle to this as well in which if I understand what people are doing throughout the day, then it becomes ripe to figure out what things might agents benefit” ▶ 17:20
Host Alex argues the behavioral dataset INT accumulates would be so valuable that AI labs, private equity, or working groups will try to acquire the company for the data itself.
prediction🗣 host (Alex)conviction: mediumAI labsprivate equityINTdata
So what: Frames the underlying work-behavior data as a strategic asset that could drive acquisition interest.
“I'm terrified that one of the you know AI lab JV FTE private equity working groups are going to try to scoop you up and then take all the data you have” ▶ 18:32
Monitoring, privacy and data ownership 4
INT is deployed entirely within the customer's own cloud boundary and INT itself cannot see the data, because Global 2000 buyers demand to own their data and keep IP from going to third-party supply chains.
thesis🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highdata sovereigntyGlobal 2000SaaSself-hosting
So what: Data-residency-first design is a deliberate wedge into regulated large enterprises wary of sending IP to vendors.
“Ant is deployed within the customer's boundary like we don't even get to see it” ▶ 22:44
INT offers granular collection controls—per-toggle data collection, user and endpoint groups, plus role-based and attribute-based access control—so, e.g., developers can be monitored heavily while legal collects almost nothing and SOC analysts are blocked from seeing investigation screenshots.
framework🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highRBACABACscreenshotsuser groups
So what: Configurable, permissioned collection is the guardrail that lets granular monitoring pass enterprise privacy review.
“for everything that we collect, there's toggles that basically allow the business to turn it on and off” ▶ 23:27
Employee acceptance of granular work monitoring is unsettled—Dixon calls norms 'TBD' and Alex cites Meta's unpopular push to scrutinize what its engineers were doing as a cautionary signal.
anecdote🗣 Brandon Dixon and host (Alex)conviction: lowMetaemployee monitoringcorporate policy
So what: Workforce backlash is the key adoption risk for granular behavioral monitoring products.
Dixon argues corporate policy 'lacks teeth' because it is written in natural language and left to employee interpretation, and INT's role is to turn that policy into an actual enforceable control point at the endpoint.
framework🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highcorporate policycontrol pointnatural language
So what: Reframes the product as the enforcement layer that makes written policy operational—an ROI story for compliance buyers.
“part of the problem with like a corporate policy is that it it lacks teeth. It's only as good as its ability to put it into a control point” ▶ 24:27
Funding, go-to-market and competitive positioning 5
INT emerged from stealth with a $100M round and general availability; Dixon justifies the raise by the cost of building a cross-platform endpoint agent, putting AI at the edge, supporting AWS/Google/Azure self-hosting, and landing large enterprise accounts.
data_point🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: high$100 millionAWSAzureGoogleendpoint market
So what: A $100M seed-scale raise signals investor conviction that behavioral endpoint AI is a large, capital-intensive market.
≈ “you announced a $100 million round” paraphrase
Dixon says INT deliberately does not compete head-on with EDR—he calls EDR a commodity everyone already has—and instead augments the gaps EDR, DLP, and insider-risk tools leave, positioning INT as a 'programmable endpoint.'
thesis🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highEDRDLPinsider riskprogrammable endpoint
So what: Avoiding the crowded EDR fight and selling augmentation eases the enterprise buy versus rip-and-replace.
“From my perspective, EDR is a commodity at this point. Everybody's got something in place” ▶ 29:01
INT offers both a hosted SaaS (dedicated tenant) and one-click self-hosted deployment inside the customer's cloud, but is not open source; when the customer self-hosts, hosting cost shifts to their COGS while licensing remains.
framework🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highSaaSself-hostinglicensingCOGS
So what: A licensing model decoupled from hosting gives predictable pricing and flexibility without an open-source strategy.
Dixon reports strong demand from goodwill built over multiple prior exits: he has hired over 75 people with zero recruiting fees and retained a healthy pipeline of interested buyers even before formally leaving stealth.
data_point🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highhiringMicrosoftpipelinerecruiting
So what: Founder reputation converting into free hiring and warm pipeline is a de-risking signal for the raise.
“I've hired well over, you know, 75 people now. Uh and I've not paid a single recruiting fee” ▶ 32:11
INT targets Global 2000 and above—Fortune 500-scale enterprises—and is currently building out sales reps, sales engineering, and forward-deployed engineering to handle post-stealth inbound.
data_point🗣 Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INTconviction: highGlobal 2000Fortune 500sales engineeringforward deployed engineering
So what: An enterprise-only motion means long sales cycles and heavy GTM spend, informing how to read future ARR milestones.
“we go and target big enterprises our our environments are global 2000 and above. So think Fortune 500” ▶ 17:57
Sume Labs: video-model orchestration 5
David Im's Sume Labs (sume.com) is building an agent-orchestration/router layer wrapping five or six video, image, and audio models to produce a production-ready result and eventually one-shot full marketing videos.
thesis🗣 David Im, founder of Sume Labsconviction: highSume Labssume.comvideo generationagent orchestration
So what: A model-agnostic orchestration layer is a bet that value moves to routing/reliability above commoditized generative models.
“what we're trying to do is make an API that is basically unwrapper of like let's say five or six models to get the production ready result” ▶ 35:36
Im frames current AI video generation as a 'slot machine'—stochastic and unpredictable, requiring repeated prompting where each attempt takes ~30 seconds—and argues removing that randomness raises utilization.
causal🗣 David Im, founder of Sume Labsconviction: highvideo generationstochastic modelsutilization
So what: Reliability/predictability, not raw quality, is the wedge Im is betting monetizes in generative video.
“the slot machine is not fun when you're making videos and images and they take 30 seconds. It's incredibly frustrating” ▶ 35:16
Individual video-generation models cap at roughly 15 seconds (about 30 for some), so Sume routes across models and stitches clips with one consistent audio track to produce up to 60 seconds of consistent video.
data_point🗣 David Im, founder of Sume Labsconviction: highGemini Omnivideo generation modelsaudio persistence
So what: Length and consistency limits of base models create the technical gap an orchestration layer monetizes.
“video generation models only like support up to 15 seconds it's like 30 seconds for our like CDS like next model” ▶ 37:38
Facial consistency is a core unsolved problem in AI video—a generated person's face often looks right for the first ~7 seconds then distorts toward the end—and maintaining it throughout is a key differentiator Sume targets.
data_point🗣 David Im and host (Alex)conviction: mediumfacial consistencyvideo clipsAI video
So what: Whoever solves temporal face consistency unlocks longer, usable AI marketing videos—a concrete technical moat.
“for the first like seven seconds of the video it'll look like the person's face and then it sometimes gets like distorted towards the end” ▶ 39:47
Sume has ~20K users and roughly 90% of its paid customers are brands, with its core audience on Instagram and TikTok rather than Twitter; users just pick an avatar and write a script, and each scene is generated three times for selection.
data_point🗣 David Im, founder of Sume Labsconviction: highSume LabsbrandsInstagramTikTok
So what: Brand-heavy paid mix and a simple avatar+script UX indicate a UGC-ad product-market fit signal to track.
“right now we have 20K users and the main customers espe especially about 90% of the paid customers are brands” ▶ 40:30

War on the Rocks 1 episode(s) · 28 insights

How Ukraine Put Russia on the Backfoot30 min28 insights2026-07-21
▶ Watch on YouTube
Carnegie analysts Mike and Dara argue mid-2026 is a genuine turning point in the Russia-Ukraine war: Ukraine holds the initiative via a sequenced drone/strike campaign against Russian logistics, Crimea, and energy/refining infrastructure that has produced a real fuel crisis inside Russia, while Russian ground innovation, manpower quality, and economics deteriorate. They caution this is a turning point, not imminent victory, and push back hard on NATO/nuclear escalation fears as not credible given Russia's forces are tied down in Ukraine. This is a defense/geopolitics episode; investor relevance is indirect (defense procurement, Russian energy/refining, Russian macro sustainability, Starlink dependence).
Turning point, not victory · 3Sequenced strike campaign on Crimea, logistics, energy · 5War breaks the insulation of ethnic Russians · 2Russian economic sustainability strain · 2Russian innovation engine stalling · 5Russian manpower degradation · 2Escalation fears overblown · 4Ceasefire path and limits of a Ukrainian offensive · 5
Turning point, not victory 3
Ukraine is in its best military position of the war since summer 2023, before its failed offensive, and this qualifies as a turning point rather than merely an inflection point, though it is unclear whether the advantage is lasting or a temporary window.
thesis🗣 Mike (Carnegie)conviction: highUkraineRussia
So what: Frames a regime shift in war momentum that bears on defense-spending, European security, and Russia-exposed asset risk without implying a near-term Ukrainian victory.
“Ukraine is in the best position it's been in the war since summer of 2023” ▶ 1:22
The Russian offensive has floundered, with consecutive months of essentially no territorial gains or actual losses of control, while Ukraine holds the initiative by targeting Russian logistics and operational depth and isolating Crimea.
data_point🗣 Mike (Carnegie)conviction: highRussiaUkraineCrimea
So what: Stalled Russian ground progress supports a longer-war / no-breakthrough base case and reduces the probability of a decisive shift favoring Moscow near-term.
Ukraine is vulnerable heading into winter because it is largely out of ammunition for Western-provided missile defense, raising the risk that Russia's hand strengthens again in colder months, so Ukraine may be seeking leverage for pre-winter negotiations rather than a large offensive.
causal🗣 Mike (Carnegie)conviction: mediumUkraineRussia
So what: Signals a seasonal window of Ukrainian vulnerability and demand for Western air-defense interceptors (e.g. Patriot restocking) into winter 2026.
Sequenced strike campaign on Crimea, logistics, energy 5
Ukraine's Crimea campaign has been a deliberately sequenced six-plus-month effort: first degrading Russian air defenses near the land bridge, then sustained targeting of the land logistics route, forcing Russia to redeploy counter-drone teams like Rubicon from other parts of the front and opening opportunities elsewhere.
framework🗣 Dara (Carnegie)conviction: highUkraineCrimeaRussiaRubicon
So what: Shows Ukraine imposing multi-front dilemmas that thin Russian defenses, a dynamic worth tracking for where the next Ukrainian pressure lands.
“Russia has had to compensate by moving counter drone teams like Rubicon and other elements like that from other parts of the front” ▶ 2:57
Ukraine's strike campaign on Crimea's electrical grid is causing gas and food shortages severe enough that Russians are trying to leave the peninsula.
data_point🗣 Dara (Carnegie)conviction: highUkraineCrimeaRussia
So what: Demonstrates strategic-effect targeting of civilian-supporting infrastructure that could compound Russian occupation costs and domestic strain.
After two years targeting Russian energy infrastructure and fuel-refining capacity, Ukraine has finally generated a genuine fuel crisis in 2026 because it now has the number of drones, the sortie rates, and the range to hit most refineries within several thousand kilometers, and has done enough cumulative damage during peak-summer demand.
causal🗣 Mike (Carnegie)conviction: highUkraineRussiaenergy infrastructureoil refining
So what: Directly relevant to Russian refined-product supply, domestic fuel prices, and export capacity; a sustained refinery-strike campaign is a bearish factor for Russian downstream output.
“this year Ukraine has been able to generate a genuine fuel crisis what they've been trying to do for the last 2 years” ▶ 4:12
The Ukrainian strike on Moscow carries the greatest political embarrassment for the Kremlin because Moscow is the most heavily defended city on Earth, and the fact that many Ukrainian drones got through points to high sortie rates overwhelming Russian air and drone defenses.
thesis🗣 Dara (Carnegie)conviction: mediumUkraineMoscowRussia
So what: Deep strikes on Moscow raise the political and reputational cost of the war for Putin even where physical damage is limited.
“the most heavily defended city on Earth” ▶ 6:01
When Russian forces stall on the ground, Russia predictably retaliates against Kyiv with strikes moving away from purely defensive targets toward the city center and apartment blocks, then broadcasts those strikes on state media to give its embarrassed population something to show after events like a Moscow fuel-tank explosion.
causal🗣 Dara (Carnegie)conviction: mediumRussiaKyivUkraine
So what: Establishes a behavioral tell: escalation against Kyiv civilians is a signal of Russian battlefield frustration rather than strategic strength.
War breaks the insulation of ethnic Russians 2
What is genuinely new in this phase is that the war's effects are now visible and visceral to ethnic Russians in Moscow and St. Petersburg, whom the Russian state had gone to great lengths to insulate; fuel shortages and drone uncertainty are ending the ability of most Russians to ignore the war.
thesis🗣 Dara (Carnegie)conviction: highRussiaMoscowSt. PetersburgUkraine
So what: Erosion of the domestic insulation that let Russia sustain the war could gradually raise Kremlin political costs, a slow-burn risk factor for regime stability narratives.
Bringing the war home to Russian civilians is unlikely to shift Putin's political calculus because he does not care much what average Russians or even the elite think, and only real, unhideable battlefield reversals would change the Russian military's assurances to him that success is inevitable.
thesis🗣 Mike (Carnegie)conviction: highPutinRussia
So what: Tempers any thesis that domestic pain forces a Russian policy change; the binding constraint is battlefield outcomes, not public opinion.
Russian economic sustainability strain 2
The head of Russia's central bank (Nabiullina) is clearly sounding the alarm that defense spending is not sustainable and is structurally imbalancing the economy, and Russia's technocratic elite are increasingly discussing sustainability publicly even as she may eventually retire.
data_point🗣 Mike (Carnegie)conviction: mediumRussiaNabiullinaCentral Bank of Russia
So what: Public elite acknowledgment of fiscal/structural strain is a leading indicator for Russian macro stress and for how long Moscow can fund the war.
“this all of defense spending is not sustainable and is structurally imbalancing for the economy” ▶ 9:19
Much of the Russian elite did not support the war but has adapted by realigning its revenue streams to make money off of defense spending, blunting elite pressure to end it.
thesis🗣 Mike (Carnegie)conviction: mediumRussia
So what: Explains why elite economic pain has not translated into political pressure to stop the war; defense-spending rents co-opt would-be opponents.
Russian innovation engine stalling 5
Russian ground tactics have not meaningfully evolved in the past year; their infiltration methods, motorcycle and small-unit assaults have plateaued, exposing the limits of Russia's ability to find a tactical solution to a complex drone-saturated battlefield.
thesis🗣 Dara (Carnegie)conviction: highRussiaUkraine
So what: Tactical stagnation caps Russian breakthrough potential and supports a grinding-attrition, no-decisive-gain outlook.
“it's just the same idea applied 12 months” ▶ 10:32
Russia's historical edge of innovating better at scale is stalling: it did expand Rubicon drone formations and substantially degrade Ukraine's drone advantage last year, but the rest of the force could not exploit it because Russia committed to grinding through a perpetually regenerating assault layer across a broad front rather than discrete offensives.
framework🗣 Mike (Carnegie)conviction: highRussiaUkraineRubicon
So what: A structural mismatch between Russia's tactical drone gains and its attritional doctrine means innovation is not converting into operational breakthroughs.
Russia gained more territory in 2025 than in 2024 on paper, but is fighting less and less efficiently at higher losses, and has grown complacent, believing it can reach its objectives without substantially changing its approach while Ukraine keeps optimizing against it.
contrarian🗣 Mike (Carnegie)conviction: highRussiaUkraine
So what: Headline territorial gains overstate Russian success; the efficiency trend favors Ukraine and undercuts a Russian-momentum narrative.
“they gained more territory in 2025 than 2024” ▶ 12:06
Ukraine spent 2025 finally reforming long-standing force problems dating to 2022, stabilizing the manpower situation, restructuring brigades into corps, and experimenting with drone-enabled localized counterattacks, which combined to blunt the near-identical Russian offensive that reopened in late March 2026.
data_point🗣 Mike (Carnegie)conviction: highUkraineRussia
So what: Institutional reform, not just technology, underpins Ukraine's improved position and its durability against repeat Russian offensives.
Russia's planned large-scale introduction of combat robotics failed for internal reasons: many systems were built using covert 'black' Starlink and stopped working once Russia was cut off from Starlink, compounded by corruption scandals and competing entities fighting to lead the effort.
causal🗣 Dara (Carnegie)conviction: highRussiaStarlinkcombat robotics
So what: Highlights Russian dependence on Starlink for unmanned systems and how denial plus corruption stalled a key capability, relevant to defense-tech and satcom themes.
“Russians got cut off from Starlink and a lot of those combat robotics that they were testing were built using covert or clandestine black Starlink” ▶ 13:40
Russian manpower degradation 2
Russia has likely been missing its recruiting targets for roughly two to three months, a shortfall it has recovered from before, but a four-to-six-month miss would start to have ground-level consequences.
data_point🗣 Dara (Carnegie)conviction: mediumRussia
So what: Gives a concrete threshold (4-6 months of missed recruitment) to watch as a signal of genuine Russian manpower trouble.
Russian force quality is in steady decline, with junior officers either pulled from reserve in their 40s and 50s who have not served in 20-plus years or cadets rushed through condensed education, alongside increasingly systematic violence and declining discipline across the front.
causal🗣 Dara (Carnegie)conviction: highRussia
So what: Cumulative quality and discipline decay is a slow-moving erosion of Russian combat effectiveness that compounds over time.
Escalation fears overblown 4
Talk that Russia will escalate militarily against a NATO member state is not credible because Russia's land power is tied down in Ukraine and its air force cannot do it; there are no realistic Russian prospects for a military operation against NATO even if the war ended.
contrarian🗣 Dara (Carnegie)conviction: highRussiaNATOUkraine
So what: Pushes back on NATO-war escalation narratives that periodically spook markets; the analysts see them as recurring and unfounded whenever Russia is losing.
≈ “We don't need to panic on behalf of the Kremlin.” paraphrase
Russia will likely escalate against Ukraine this summer to test whether the reported Patriot interceptor depletion is real, meaning more damaging ballistic missile strikes against Kyiv, because Moscow will not take the depletion claim at face value.
prediction🗣 Dara (Carnegie)conviction: mediumRussiaUkraineKyivPatriot
So what: Flags a near-term escalation vector tied to Ukrainian air-defense interceptor shortages and Western resupply urgency.
Russian escalation against NATO will stay in the realm of sabotage and arson operations, which have been ongoing for four years and are mostly intercepted, and while these will get 'sportier,' they fall well short of an overt military attack, which would be a suicide mission that surrenders control over next steps.
prediction🗣 Dara (Carnegie)conviction: highRussiaNATO
So what: Bounds the realistic Russian escalation ladder against NATO to gray-zone sabotage, not conventional war, informing defense and risk-premium assumptions.
The 2022 nuclear crisis was a misinterpretation of multiple signals, and analysts should resist perceiving escalation fears on the Kremlin's behalf every time Russia stalls on the ground; tactical nuclear use would gain Russia nothing because it would irradiate territory it wants to annex.
contrarian🗣 Dara (Carnegie)conviction: highRussiaPutin
So what: Argues the current situation is materially different from the fall-2022 nuclear scare, deflating tail-risk narratives around Russian nuclear use.
“there's a duty to warn, but there is also a duty to provide reasoned judgment along with it” ▶ 22:49
Ceasefire path and limits of a Ukrainian offensive 5
Putin has an easy off-ramp because Western and Ukrainian demands have narrowed so far that even a conditional or 'Iran-style' ceasefire could be agreed tomorrow, and no major Russian group (the checked-out public, relieved elites, or even the controlled right-wing Telegram crowd) would strongly oppose a ceasefire along the current line of contact.
thesis🗣 Mike (Carnegie)conviction: mediumPutinRussiaUkraine
So what: Implies the binding obstacle to a ceasefire is Putin's own maximalism, not domestic Russian opposition, shaping any peace-deal probability assessment.
Former Ukrainian commander-in-chief Zaluzhny issued a sobering corrective in a Telegraph piece arguing Ukraine is doing well but the ground situation is still closer to a stalemate, warning against overloading the moment with expectations, though he has political motives as the likely challenger to Zelensky.
anecdote🗣 Ryan Evans (host)conviction: mediumZaluzhnyZelenskyUkraineThe Telegraph
So what: A credible Ukrainian insider is deliberately deflating victory expectations, a caution against over-optimistic Ukraine-wins positioning.
Absent a collapse of Russian forces, Ukraine lacks the manpower and materiel to mount a decisive offensive to retake territory and cannot fully substitute robots for troops, so any Ukrainian offensive is likely to be a successful localized action rather than something that decisively changes the war.
thesis🗣 Mike (Carnegie)conviction: highUkraineRussia
So what: Caps the upside case: a turning point does not equal an imminent Ukrainian breakthrough, arguing against pricing in a near-term decisive outcome.
The one thing that could change Putin's mind is a battlefield reversal the Russian military cannot hide, such as a loss of currently occupied territory too large to disguise with an inaccurate map, rather than movements of a few hundred meters.
framework🗣 Mike (Carnegie)conviction: highPutinRussiaUkraine
So what: Defines the specific condition (unhideable territorial loss) that would be the real signal of a shift toward Russian concessions.
Ukraine produces its best operations and best understands its opponent when it is not being pressured by Western partners to launch an offensive and absorb that risk, a lesson from the pressured, expectation-laden 2023 offensive.
thesis🗣 Dara (Carnegie)conviction: highUkraine
So what: Suggests Western pressure for a showcase offensive would be counterproductive; patience favors Ukrainian effectiveness.
“as the kids say, let them cook” ▶ 29:01

Mindscape · Sean Carroll 1 episode(s) · 27 insights

Bonnie Bassler on How Bacteria Talk and Work Together | Mindscape 36178 min27 insights2026-07-20
▶ Watch on YouTube
Molecular biologist Bonnie Bassler explains quorum sensing—how bacteria use secreted chemical signals to count their density, identify kin vs. rivals, and switch on collective behaviors like virulence, bioluminescence, and biofilms. This is a basic-science interview with no market or investment content; the actionable 'edge' is scientific: mechanisms, hard biological data points, and emerging frontiers (microbiome-driven personalized medicine, anti-virulence and phage therapeutics, cross-domain signaling).
Quorum-sensing mechanism · 3Scale and dominance of microbial life · 4Microbiome and human health · 5Chemical languages, cheating and game theory · 3Bioluminescence: the founding discovery · 2Anti-virulence and biotech applications · 3Cross-domain signaling frontier · 3Programmed cell death and altruism in biofilms · 1Self-organization and the road to multicellularity · 3
Quorum-sensing mechanism 3
Bacteria coordinate group behavior via 'quorum sensing': each cell secretes small signal molecules called auto-inducers whose concentration rises in step with cell density, and when the level crosses a detection threshold the whole population simultaneously changes gene expression to perform collective tasks.
framework🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: highquorum sensingauto-inducersbacteria
So what: The core mechanism underlying most bacterial virulence, biofilms, and beneficial microbiome functions—any therapeutic that manipulates bacterial behavior likely acts on this system.
“the molecules are their words, if you will” ▶ 24:52
Individual bacteria have no way to count neighbors directly; they use the buildup of secreted auto-inducer molecules as a proxy for cell number, inferring that a quorum is present when molecule concentration is high.
framework🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: highquorum sensingauto-inducers
So what: Explains why disrupting or spoofing the chemical signal (rather than killing cells) can control bacterial populations.
“They're using these the buildup of these molecules as a proxy for cell number.” ▶ 24:23
Quorum sensing behaves like a phase transition: below the molecular threshold bacteria stay individually quiescent, and above it they switch in unison into collective action—Bassler accepted the physicist's 'phase transition' framing.
framework🗣 Bonnie Bassler / host Sean Carrollconviction: mediumquorum sensingphase transition
So what: Provides a physics-tractable model for predicting the sharp density tipping point at which bacterial populations become dangerous or productive.
Scale and dominance of microbial life 4
By current estimates a human carries roughly 10x more bacterial cells than human cells and about 100x more bacterial genes than human genes, amounting to roughly five pounds of bacteria—though because human cells are 500–1000x larger, we remain mostly human by mass.
data_point🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: mediummicrobiomebacteriahuman cells
So what: Quantifies the microbiome as a substantial biological organ, grounding the case for microbiome-targeted diagnostics and therapeutics.
≈ “you have 10 times more bacterial cells than human cells 100 times more bacterial genes than human genes but you're right human cells are much bigger so you have about five pounds of bacteria” paraphrase
Bacteria are Earth's most ancient organisms, evolving roughly four billion years ago with only a few thousand genes, and they still constitute nearly all of the planet's biodiversity—every visible plant, animal and person occupies just one tip of one branch of the eukaryotes on the tree of life.
data_point🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: highbacteriaarchaeaeukaryotestree of life
So what: Reframes the biosphere as overwhelmingly microbial, the premise behind treating bacteria as the dominant chemistry engine of ecosystems and human health.
“the world is microbial and it's mostly bacterial” ▶ 18:32
Essentially every surface on Earth, animate or inanimate, is coated in a bacterial biofilm—a community structure bacteria use as their predominant mode of living in nature.
data_point🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: highbiofilmbacteria
