So what: Biofilms are the real-world state bacteria live in, so anti-biofilm and biofilm-engineering approaches matter far more than test-tube (planktonic) results suggest.
≈ “Every surface on this earth. Absolutely. The piece of paper in front of me is covered with a bofilm.” paraphrase
Life comprises three domains, with archaea giving rise to both bacteria and eukaryotes; archaea genomes actually resemble eukaryotic genomes more than bacterial ones, and surviving archaea persist in hostile niches like thermal vents and Yellowstone that approximate early Earth.
data_point🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: mediumarchaeabacteriaeukaryotesYellowstone
So what: Context for origin-of-life inference: extremophile archaea are the closest living proxies to the first replicating organisms.
Microbiome and human health 5
The human genome lacks the enzymes needed to digest plant matter; gut bacteria supply those enzymes, which is why vegetables yield calories and nutrients at all—making digestion of plant food a directly bacteria-dependent function.
causal🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: highmicrobiomegut bacteriadigestion
So what: A settled, mechanistic microbiome function that anchors otherwise speculative gut-health claims.
“You your human genome does not have the enzymes that allow you to digest that food” ▶ 55:28
The skin and gut microbiome acts like a 'suit of armor,' occupying physical real estate so that incoming pathogenic bacteria cannot get a foothold—a colonization-resistance defense distinct from the immune system.
framework🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: highmicrobiomebiofilmskingut
So what: Explains why disrupting the microbiome (e.g., with antibiotics) opens the door to infection, and why probiotic 'real estate' strategies are pursued.
“the real estate in your gut is taken up by what we call your microbiome” ▶ 19:31
An individual's microbiome likely governs how they respond to medications—some people carry bacteria that degrade a drug—making the microbiome a central variable in personalized medicine and in why the same drug helps some patients, fails others, or causes side effects.
causal🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: mediummicrobiomepersonalized medicinedrug metabolism
So what: Points to microbiome profiling as a frontier for drug-response prediction and dosing.
Claims that the microbiome shapes mood, personality, or athletic performance are plausible but, by Bassler's account, not yet proven—mounting evidence exists given bacteria produce vast numbers of molecules circulating in the body, but the causal links remain unestablished.
exploratory🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: exploratorymicrobiomemoodgut-brain
So what: A caution flag: separates hyped consumer 'gut-brain' marketing from what the field can actually support.
“whether they can affect your mood that people think that's a possibility that is to my knowledge not proven yet” ▶ 56:07
The microbiome is highly individual and dynamic: people are born sterile and colonized on the way out of the birth canal, the community shifts with diet, cohabitation, antibiotics and environment, and its diversity tends to plummet with age—possibly linked to aging and health.
data_point🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: mediummicrobiomeaging
So what: Individual variability and age-related diversity loss frame microbiome interventions as personalized and time-dependent rather than one-size-fits-all.
Chemical languages, cheating and game theory 3
Bacteria use multiple auto-inducer 'words' at once—a species-specific molecule meaning 'twin/clone,' a family-level molecule meaning 'kin,' and a universal molecule meaning 'other'—and read the ratios to gauge both how many cells are present and whether neighbors are kin or enemies before choosing behavior.
framework🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: highauto-inducersquorum sensingbacteria
So what: Shows bacterial signaling encodes identity and social context, not just headcount—key to engineering species-selective interventions.
“one and only one species of bacterium makes. So that molecule says you are my twin.” ▶ 27:33
Quorum-sensing systems are riddled with cheating, freeloading and eavesdropping: some bacteria make the signal molecule but skip the expensive public-goods genes, and some molecules are dual-function—a signal to kin but an antibiotic that kills competing species that tune into it.
contrarian🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: highquorum sensingauto-inducersantibiotics
So what: Dual-function signal/antibiotic molecules and cheating strategies are leads for antimicrobial discovery and for understanding microbial competition.
≈ “there is all kinds of attempted and cheating and freewriting and eavesdropping” paraphrase
Bacteria are an ideal experimental system for game theory and information theory because researchers can engineer defined mutants—a 'cheater' that makes the molecule but not the trait, or a 'deaf' cell that cannot detect signals—and run controlled tragedy-of-the-commons experiments on a petri dish, adding synthetic molecules and blends at will.
framework🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: highgame theoryinformation theorybacteriaNed Wingreen
So what: Explains the deep physics/theory collaborations (e.g., 25 years with theorist Ned Wingreen) and why bacterial data feed quantitative models of cooperation.
“we can make a cheater” ▶ 43:34
Bioluminescence: the founding discovery 2
Quorum sensing was first uncovered in bioluminescent marine bacteria by Woody Hastings in the 1970s: the cells grow dark in a flask and then all switch on blue light together, which made an otherwise invisible collective behavior measurable and launched the entire field.
anecdote🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: highWoody Hastingsbioluminescenceluciferasequorum sensing
So what: Historical proof that a visible reporter unlocked the field—the same 'make the invisible visible' strategy still drives discovery.
“it made the invisible world visible to the scientist” ▶ 47:34
The light-making enzyme luciferase evolved independently twice—once in ocean bacteria and once in land fireflies—with nothing in common except a requirement for oxygen, and firefly light serves mating (not quorum sensing).
data_point🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: highluciferasefirefliesbioluminescence
So what: A clean case of convergent evolution; clarifies that bioluminescence and quorum sensing are not intrinsically linked.
“that's about sex” ▶ 49:20
Anti-virulence and biotech applications 3
The virulence factors of clinically important pathogens—toxins, poisons, host-entry machinery—are all under quorum-sensing control, so mutants that cannot 'talk' or 'hear' are completely avirulent even though their full weaponry is intact; they simply never get the 'go' command.
thesis🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: highquorum sensingvirulencepathogens
So what: The mechanistic basis for anti-virulence drugs that disarm infections without killing bacteria, lowering selective pressure for resistance.
“it's command and control. It's like the army. If you don't say go one, two, three, shoot, right? They don't know to do it.” ▶ 53:31
Bassler's lab has synthesized auto-inducer look-alike antagonist molecules that slot into quorum-sensing receptors and block the real signals, shutting down virulence in animal models—buying time for the immune system—though these are not yet in clinical medicine.
prediction🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: mediumquorum sensingantagonistsreceptorsimmune system
So what: A concrete, pre-clinical anti-infective modality distinct from antibiotics; watch for translation into human trials.
“those shut down vir in animal models” ▶ 53:04
The flip-side application is boosting quorum sensing in beneficial bacteria for agriculture, human health, and industry—including bioremediation such as cleaning up oil spills.
thesis🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: mediumquorum sensingbioremediationagriculture
So what: Maps a broad industrial/agricultural opportunity set around engineering microbial cooperation, not just suppressing it.
Cross-domain signaling frontier 3
Human gut cells make molecules almost identical to bacterial quorum-sensing auto-inducers, which the gut bacteria perceive as their own signals—suggesting humans may actively drive their microbiome's collective behaviors, an emerging area of cross-domain (eukaryote-to-bacteria) chemical communication.
thesis🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: mediumquorum sensingguteukaryotesmicrobiome
So what: If humans steer their microbiome chemically, it opens host-directed control of bacterial behavior as a therapeutic lever.
“your human gut cells make molecules that are almost identical to these bacterial qumsensing molecules” ▶ 1:03:24
Bacterial viruses (phages) have captured or evolved quorum-sensing receptors and eavesdrop on their host's auto-inducer molecules, switching from dormant lysogeny to host-killing lytic mode only when the signal shows many host cells are around to infect next.
causal🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: highphagesquorum sensinglysogenylytic
So what: Reveals a signal that governs the phage kill-decision—exploitable to make phage therapies kill or spare hosts on demand.
“The viruses are eavesdropping. They're surveilling the quorumsensing molecules” ▶ 1:07:01
Because phages tune into quorum sensing, they can be engineered as phage therapies that are triggered to kill (or not kill) host bacteria on demand.
prediction🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: mediumphagesphage therapyquorum sensing
So what: A programmable antibacterial concept distinct from small-molecule drugs.
“we can like trick these viruses and make fage therapies” ▶ 1:07:58
Programmed cell death and altruism in biofilms 1
In mature biofilms, quorum sensing regionally controls the death of specific cells—the oldest central cells die and spill their nutrients, an altruism- or cannibalism-like sacrifice that appears to help younger community members survive lean times.
anecdote🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: mediumbiofilmquorum sensingprogrammed cell death
So what: Suggests bacteria may have programmed-cell-death machinery long thought exclusive to eukaryotes, a target for controlling biofilm persistence.
Self-organization and the road to multicellularity 3
Quorum sensing is genuine self-organization—no 'boss' bacterium directs the others; emergent collective behavior arises purely from the biochemistry of making and detecting molecules in simple single cells.
framework🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: highself-organizationquorum sensingemergence
So what: A clean biological model of decentralized emergence relevant to complexity science and collective-behavior modeling.
≈ “you can get this emergent behavior without a boss” paraphrase
Cells within a biofilm carry identical genomes yet differentiate into distinct fates by region, like organs—but unlike true multicellular tissue, any single bacterium survives on its own and can rebuild the entire biofilm, so quorum sensing is a partial, not complete, step toward multicellularity.
contrarian🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: mediumbiofilmmulticellularitydifferentiation
So what: Sets the precise boundary between bacterial collectives and multicellular organisms—relevant to origin-of-multicellularity debates.
“if I scramble it up, every one of those cells like they're sort of like the ultimate stem cells” ▶ 1:12:08
Endosymbiosis illustrates the endpoint of runaway cooperation: mitochondria and chloroplasts were once free-living bacteria that became so integrated they lost the ability to live independently—an analog of primitive cells cooperating so successfully they surrender autonomy en route to eukaryotes.
framework🗣 Bonnie Basslerconviction: highendosymbiosismitochondriachloroplasteukaryotes
So what: Grounds the theory that today's cellular cooperation descends from ancient bacterial partnerships—bacteria 'made the rules' of collective life.
“The bacteria made the rules, Sean” ▶ 38:12

In Good Company · Nicolai Tangen 1 episode(s) · 26 insights

Venki Ramakrishnan: The Science and Hype of Living Longer | Podcast | In Good Company48 min26 insights2026-07-22
▶ Watch on YouTube
Nobel chemist Venki Ramakrishnan argues that most radical life-extension claims are hype: aging science can raise health span but the ~120-year maximum lifespan ceiling has barely moved. He is skeptical of billionaire-funded longevity ventures (Altos Labs, cryopreservation, young-blood clinics), sees cellular reprogramming as the only plausible route past 120 but far from safe human use, and views biology as an analog problem AI cannot shortcut.
Lifespan ceiling vs. average · 3Cellular reprogramming: the only path past 120 · 4The billionaire longevity money · 3Specific ventures and bets · 4AI in biology: powerful but bounded · 3What actually works for health span · 6Societal and ethical second-order effects · 3
Lifespan ceiling vs. average 3
Human life expectancy has doubled over 150 years almost entirely from reducing early-life mortality (infant mortality, infectious disease, accidents) via public health, vaccination, nutrition and medicine, not from slowing aging, so the maximum lifespan of roughly 110-120 years has barely moved.
framework🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highlongevitypublic healthvaccination
So what: Separates the addressable market: gains come from more people reaching ~100 (health span), not from breaking the 120 ceiling, tempering valuations built on radical extension.
“life expectancy has doubled in the last 150 years, but that's mostly due to improvements early in life” ▶ 9:28
Ramakrishnan classifies multi-hundred-year lifespans as 'highly unrealistic today' alongside colonizing other galaxies: no physical law forbids it, but the engineering difficulty makes it near-impossible with current science.
contrarian🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highlongevity
So what: A Nobel laureate publicly bucketing radical life-extension as fantasy is a caution flag for capital chasing immortality narratives.
“I put a this long life extension, hundreds of years in that category, you know, which is highly unrealistic today, despite what, you know, you may hear from various uh people promoting hype” ▶ 2:12
Slowing aging is unlikely to deliver large lifespan gains; its main effect is pushing more people to reach ~100, while cracking the 120-year barrier would require cellular reprogramming that reverses biological age.
prediction🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: mediumcellular reprogramminglongevity
So what: Frames where breakthrough optionality actually sits (reprogramming) versus incremental anti-aging plays.
Cellular reprogramming: the only path past 120 4
Partial cellular reprogramming (turning cells slightly backward in developmental age while keeping their identity) is the most plausible route to reverse aging, building on Yamanaka's finding that just four introduced genes can revert cells to a stem-cell state.
framework🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highcellular reprogrammingYamanakaJohn Gurdonstem cells
So what: Identifies the specific scientific mechanism underpinning flagship longevity bets like Altos Labs.
Reprogramming works in mice but is highly heterogeneous even within one organ (only clusters of cells in a targeted pancreas get reprogrammed) and, critically, did not increase the lifespan of reprogrammed mice, which merely looked healthier by various criteria.
data_point🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highcellular reprogrammingmice
So what: The core animal data show cosmetic health improvement without longevity gain, a due-diligence red flag for reprogramming-based ventures.
“in mice it didn't actually increase the longevity of the mice” ▶ 13:23
The genes used in reprogramming carry unknown long-term safety risk because some are oncogenes that can cause cancer, and delivering them via adenoviral vectors uniformly across all target-tissue cells in humans remains unsolved.
causal🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highcellular reprogrammingoncogenesadenoviral vectors
So what: Names the specific safety and delivery bottlenecks that keep human reprogramming years from approval.
“Some of them are anko genes for example which can cause cancer” ▶ 12:15
A Boston experiment injecting reprogramming factors directly into the eye to regenerate retinal tissue (targeting age-related retinal degeneration and blindness) has been approved for clinical trials, but many mouse successes fail in human trials.
data_point🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: mediumcellular reprogrammingretinal degenerationclinical trials
So what: A concrete near-term clinical milestone to watch as a real read on whether reprogramming translates to humans.
“that's actually been approved for clinical trials. But again you know many things that work in mice fail in clinical trials” ▶ 26:41
The billionaire longevity money 3
Tens of billions flowing into longevity has two distinct drivers: a broadly-supported public-health case (aging societies with shrinking working-age populations need to extend health span) and a narrower group of billionaires with grandiose visions who made fortunes young and simply do not want to die.
framework🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highlongevityaging societies
So what: Distinguishes durable, policy-backed demand (health span) from ego-driven capital that may misprice risk.
Tech founders systematically mis-model biology as a software problem that can be hacked, when the world is analog: AI can surface drug and disease clues digitally, but turning them into real medicines happens in the slow analog world of real people.
contrarian🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highAItech billionairesdrug development
So what: Explains why tech-led longevity timelines are likely too optimistic; the analog translation step is the real bottleneck.
“the and the world is not digital the world is analog and uh I think they simply don't want to want to confront that” ▶ 18:08
Aging societies plus falling fertility, illustrated by Japan producing more adult diapers than baby diapers, are the structural macro reason governments and health agencies are funding aging research.
data_point🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highJapanfertility ratesaging societies
So what: Demographic decline is the durable secular tailwind for health-span (not immortality) investment.
“In Japan, they produce more diapers for old people than for babies” ▶ 10:50
Specific ventures and bets 4
Altos Labs (roughly $5 billion, backed by Bezos with Yuri Milner as a driving force) hired top academic scientists at salaries far above academia; its stated goal is health not lifespan, but funders likely believe there is big money in aging and that the team might crack radical life extension.
thesis🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: mediumAltos LabsJeff BezosYuri MilnerRick Klausner
So what: Frames Altos as a talent-arbitrage bet with option value on a reprogramming breakthrough, not a near-term product play.
“our goal is for everybody to die young after a long time” ▶ 22:30
Brian Johnson's self-experimentation (~$2 million a year on his own longevity) is scientifically uninterpretable because it has no control experiment: one person mixing many interventions at once cannot show what, if anything, works, and Ramakrishnan's near-50 son looks just as young untreated.
contrarian🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highBrian Johnsonlongevity treatments
So what: Undercuts the evidentiary basis for the influencer-driven biohacking/supplement market.
“the trouble of Brian Johnson is no control experiment” ▶ 24:54
Young-blood therapy clinics arose from experiments where connecting old and young rats' blood supply benefited the old animal; companies now buy blood from young donors and sell it at huge markups to rich old men, and Ramakrishnan is highly skeptical it works.
contrarian🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highyoung bloodlongevity clinics
So what: Flags young-blood and stem-cell-injection clinics as commercialization ahead of evidence.
“there were companies that were starting to sell young blood” ▶ 27:22
Cryopreservation of humans is currently science fiction: no one has frozen and revived even a mouse, and a widely cited frozen 'mouse brain' was only a thin section, not a whole brain.
contrarian🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highcryopreservation
So what: Dismisses cryonics ventures as having no near-term scientific basis.
“when you can freeze a mouse and thaw it so that it can run around again, uh then come and talk to me” ▶ 29:17
AI in biology: powerful but bounded 3
AI has revolutionized structural biology by predicting protein structures directly from gene sequence, but aging is a complex multifactorial process where it is unclear what data would even train a model, so AI's impact there is far less certain.
framework🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highAIstructural biologyAlphaFold
So what: Tempers AI-for-longevity hype: pattern recognition helps in well-structured problems, not in messy multifactorial aging.
Ramakrishnan calls Demis Hassabis's prediction that AI will leave no illnesses left within 10-15 years overoptimistic, because going from AI-generated drug or disease-cause clues to actual treatments is a slow analog process in real people.
contrarian🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highDemis HassabisIsomorphic LabsAlphaFoldAI
So what: A direct expert pushback on the most bullish AI-drug-discovery timeline, relevant to Isomorphic Labs-style valuations.
“going from there to having treatments is a complicated process and it doesn't happen in the digital world. It happens in real people” ▶ 17:30
Sequencing large cohorts of centenarians and using AI to detect genomic patterns is a concrete, sensible near-term application of AI to aging research.
framework🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: mediumAIcentenariansgenomics
So what: Points to genomic-cohort data plus AI as a more grounded longevity R&D approach than reprogramming hype.
What actually works for health span 6
Ramakrishnan would allocate research funding across four areas: caloric-restriction pathways (his most promising short-term goal), differences between old and young blood, targeted destruction of senescent cells, and cellular reprogramming as the exciting long-term goal.
framework🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highcaloric restrictionsenescent cellscellular reprogramming
So what: A Nobel laureate's capital-allocation map across aging science, ranking near-term (caloric restriction) vs long-term (reprogramming) bets.
Caloric restriction reliably slows aging across many species by turning on pathways that recycle defective molecules and organelles and reduce misfolded-protein production; the practical takeaway is to eat moderately and avoid obesity rather than stay hungry.
causal🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highcaloric restrictionautophagy
So what: The most evidence-backed lever, and the mechanistic rationale behind weight-control interventions.
GLP-1 drugs have shown effects well beyond diabetes and extreme obesity and may make old age healthier, though they carry side effects like muscle loss and would need to be made safer before use in healthy people.
thesis🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: mediumGLP-1 drugsobesitydiabetes
So what: A Nobel scientist framing GLP-1s as a possible health-span play beyond their current indications, with a safety caveat as the gating factor.
“GLP1 drugs have now shown all kinds of effects, not just for preventing diabetes or for extreme obesity” ▶ 32:05
Senescent cells (damaged cells secreting inflammatory compounds) serve as a cancer-prevention mechanism early in life but accumulate beyond the body's clearance capacity with age, so selectively destroying them is a promising anti-aging target if it can be dosed without harming healthy cells.
causal🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: mediumsenescent cellsinflammation
So what: Explains the senolytics thesis and its key risk (selectivity), relevant to that class of longevity biotech.
Sleep and exercise are underrated aging interventions: much molecular recycling and repair happens during the sleep cycle, and exercise actively stimulates tissue rejuvenation and regeneration.
causal🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highsleepexercise
So what: Low-cost, evidence-based levers that compete with expensive longevity products.
Ramakrishnan is highly skeptical of ice baths, cold plunges and saunas, saying they rest on one or two unvalidated observations with no control experiment, while conceding anything that reduces stress and feels good is probably beneficial.
contrarian🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: mediumcold plungesaunawellness industry
So what: A caution on the wellness/biohacking product category's thin evidence base.
“I would be very very skeptical uh of those sorts of uh treatments. I I doubt that they do much to be honest” ▶ 35:43
Societal and ethical second-order effects 3
Doubling lifespan risks a stagnant society because change is driven by generational turnover and the young, while older people accumulate wealth, influence and power together, entrenching the same group in control without turnover.
framework🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highlongevitysociety
So what: A structural argument that radical life extension could ossify power and capital concentration.
“you might end up with a very stagnant society” ▶ 37:07
Expensive, sophisticated aging treatments would create a two-tier society: the rich (who already live ~15 years longer than the poor in the US and ~10 years in the UK) would extend that gap in both wealth-derived power and years of life, with advantages passed to their children.
data_point🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highinequalitylongevityUnited StatesUnited Kingdom
So what: Flags inequality and access as the central regulatory/ethical risk for premium longevity treatments.
“15 years in the US and about 10 years in the UK” ▶ 39:37
Delegating cognition wholesale to AI would make humanity stupider and more ignorant; AI should be treated as one more tool in the scientific armory, like computation or robotics, not a replacement for human understanding.
thesis🗣 Venki Ramakrishnanconviction: highAIeducation
So what: A prominent scientist framing AI as augmentation, relevant to debates on AI's role in R&D productivity.
“we are going to uh simply delegate everything to AI which would be a recipe for making humanity stupider” ▶ 43:11

Capital Allocators · Ted Seides 1 episode(s) · 25 insights

Luis Laboy: Why Every Market Is Now an Emerging Market | Hewlett Foundation50 min25 insights2026-07-20
▶ Watch on YouTube
Luis Laboy, Director of Public Equities at the Hewlett Foundation, argues that geopolitical, policy, and market-structure risks once unique to emerging markets now describe every market, so allocators should rethink portfolio construction around quality and disruption risk. He details Hewlett's manager-selection machinery (explore/exploit sleeves, kill lists, need-vs-want criteria) and concrete positioning shifts (cutting value managers post-COVID, adding Japan).
Every Market Is an Emerging Market · 3Positioning: Value-to-Quality, Japan, Disruption Risk · 5Manager-Selection Machinery · 6Evaluating Managers & Communication · 4Team, Process & Portfolio Construction · 3Investing Philosophy & Career Lessons · 4
Every Market Is an Emerging Market 3
Luis Laboy argues that geopolitical risk, political uncertainty, social polarization, policy risk, and changing market structure — traits once used to define emerging markets — now describe every market in the world, collapsing the old EM-vs-developed distinction.
contrarian🗣 Luis Laboy (guest, Hewlett Foundation Director of Public Equities)conviction: highemerging marketsdeveloped marketsHewlett Foundation
So what: If the EM/DM dichotomy no longer holds, allocators should apply emerging-market-style political and structural risk analysis to developed-market portfolios and rethink asset-class buckets.
“If I describe a market to you and I tell you this market has geopolitical risk, it has political uncertainty. It has social polarization. It's got policy risk.” ▶ 37:40
Laboy contends the central task for allocators today is questioning whether they are asking the right questions about markets, because frameworks built over prior careers are now being invalidated by structural change.
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highHewlett FoundationAnna Marshall
So what: A signal to interrogate inherited assumptions (currency, growth, factor) rather than plug them into models; edge comes from reframing questions, not refining spreadsheets.
“are we asking the right questions about markets” ▶ 38:53
Laboy invokes Anna Marshall's 'depends on why' answer — that whether a strong or weak currency helps emerging markets has no fixed answer — as evidence that the underlying question is often wrong and that models cannot substitute for causal understanding.
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highAnna Marshallemerging marketscurrencies
So what: Warns quants and macro traders against seeking single-variable rules; the driver's sign depends on the underlying cause each cycle.
“is a strong currency or a weak currency good or bad for emerging markets? And she just said, depends on why” ▶ 38:23
Positioning: Value-to-Quality, Japan, Disruption Risk 5
During COVID, Hewlett reduced exposure to value managers on the view that value would be a tougher place going forward, and transitioned toward a quality, compounding-oriented equity portfolio.
thesis🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highHewlett Foundationvalue managersquality compoundersCOVID
So what: A named endowment tilting from value toward quality compounders around 2020 is a concrete style-rotation datapoint for factor-aware LPs.
“we ended up starting to uh reduce our exposure to for example value managers. We thought value would just be a tougher place to be going forward” ▶ 23:02
Hewlett arrived at the Japan opportunity via technical analysis — spotting a breakout in the market — then did emerging-markets-style ground research before eventually building a Japan position.
thesis🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highJapanHewlett Foundationtechnical analysisAnna Marshall
So what: Confirms an institutional LP is invested in Japan and shows technicals used as a top-down idea-generation trigger, not just risk timing.
“we got to Japan idea through technical analysis through understanding a breakout in markets” ▶ 35:24
Laboy frames technology and AI as an accelerating disruption risk that no longer separates 'tech' from the rest of the market — old-economy companies will increasingly differentiate based on how they apply technology across their stack.
thesis🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highAItechnologyold economy
So what: Argues for evaluating every company (not just tech names) on AI/tech adoption; supports managers who assess the 'tech stack' of traditional businesses.
“It's no longer about tech versus the rest of the world. It's old economy names are now going to differentiate themselves based on how they apply tech.” ▶ 41:27
Hewlett structures its portfolio around a widening set of disruption risks — technology, regulatory/government intervention, policy, geopolitical, and value-chain risk — and selects managers who explicitly manage disruption risk in their process.
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highHewlett Foundationregulatory riskgeopolitical riskvalue chain risk
So what: Maps the specific risk axes an endowment now underwrites; managers marketing 'disruption risk management' are being actively sought.
“AI is apparently going to eat the world” ▶ 40:14
Laboy applies the technical-analysis concept of 'curve fitting' — over-adapting to the past — to portfolio construction, deliberately letting go of biases and building the book dynamically rather than as a fixed asset-class checklist.
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: mediumtechnical analysisportfolio construction
So what: A caution against anchoring allocations to historical relationships; favor dynamic, forward-looking sizing over static policy weights.
“the risk of over adapting to the past” ▶ 40:38
Manager-Selection Machinery 6
Hewlett splits its equity book into a concentrated core portfolio (exploit) plus a carved-out next-generation manager sleeve (explore) with a lower confidence hurdle — not a lower quality hurdle — to keep a steady velocity of new ideas without diluting the core.
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highHewlett Foundationexplore vs exploit
So what: A reusable structure for concentrated allocators who otherwise can't onboard emerging managers because the incumbent bar is too high.
“You're lowering the confidence hurdle. You're not lowering the hurdle of quality.” ▶ 27:28
For every manager, Hewlett maintains a 'kill list' and 'key debates' — borrowed from Annie Duke's book 'Quit' — enumerating what would drive a redemption, so exits are made for fundamental reasons rather than chasing performance.
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highAnnie DukeQuitHewlett Foundation
So what: A pre-commitment device to counter the disposition effect in manager redemptions; concrete practice LPs can copy.
“we've borrowed this from Annie Duke and her book quit, right, is we've put together what we call as a kill list” ▶ 29:36
Laboy says most manager redemptions at Hewlett happen not because the manager is failing but because Hewlett is shifting its own strategy, leaving strong managers with no remaining alignment to the portfolio's needs.
causal🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highHewlett Foundation
So what: Reframes redemptions as portfolio-fit decisions, not performance verdicts — relevant to GPs interpreting why an LP left.
“you end up redeeming from a manager because we're shifting our strategy” ▶ 30:13
Laboy insists on defining manager-selection criteria in advance because the biggest danger is picking the 'shiny object' you want rather than what the portfolio needs — otherwise you inject your own biases as a factor risk.
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highHewlett Foundationmanager selection
So what: Pre-set criteria act as a bias-control mechanism; treats an allocator's personal preference as an unhedged factor exposure.
“you look for what you want instead of what you need” ▶ 27:56
The single most important thing Hewlett looks for in a manager is coherence — alignment between the manager's investment strategy and how they actually think and their emotional makeup — not adherence to a borrowed model.
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highHewlett Foundationmanager selection
So what: A due-diligence lens: probe whether a manager's process is internalized or imitated (e.g. copying Buffett), as imitation signals fragility.
“their investment strategy aligns with how they think, right? Their emotional makeup. That's the most important thing we look for with a manager” ▶ 48:52
Laboy separates opportunity-set conviction from manager selection: Hewlett first gains conviction on the opportunity set, then decides which asset class best expresses it, then the right strategy, then finally the manager.
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highHewlett FoundationAnna Marshall
So what: A top-down-then-bottom-up sequencing that GPs can anticipate; managers are selected last, as executors of a pre-formed thesis.
“we gain conviction on the opportunity set” ▶ 34:07
Evaluating Managers & Communication 4
Laboy warns that when a manager uses a buzzword like 'quality compounders,' they cede control of the conversation to the LP, who fills the gap with their own interpretation — and any later process detail that contradicts it can sink the relationship in a single meeting.
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highquality compoundersGPsLPs
So what: Actionable for GPs raising capital: define terms like 'quality' precisely because LPs have limited time and won't grant a second meeting after a perceived contradiction.
“the minute a manager comes in and tells you that they invest in quality compounders... they've seated control of the conversation now to the listener” ▶ 46:50
Laboy states 'quality' is a spectrum, not a fixed point, making it one of the most dangerous words in allocator conversations because speaker and listener rarely mean the same thing.
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: mediumqualitymanager selection
So what: Forces precision in style definitions; LPs should ask managers to operationalize 'quality' rather than accept the label.
“quality is a spectrum, right? It's not a fixed point” ▶ 47:13
Laboy credits 15 years marketing a hedge fund with letting him interpret manager answers from the inside — he once cut off a manager's evasive risk-management answer by noting he 'used to give your exact answer,' and the manager conceded they lacked a strong risk practice.
anecdote🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highEverest Capitalrisk management
So what: Illustrates a diligence tactic: recognizing rehearsed non-answers by pattern-matching to one's own former pitches.
“I just spent 15 years marketing and I used to give your exact answer. What I'm picking up here is that you guys really don't have a very strong risk management practice” ▶ 19:53
Laboy notes the decision cadence differs sharply between direct investing (high turnover, lighter analysis per decision) and LP allocation (far fewer decisions, much deeper analysis), and mis-calibrating it leads to either uninformed decisions or analysis paralysis.
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: mediumhedge fundLPEverest Capital
So what: Relevant for investors transitioning between direct and allocator seats; the analytical dial must be deliberately re-tuned.
Team, Process & Portfolio Construction 3
Hewlett's public equity team looks through its managers to the underlying holdings, building spreadsheets of all underlying positions and treating the book as a single portfolio of companies while concentrating a formerly large manager roster.
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highHewlett Foundationpublic equity
So what: Signals an LP that monitors aggregate factor and name concentration across managers — GPs may see look-through overlap questions.
“we started to you know I started to build out spreadsheets that showed all the underlying positions” ▶ 24:12
Hewlett's small public-equity team (Laboy and Anna Marshall as CIO, plus directors Brett Johnson and Tom Mitchkowski) surfaces half-baked manager ideas at Monday meetings so that no final decision surprises anyone on the team.
anecdote🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: mediumAnna MarshallBrett JohnsonTom MitchkowskiHewlett Foundation
So what: Names Hewlett's decision-makers and shows a consensus-building cadence GPs can map when navigating the relationship.
Laboy describes a permanent tension between spending time on existing roster managers versus sourcing new ones, arguing getting current holdings right is as important as finding the next manager and requires an increasing share of time.
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: mediumHewlett Foundationmanager sourcing
So what: A time-allocation model for allocators; monitoring incumbents deserves parity with prospecting, not residual attention.
Investing Philosophy & Career Lessons 4
Laboy's formative investing lesson from Everest's Marco was that the job is to make money, not to be right — a distinction he now applies differently to core versus emerging-manager sleeves.
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highEverest CapitalMarco
So what: A mental model separating ego from P&L; also shapes how much conviction is required before sizing a position.
“You're trying to be right rather than make money. And our job is to make money.” ▶ 9:27
Laboy recounts a contrarian early-2000s Brazil trade: after meeting Lula's chief of staff and central bank when the market expected devaluation and default, Everest maxed its Brazil limit and added an options structure needing a 20% rally in two months for a 5:1 payout — the market rose 23% and the trade paid off.
anecdote🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highBrazilLulaEverest Capitaloptions
So what: A case study in high-conviction contrarian macro sizing against consensus default fears; illustrates convex option expression of a directional macro view.
“within two months, the market up went up 23%. We made our 5 to1 payout on our option strategy” ▶ 11:43
Laboy's advice to young allocators is to model every investor you meet but never adopt anyone's process wholesale — an edge is personal and must align with how you think, captured as 'borrow freely from others but make it your own.'
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: highWarren Buffettallocators
So what: Warns against cargo-culting famous investors (e.g. Buffett); a coherent, internalized process is the durable edge.
“borrow freely and liberally from others, but in the end, make it your own” ▶ 49:34
Laboy learned from Anna Marshall to always add distinct value to a team as insurance against inevitable bad performance years — he used heavy marketing at Everest as both defense (a buffer) and offense (working through the thesis in meetings).
framework🗣 Luis Laboyconviction: mediumAnna MarshallEverest Capital
So what: Career-durability framework for investment professionals: build a second axis of contribution beyond raw returns.

Sourcery with Molly O'Shea 1 episode(s) · 24 insights

Data is Back: MongoDB, Databricks, Snowflake43 min24 insights2026-07-20
▶ Watch on YouTube
MongoDB CEO CJ Desai argues that AI's model boom is driving an unexpected data explosion that benefits operational databases, and that hyperscaler capacity shortages plus data-sovereignty demands are fueling a genuine comeback of on-prem and enterprise data centers. He details MongoDB's three AI customer classes (frontier labs, AI-native startups, enterprises) and lessons on the messy, fast-changing agentic architecture stacks enterprises are building.
On-prem comeback & hyperscaler capacity crunch · 4Data as the downstream AI winner · 5Data sovereignty · 2Competitive positioning vs Snowflake & Databricks · 3Enterprise agentic architecture reality · 4Scaling pain & autonomous database operations · 2Pace of change & the forward-deployed-engineer shift · 2Sovereign/space compute & leadership · 2
On-prem comeback & hyperscaler capacity crunch 4
Hyperscalers are running out of capacity and turning away even top customers: a Fortune 100 company in Texas that is a top-50 customer of one hyperscaler was told there was no capacity for additional cloud and AI workloads, pushing it to reverse a data-center decommissioning and run workloads on-prem instead.
anecdote🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highhyperscalersMongoDBAWScloud
So what: Signals hyperscaler capacity is a binding constraint even for large customers, supporting demand for on-prem/data-center buildout and multicloud database vendors.
“the hyperscaler said sorry we don't have a capacity and they are one of the top 50 customers for that hyperscaler” ▶ 0:15
The 2010-2020 'everything moves to public cloud' consensus is reversing: capacity shortages and the need to keep proprietary data on-premises are driving data-center and on-prem demand back up, contradicting the assumption that all Fortune 500 workloads simply migrate to hyperscalers.
contrarian🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highpublic cloudon-premdata centersFortune 500hyperscalers
So what: A structural bullish signal for on-prem infrastructure, data-center operators, and databases that run outside a single cloud.
“data centers are back and nobody is now saying hey uh especially in the fortune 500 and most people get that wrong because everybody thought hey all the workloads are just going to move to this hyperscaler” ▶ 10:06
Hyperscaler capacity denial is forcing enterprises into complex multicloud setups: a large US telecommunications company was refused additional regional capacity by one hyperscaler and had to sign with a second hyperscaler, now managing its application real estate across multiple clouds.
anecdote🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: hightelecommunicationshyperscalersmulticloud
So what: Regional capacity gaps push customers to multicloud, favoring cloud-agnostic infrastructure and adding operational complexity that vendors can monetize.
“this hyperscaler refused to give them additional regional capacity that they needed. So they signed with the second hyperscaler” ▶ 10:49
MongoDB's multicloud portability is now a purchasing driver for AI companies: a frontier lab chose MongoDB specifically because it runs across multiple clouds for resiliency, and labs running out of capacity in AWS want to shift MongoDB workloads to GCP on demand.
thesis🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highMongoDBAWSGCPfrontier labsmulticloud
So what: Cloud-portability is becoming a competitive moat as capacity scarcity forces workload migration between clouds.
“I'm running out of capacity in AWS so I want to move some of MongoDB to GCP how do we make it easy for them” ▶ 26:06
Data as the downstream AI winner 5
The AI model boom is a data-generation boom: tokens and agents create ever more data, making the data layer a non-obvious downstream beneficiary of AI even as attention stays fixed on the models themselves.
thesis🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highMongoDBAIagentsdata
So what: Frames operational data infrastructure (MongoDB) as a pick-and-shovel AI play distinct from the crowded model layer.
“data is the unsung hero and data is back. You cannot create an AI application without a great data layer and your AI application is as good as your data” ▶ 41:46
MongoDB serves three distinct AI customer classes: frontier labs (multiple, undisclosed use cases including inference, voice, video and image products), AI-native companies (vibe-coding platforms, robotics/physical AI), and enterprises building still-early agentic workloads.
framework🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highMongoDBfrontier labsroboticsphysical AIenterprises
So what: Segmentation shows MongoDB's AI revenue spans the full stack, with enterprise agentic adoption as the still-untapped growth leg.
“we have seen on the AI super cycle three classes of customers specifically to AI” ▶ 5:15
ElevenLabs runs north of 50 million agents entirely on MongoDB, and AI-native startups including Emergent and Base44 (vibe-coding platforms) and Mistral.ai are built on MongoDB, evidencing fit for agentic workloads at scale.
data_point🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highElevenLabsEmergentBase44Mistral.aiMongoDB
So what: Concrete scale reference for MongoDB's traction with high-growth AI-native customers; a proof point for the agentic-memory-layer thesis.
“they have north of 50 million agents depending on when you look at it all running on MongoDB” ▶ 14:30
Enterprise customer-facing agentic applications are still not running at scale — even at airlines and banks, users do not yet see many agents in production apps — indicating the agentic enterprise wave is still in the tinkering/prototyping phase.
prediction🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: mediumenterprisesairlinesbanksagentic applications
So what: Enterprise agentic deployment is earlier than headlines suggest; the inference/production ramp is a future catalyst, not a present one.
≈ “I have not seen end customer facing agentic applications run at scale” paraphrase
MongoDB frames frontier labs as its 'holy grail' customers because their user growth is near-vertical rather than linear, validating its architecture for storing unstructured data in real time and acting as an agent memory layer.
thesis🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highMongoDBfrontier labsmemory layer
So what: Ties MongoDB's growth to the steepest-growing segment in AI; frontier-lab adoption is a leading indicator to watch.
“their scale and how fast they are growing is like not even linear in some of the cases when I see weekly active users or daily active users is almost literally a vertical uh spike” ▶ 14:02
Data sovereignty 2
Data sovereignty is now the top infrastructure priority for regulated European enterprises: a large Paris-based customer told MongoDB it will not move workloads to public cloud at all, keeping everything on-prem because French regulation makes sovereignty the highest priority.
causal🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highFrancedata sovereigntyon-premMongoDB
So what: Regulatory sovereignty is a durable tailwind for on-prem and sovereign-AI deployments, especially in Europe.
“We are not going to move our workloads to public cloud. We are going to run it on prem. Data sovereignty is highest priority. French regulations forces us to have that as the highest priority” ▶ 7:02
Sovereignty concern is broadening beyond regulated industries — the Texas Fortune 100 customer moving workloads on-prem is not in a regulated industry, showing proprietary-data protection is becoming a general enterprise driver, echoing Alex Karp's public warnings about data caution when working with AI labs.
thesis🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: mediumAlex KarpPalantirdata sovereigntyenterprises
So what: Proprietary-data protection as a mainstream (not just regulated) motive expands the addressable on-prem/sovereign market.
“this is not a regulated industry customer they're just a large customer in Texas” ▶ 9:51
Competitive positioning vs Snowflake & Databricks 3
MongoDB positions itself in a different category from Snowflake and Databricks: it is an operational/real-time OLTP document database (for credit-card transactions and other real-time workloads), whereas Snowflake and Databricks are analytical OLAP systems that answer business-analyst queries and are not real-time.
framework🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highMongoDBSnowflakeDatabricksOLTPOLAP
So what: Clarifies MongoDB competes on the operational data layer, not analytics — relevant for sizing overlap and TAM against the analytics names.
“MongoDB is a operational database or a real-time database... The category is called online transaction processing or OLTP” ▶ 11:19
MongoDB integrates vector search and embeddings from Voyage AI natively so data can be stored, searched, and reasoned over in one system without separate pipelines, and claims 75% of the Fortune 100 plus leading AI-native startups run on it.
data_point🗣 sponsor read / CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highMongoDBVoyage AIvector searchFortune 100
So what: Consolidated vector + operational store is MongoDB's differentiation pitch against stitching multiple tools; Fortune 100 penetration is a stated moat metric.
“It's why 75% of the Fortune 100 and leading AI native startups run on MongoDB” ▶ 33:32
MongoDB does not compete with data-labeling / RL companies and views them as complementary, while noting they are growing exponentially fast.
thesis🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: mediumdata labelingreinforcement learningMongoDB
So what: Removes a perceived competitive overlap and flags data-labeling as a separate fast-growing segment.
Enterprise agentic architecture reality 4
Enterprise agentic stacks are exploding in complexity: a large New York bank's agentic architecture diagram had roughly 55 boxes (varied LLMs, frameworks, databases, vector databases, embeddings), and half of them did not exist a year earlier, with the customer expecting even more boxes to be added.
data_point🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highbanksLLMsvector databasesagentic architecture
So what: Rapidly expanding agent stacks mean sustained tooling spend and integration complexity across observability, security, and guardrails.
“this particular picture had I want to say 55 different boxes for the agentic architecture... Half of these boxes would have not been there” ▶ 22:44
Enterprises need deterministic, auditable, human-in-the-loop agents for regulated decisions: a large insurance-company MongoDB customer requires state-by-state, bias-free, deterministic outputs with audit and human oversight before agents can quote or bind policies.
causal🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highinsuranceagentsregulationMongoDB
So what: Determinism, auditability and compliance are gating requirements slowing regulated-industry agent deployment — a bottleneck and an opportunity for governance tooling.
“they have to make sure these agents don't give something there is no bias involved and give a deterministic outcome uh and do you need human in the loop” ▶ 30:22
There is no model standardization inside enterprises: for different use cases a single customer mixes domain-specific LLMs, open-source models, small language models, and closed models like OpenAI's, spanning open-to-closed and small-to-large horizontal-to-domain-specific.
framework🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highLLMsOpenAIAnthropicopen sourcesmall language models
So what: Multi-model heterogeneity persists, favoring model-agnostic infrastructure and undercutting any single-model winner-take-all thesis.
“there is no like standardization even when it comes to these models... it goes anywhere from open source to closed source small to large horizontal to domain specific based on the use cases” ▶ 29:22
The open- vs closed-source model debate has no clear winner in practice: coding agents almost always use proprietary/closed models (Claude Code, Codex, Grok, Cursor options), while some domain-specific use cases pull open-source models from Hugging Face, leaving a persistently mixed world.
contrarian🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: mediumopen sourceClaude CodeCodexGrokCursorHugging Face
So what: Pushes back on the 'open source is taking over' narrative; closed models still dominate high-value coding-agent workloads.
“I have not seen a complete pivot towards open source or a complete pivot towards proprietary” ▶ 33:04
Scaling pain & autonomous database operations 2
A prediction-market customer (founder-CEO) nearly ran out of MongoDB capacity days before World Cup soccer demand peaked, texting CJ Desai specific terabyte needs to avoid going down — illustrating extreme, unplanned capacity spikes at fast-growing AI/real-time firms.
anecdote🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highprediction marketsWorld CupMongoDB
So what: Event-driven demand spikes create acute infrastructure risk; vendors that solve elastic autoscaling capture stickier high-growth accounts.
“it looks like we may be out of capacity in few days on MongoDB and the World Cup soccer is still couple of weeks away” ▶ 25:01
AI-native companies refuse to hire expensive database administrators (historically the most expensive Oracle hires) and instead demand machine-based, self-managing, proactive autoscaling databases — reshaping infrastructure-software product requirements.
thesis🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highOracleDBAMongoDBautoscaling
So what: Autonomous DB operations become a must-win feature; labor substitution via automation is a product-roadmap priority for infra vendors.
“these AI companies are saying we don't have time to hire these people. If we are using your software, it should have its own machine-based database administration” ▶ 27:42
Pace of change & the forward-deployed-engineer shift 2
Every hyperscaler announced forward-deployed-engineer programs within roughly the last 8 weeks, effectively adopting Palantir's playbook, because customers going through rapid AI change need hands-on help.
data_point🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highhyperscalersPalantirforward deployed engineers
So what: A visible industry-wide shift toward high-touch deployment services validates Palantir's model and signals a services layer growing atop AI infra.
“over last 8 weeks lot of announcements from hyperscalers on forward deployed engineers they are using Palunteer's uh playbook” ▶ 20:45
Because software now writes software, the incremental cost of innovation and the cost of experimentation have both collapsed, so companies pivot roadmaps faster than ever and 'this is the slowest it will ever be.'
framework🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: highAIsoftwareinnovation
So what: Falling cost of experimentation accelerates product churn and shortens planning horizons — a structural change in how software companies operate and compete.
“this is the slowest I think it's it is going to get even faster” ▶ 23:47
Sovereign/space compute & leadership 2
MongoDB's CEO is cautiously non-bearish on data centers in space, reasoning that if energy (not physical space) is AI's binding constraint, proximity to the sun for continuous solar energy makes it physically plausible, citing SpaceX offering capacity to Anthropic alongside terrestrial builds in Mississippi and Memphis.
prediction🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: exploratorydata centers in spaceSpaceXAnthropicenergysolar
So what: Reinforces energy-as-the-binding-constraint framing for AI compute; space data centers are a speculative long-dated optionality he rates as non-zero.
“if truly energy is the constraint for the world of AI... how closer do you go to the sun to get as much sun as possible” ▶ 35:32
MongoDB has become a talent farm that competitors actively poach: ElevenLabs' Mati and others target MongoDB salespeople (well-trained go-to-market) and engineers (strong on distributed systems and data), a signal of the company's talent density.
anecdote🗣 CJ Desai (MongoDB CEO)conviction: mediumMongoDBElevenLabstalent
So what: High talent density and poaching pressure is a soft indicator of organizational quality but also a retention risk to monitor.
“everybody told me we should hire salespeople from MongoDB because you guys do really good job of training them” ▶ 40:01

Wealthion 1 episode(s) · 19 insights

Why Smart Investors Still Make Costly Mistakes27 min19 insights2026-07-21
▶ Watch on YouTube
Mental performance coach Evan of M1 Performance Group, a former money manager who left Wall Street at 46 after a panic attack, tells Wealthion that ~90% of investing outcomes are psychological: the edge comes from moving from reactive to responsive decision-making, clearing the nervous system's residue between trades, and reframing regret over missed moves like the recent gold and silver runs. Despite the 'long' metadata tag this is a ~27-minute behavioral-psychology interview with no market forecasts or tickers.
Reacting vs Responding · 3Neuroplasticity and rewiring patterns · 3Performance equation and interferences · 2Best decisions vs best outcomes · 2Nervous-system residue between decisions · 2Regret and missed moves (gold and silver) · 2Flexibility, adaptation and anchoring · 2Learning in success and process over win rate · 3
Reacting vs Responding 3
Evan of M1 Performance Group argues no decision is made without emotion, but for most investors emotions are tied to past programming and produce reactive rather than responsive choices; the coaching goal is to create space, in the face of any emotion, to pick conscious behavior.
framework🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group, ex-money manager)conviction: highM1 Performance Grouptrading psychologydecision-making
So what: Frames trading errors as reactive impulses; the actionable lever is pausing to choose behavior rather than trying to suppress emotion.
“impulsivity is chaos. That's reacting. Intuition is calmness. That's responding.” ▶ 8:18
Evan warns experienced investors who assume age has made them 'intuitive' to first understand their impulsivity before trusting intuition, because what feels like a seasoned gut read is often an unexamined reaction.
framework🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: hightrading psychologyintuition
So what: A check against overconfident 'gut' trades from veteran investors: distinguish calm intuition from chaotic impulse before acting.
“understand your impulsivities first before you you trust your intuition” ▶ 8:39
Evan prescribes asking yourself 'self-exploratory questions' out loud (e.g. 'What am I trying to accomplish?', 'Why am I reacting and not responding?') because verbalizing them creates mental space that shifts you from reacting to responding.
framework🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: highmental performancedecision-making
So what: A concrete, no-cost pre-trade routine to interrupt impulsive execution.
“it has to come out of your mouth. You just can't think this. It creates mental space” ▶ 7:57
Neuroplasticity and rewiring patterns 3
Evan contends behavioral patterns are not permanent: repeatedly tying a new conscious behavior to a recurring emotion like fear builds new experiences that rewire neural circuitry (neuroplasticity), so investors are not locked into destructive habits.
causal🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: highneuroplasticitytrading psychology
So what: Reframes chronic behavioral mistakes as fixable through deliberate repetition rather than innate temperament.
“if I consistently take an emotion, fear of whatever, tie a new behavior to it, the experience is going to be different” ▶ 12:31
Evan holds that behavior changes before feelings and that emotions cannot be directly controlled, so the practical starting point for change is altering how you respond, not trying to change how you feel.
framework🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: highbehavioral changetrading psychology
So what: Directs effort toward controllable actions (process, sizing, routines) rather than unproductive attempts to feel differently.
“Behavior changes before feelings.” ▶ 22:18
Evan describes the brain as a predicting mechanism and the source of anxiety, arguing that feeding new positive experiences into that 'predictive pond' changes the whole equation of how one anticipates and reacts.
framework🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: mediumneuroscienceanxiety
So what: Explains anxiety around uncertain outcomes as prediction, and offers deliberate new experience as the counter.
“the brain is a predicting mechanism. That's where anxiety comes from.” ▶ 12:41
Performance equation and interferences 2
Evan's core framework is Performance = Potential minus Interferences: interferences never disappear entirely, but reducing them frees energy to build more potential, which is what shifts performance higher.
framework🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: highmental performance
So what: A diagnostic lens: work on removing psychological interference rather than only adding skill or data.
“Performance equals potential minus interferences.” ▶ 0:06
Evan argues success itself creates a hidden blind spot: while things go well, small 'kinks in the foundation' develop unnoticed, so staying successful requires periodic self-reflection to 'look under the hood.'
thesis🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: highself-reflectiontrading psychology
So what: Warns wealthy, previously successful investors that a good track record can mask deteriorating process.
“what got you here is not going to get you where you need to go” ▶ 6:37
Best decisions vs best outcomes 2
Evan stresses that a best decision does not guarantee a best outcome and only time reveals if you were right; the edge comes from stacking best decisions repeatedly so probability, not any single trade, ends up in your favor.
framework🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: highprobabilitydecision-making
So what: Separates process from outcome, discouraging results-based self-judgment on individual trades.
“one best decisions doesn't mean you're going to have a best outcome” ▶ 14:09
Evan argues that two investors with identical data and the same trade idea can have opposite P&L, so the decisive differentiator is the mental edge rather than the information itself.
thesis🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: hightrading psychologymental edge
So what: Reframes 'I did all the research and still lost' as an execution/psychology problem, not an analysis gap.
“It all becomes little the mental edge is everything.” ▶ 14:59
Nervous-system residue between decisions 2
Evan disputes the textbook claim that every trade is an independent event: intellectually it should be, but the nervous system carries residue from each prior decision, so investors must deliberately clear to a near 'zero-zero mindset' before the next trade.
contrarian🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: hightrading psychologydecision-making
So what: Explains tilt and revenge trading; the practical skill is resetting between decisions rather than assuming a clean slate.
“our nervous systems carry residue from each decision we make. Just knowing that is powerful.” ▶ 15:36
Evan identifies the ability to reset, down-regulate and create space after a loss as one of the most valuable and underrated professional skills, one that people wrongly assume happens automatically.
framework🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: highmental performancerisk management
So what: Points to recovery/recalibration as a trainable edge, especially through drawdowns and stressful regimes.
“how do you recalibrate to come back again? That is one of the most important things to learn right there.” ▶ 16:33
Regret and missed moves (gold and silver) 2
On gold's roughly 40-50% run and subsequent ~20% pullback that left many Wealthion viewers feeling they missed selling, Evan reframes the choice around pre-structuring the position sizing so you own some and sell some, accepting Buffett's idea that it may be 'your worst sell ever,' rather than ruminating over top-ticking.
framework🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: mediumgoldWarren Buffettposition sizing
So what: A behavioral playbook for managing regret in extended commodity moves: size to avoid regret in either direction ahead of time.
“how will you feel if you don't be a if you're not a part of this?” ▶ 20:03
Evan shares that he had held silver since 2008 and sold most of his position around $92, after which silver rose toward $130, and he treats that as a well-executed decision to accept rather than a source of rumination.
anecdote🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: highsilvertrading psychology
So what: Models detaching self-worth from top-ticking; a sold winner that keeps running is not a failure of process.
“I was in silver since '08. And I think I sold most of my position at 92. And then it obviously went much higher.” ▶ 20:28
Flexibility, adaptation and anchoring 2
Evan advises holding a structured way of thinking but staying flexible within that structure rather than rigid, because rigidity behaves like scarcity and shrinks you while flexibility opens up clarity and space to respond to changing markets.
framework🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: highadaptationtrading psychology
So what: A guard against clinging to a plan; distinguishes disciplined structure from brittle rigidity.
“Think about rigidity as as scarcity, as you get smaller. Flexibility, you get to open up” ▶ 10:45
Evan reframes confidence as knowing you don't know everything and warns that anchoring to a single past reference point shuts investors off from adapting to ever-changing markets.
framework🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: highanchoring biasadaptation
So what: Ties overconfidence and fixed reference prices to failure to adapt; curiosity is the corrective.
“confidence is knowing that you don't know everything” ▶ 18:44
Learning in success and process over win rate 3
Evan argues investors focus on learning from struggle but neglect learning in success; because success constantly moves you into uncharted territory, the discipline is to 'recommit' to the basics that got you there rather than take them for granted.
thesis🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: highmental performancetrading psychology
So what: Counsels reviewing and reinforcing process precisely when results are good, before decay sets in.
“When you're successful, you are constantly putting yourself in uncharted territories.” ▶ 23:26
Evan says his best trading year had only a 20% win rate but a massive slugging ratio, arguing you are 'allowed to be wrong' frequently as long as you size and manage winners well, and that win-loss ratio is a misleading scorecard.
contrarian🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: highwin-loss ratiorisk management
So what: Reinforces asymmetric payoff investing: hit rate matters far less than magnitude of winners versus losers.
“my best year my win-loss ratio was 20%. I go, But, my slugging ratio was massive.” ▶ 22:50
Evan asserts that roughly 90% of investing performance is mental rather than about the analysis, comparing surface-level explanations to a 'leaf on a tree' when the real driver is always the 'roots' of one's mental makeup.
thesis🗣 Evan (M1 Performance Group)conviction: hightrading psychologymental performance
So what: Sets priorities: allocate effort to psychology and process, not just deeper research, to improve results.
“for your listeners, to know that 90% of this is mental.” ▶ 22:01
